topics.title: Materials for Shipbuilding: Metallic Materials and Their Properties, Production of Shipbuilding Steel, Various Grades, Iron Carbon Diagram
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST411 - MECHANICAL ENGINEERING FOR SHIP DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Materials for Shipbuilding: Metallic Materials and Their Properties, Production of Shipbuilding Steel, Various Grades, Iron Carbon Diagram

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What are Shipbuilding Metallic Materials?

**Metallic materials for shipbuilding** are engineered alloys—primarily based on iron (Fe), aluminium (Al), copper (Cu), and titanium (Ti)—that are selected and processed to meet the rigorous structural, mechanical, and environmental demands of marine vessels. They must simultaneously offer **high specific strength** (strength-to-weight ratio), **adequate toughness** at sub-zero sea temperatures, **weldability**, **formability**, and **resistance to corrosion** in saline and humid environments.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition**
> *Shipbuilding metallic materials* refer to ferrous and non-ferrous alloys certified by classification societies (IRS, Lloyd's Register, DNV, ABS, BV) for use in hulls, superstructures, propellers, shafts, piping, and outfitting components of ships and offshore structures.

### 1.2 The Intuitive Analogy — "The Skeleton of a Steel Giant"

Imagine a ship as a **giant whale swimming in concrete water (air is 800 times thinner than water)**. The metallic material is the whale's skeleton and ribs. Just as a whale's bones need to be:
- **Light enough** to float and move efficiently,
- **Strong enough** to withstand water pressure and waves,
- **Flexible enough** to bend without breaking in storms,
- **Resistant enough** to the corrosive salt water—

…so too must the ship's metal combine these exact four properties. **Steel** is the historical choice because iron and carbon (the two atoms that form the Iron-Carbon Diagram) act like a "concrete and rebar" inside the metal—hard cementite particles embedded in soft ferrite give the perfect balance.

### 1.3 Classification of Marine Metallic Materials

> [!NOTE]
> **Two Broad Families of Marine Metals**

**A. Ferrous Metals (Iron-based, ~95% of ship tonnage)**
- **Carbon Steel** (Mild, Medium, High-carbon)
- **High-Tensile Steel (HTS)** — also called **High-Strength Steel (HSS)**
- **Stainless Steel** (Austenitic, e.g., 304, 316 — used in propellers, pumps)
- **Cast Iron & Cast Steel** (for engine blocks, stern tubes)

**B. Non-Ferrous Metals**
- **Aluminium Alloys** (5083, 5086, 5456 — for superstructures, fast ferries)
- **Copper Alloys** (Brass, Bronze, Cupronickel — for piping, propellers)
- **Titanium Alloys** (submarine pressure hulls, seawater piping)
- **Nickel-Aluminium Bronze (NAB)** — for submarine propellers

### 1.4 Why Steel Dominates Shipbuilding?

| Property | Mild Steel | Aluminium 5083 | Requirement for Ships |
|---|---|---|---|
| Density (kg/m³) | **7850** | **2660** | Moderate |
| Yield Strength (MPa) | **235** | **215** | High |
| Cost (relative) | 1× | 3–4× | Low |
| Weldability | Excellent | Good | Critical |
| Corrosion in seawater | Moderate | Good in marine atm. | Must be protected |

> The conclusion: **steel remains the unbeatable maritime workhorse** due to its optimal cost-to-strength-to-weldability triad.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Properties of Shipbuilding Metallic Materials

**A. Mechanical Properties (The Four Pillars)**
1. **Yield Strength (σ_y)** — Stress at which the material begins to deform plastically (permanent). Measured in **MPa**. For shipbuilding, classification societies mandate **σ_y ≥ 235 MPa** for mild steel.
2. **Ultimate Tensile Strength (UTS, σ_u)** — Maximum stress the material can withstand before fracture.
3. **Elongation (% E)** — Ductility measure; the percentage increase in gauge length at fracture. Shipbuilding steel requires **% E ≥ 22%** (per IRS rules).
4. **Charpy V-Notch Impact Toughness** — Energy absorbed (in **Joules**) at a specified temperature (typically **0 °C, –20 °C, or –40 °C**). Critical for Arctic-service vessels.

**B. Physical Properties**
- **Density (ρ)** — Weight per unit volume; affects displacement.
- **Thermal Conductivity (k)** — Important for engine and boiler components.
- **Coefficient of Thermal Expansion (α)** — Critical for dissimilar metal joints.

**C. Chemical/Environmental Properties**
- **Corrosion Resistance** — Tendency to oxidize in saline media.
- **Sulphur (S) and Phosphorus (P) content** — Restricted to ≤ 0.035% and ≤ 0.040% in shipbuilding steel to avoid brittleness.

### 2.2 Production of Shipbuilding Steel — The Journey from Ore to Plate

The metallurgical journey consists of **five major stages**:

**Step 1: Ironmaking (in a Blast Furnace)**
- Iron ore (Fe₂O₃ / Fe₃O₄) + Coke (C) + Limestone (CaCO₃) → **Pig Iron** (4–4.5% C, very brittle).
- This is a **reduction** process: $$\text{Fe}_2\text{O}_3 + 3\text{CO} \rightarrow 2\text{Fe} + 3\text{CO}_2$$

**Step 2: Steelmaking (Primary Refining)**
- The Basic Oxygen Furnace (BOF) or Basic Oxygen Steelmaking (BOS) process reduces the carbon content from 4% to ≤ 0.05–1.0% by blowing **pure oxygen** through molten pig iron.
- Carbon oxidizes and escapes as CO gas: $$\text{C} + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{CO} \uparrow$$
- Alternative route: **Electric Arc Furnace (EAF)** — uses scrap steel and electricity; cleaner, used for special steels.

**Step 3: Secondary Refining (Ladle Metallurgy)**
- **Deoxidation** with Al, Si, or Mn to remove dissolved oxygen.
- **Desulphurization** using CaO/CaC₂.
- **Vacuum Degassing (VD)** to reduce H and N (which cause porosity and embrittlement).

**Step 4: Continuous Casting**
- Molten steel is poured into a water-cooled copper mould to form **slabs** (200–300 mm thick).
- These slabs are then rolled.

**Step 5: Hot Rolling & Thermo-Mechanical Controlled Processing (TMCP)**
- Slabs are reheated to ~1200 °C and rolled into plates of 4–100 mm thickness.
- **TMCP** (controlled rolling + accelerated cooling) produces fine-grained HTS with superior strength-toughness combination at lower carbon content (improved weldability).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why TMCP Matters in Shipbuilding**
> TMCP allows production of **AH36, DH36, EH36** grade plates with only **0.12% carbon** (weldable!) yet **yield strength of 355 MPa**. Conventional heat treatment would require more carbon, making the steel brittle and unweldable.

### 2.3 Various Grades of Shipbuilding Steel (Classification Society Standards)

The major classification societies (IRS, LR, DNV, ABS, BV, KR, NK) follow harmonized rules. The grades are denoted by:
- **A, B, D, E** — Increasing notch toughness at lower temperatures.
- **36, 40, 47, 51** — Yield strength in **ksi × 10** (e.g., "36" means σ_y = 355 MPa ≈ 36 ksi).

| Grade | Yield Strength (MPa) | Tensile Strength (MPa) | Impact Test Temp. | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Grade A** | **≥ 235** | **400–520** | — | Hull plates, secondary structure |
| **Grade B** | **≥ 235** | **400–520** | 0 °C | Hull plates, side shell |
| **Grade D** | **≥ 235** | **400–520** | –20 °C | Bottom shell, strength deck |
| **Grade E** | **≥ 235** | **400–520** | –40 °C | Ice-strengthened hulls |
| **AH36** | **≥ 355** | **490–620** | 0 °C | High-strength hull, deck |
| **DH36** | **≥ 355** | **490–620** | –20 °C | High-strength bottom |
| **EH36** | **≥ 355** | **490–620** | –40 °C | Arctic, ice-class vessels |
| **FH36** | **≥ 355** | **490–620** | –60 °C | Polar Class ships |

### 2.4 The Iron-Carbon Diagram — The "Phase Map" of Steel

The **Iron-Carbon (Fe–C) Phase Diagram** is the most important metallurgical map for understanding steel. It plots **temperature (°C)** on the y-axis against **carbon composition (% by weight)** on the x-axis, up to **6.67% C** (corresponding to pure cementite, Fe₃C).

**Key Single-Phase Regions:**
- **α-Ferrite (BCC)**: Solid solution of C in α-iron. Max solubility = **0.022% C** at 727 °C. Soft, ductile, magnetic.
- **γ-Austenite (FCC)**: Solid solution of C in γ-iron. Max solubility = **2.11% C** at 1147 °C. Ductile, non-magnetic.
- **δ-Ferrite (BCC)**: Exists only between 1394–1538 °C. Not relevant in heat treatment of steels.
- **Fe₃C (Cementite)**: Intermetallic compound, **6.67% C**, very hard and brittle.
- **Liquid (L)**: Above liquidus line.

**The Three Critical Invariant Reactions:**

1. **Eutectic** (at 1147 °C, 4.3% C): Liquid → Austenite + Cementite (called **Ledeburite**)
2. **Eutectoid** (at 727 °C, 0.77% C): Austenite → Ferrite + Cementite (called **Pearlite**)
3. **Peritectic** (at 1493 °C, 0.16% C): Liquid + δ-Ferrite → Austenite

**Carbon Steels vs Cast Iron — The Critical 2.11% Line:**
- **Steels**: C content **0.022% to 2.11%** (hypoeutectoid ≤ 0.77%; eutectoid = 0.77%; hypereutectoid > 0.77% up to 2.11%)
- **Cast Irons**: C content **2.11% to 6.67%**

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\sigma_y = \dfrac{F_y}{A_0}$ | Yield stress = Yield Load / Original Area | MPa |
| 2 | $\sigma_u = \dfrac{F_{\max}}{A_0}$ | Ultimate tensile stress | MPa |
| 3 | $\% E = \dfrac{L_f - L_0}{L_0} \times 100$ | Percentage elongation | % |
| 4 | $RA = \dfrac{A_0 - A_f}{A_0} \times 100$ | Reduction in area | % |
| 5 | $CVN \; (J)$ | Charpy V-Notch impact energy at test T | Joules |
| 6 | **Lever Rule** for phase fraction: $W_\alpha = \dfrac{C_0 - C_\beta}{C_\alpha - C_\beta}$ | Mass fraction of α phase in two-phase region | Dimensionless |
| 7 | $V_\alpha = \dfrac{C_0 - C_\beta}{C_\alpha - C_\beta}$ | Volume fraction (if densities equal) | Dimensionless |
| 8 | $\sigma_{\text{all}} = \dfrac{\sigma_y}{n}$ | Allowable stress using Factor of Safety n | MPa |
| 9 | $E = 210 \text{ GPa}$ (steel) | Young's Modulus of structural steel | GPa |
| 10 | $\rho_{\text{steel}} = 7850 \text{ kg/m}^3$ | Density of mild steel | kg/m³ |
| 11 | **Stiffness Criterion** for plates: $t = k \, s \sqrt{\dfrac{\sigma_y}{235}}$ | Net plate thickness for ship hulls (LR/IRS rule) | mm |
| 12 | $\Delta T = T_{\text{ambient}} - T_{\text{design}}$ | For notch toughness selection | °C |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Real-World Utility**
> The Iron-Carbon Diagram directly determines: (a) heat treatment route (annealing, normalizing, quenching, tempering), (b) weldability (high C → low weldability), (c) mechanical properties via the **Pearlite volume fraction** (more pearlite → higher strength, lower ductility), and (d) selection between cast iron and steel for a given marine component.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Application of the Lever Rule on Fe–C Diagram

**Problem Setup:**
Consider a **hypoeutectoid steel** containing **0.40% C** cooled slowly to a temperature just above the eutectoid isotherm (727 °C). Determine the mass fractions of proeutectoid ferrite (α) and austenite (γ) at, say, **740 °C**.

**Step 1 — Identify the phase boundaries at 740 °C:**
- The α/(α+γ) phase boundary at 740 °C is at composition: $C_\alpha = 0.021\% \, \text{C}$
- The (α+γ)/γ phase boundary at 740 °C is at composition: $C_\gamma = 0.77\% \, \text{C}$ (essentially the eutectoid composition, just above 727 °C)

**Step 2 — Identify the alloy composition:**
- $C_0 = 0.40\% \, \text{C}$ (the hypoeutectoid steel)

**Step 3 — Apply the Lever Rule for ferrite fraction (W_α):**
The lever rule is a mass balance: a tie-line of length $(C_0 - C_\gamma)$ lies on the ferrite side, and a length $(C_\alpha - C_0)$ on the austenite side. Mass fraction of ferrite is the ratio of the **opposite arm** to the **total arm length**.

$$
W_\alpha = \frac{C_\gamma - C_0}{C_\gamma - C_\alpha}
$$

Substitute values:

$$
W_\alpha = \frac{0.77 - 0.40}{0.77 - 0.021}
$$

$$
W_\alpha = \frac{0.37}{0.749}
$$

$$
\boxed{W_\alpha = 0.494 \;\; (\text{i.e., } 49.4\% \text{ ferrite})}
$$

**Step 4 — Mass fraction of austenite (W_γ):**
By mass balance, $W_\alpha + W_\gamma = 1$:

$$
W_\gamma = 1 - W_\alpha = 1 - 0.494 = 0.506
$$

$$
\boxed{W_\gamma = 0.506 \;\; (\text{i.e., } 50.6\% \text{ austenite})}
$$

**Step 5 — Verification of the mass balance (sanity check):**
Total carbon must be conserved: $C_0 = W_\alpha \cdot C_\alpha + W_\gamma \cdot C_\gamma$

$$
C_0 = (0.494)(0.021) + (0.506)(0.77)
$$

$$
C_0 = 0.01037 + 0.3896 = 0.400 \, \% \text{C} \;\;\checkmark
$$

**Step 6 — Composition of each phase at 740 °C:**
- Ferrite contains 0.021% C
- Austenite contains 0.77% C (essentially saturated)

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Effect of Carbon Content on Steel Properties

| Carbon Content (% C) | Microstructure | Yield Strength (MPa, approx.) | Hardness (HB) | Elongation (%) | Weldability | Common Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0.05 – 0.15 | All ferrite | 200 – 250 | 90 – 110 | 30 – 40 | Excellent | Ship hull plates (Grade A) |
| 0.15 – 0.30 | Ferrite + pearlite | 250 – 320 | 120 – 150 | 25 – 30 | Good | General ship structure |
| 0.30 – 0.45 | More ferrite + pearlite | 320 – 400 | 150 – 180 | 20 – 25 | Moderate | Shafts, crankshafts |
| 0.45 – 0.60 | Ferrite + pearlite | 400 – 500 | 180 – 220 | 15 – 20 | Poor | High-stress machinery |
| 0.60 – 0.77 | Predominantly pearlite | 500 – 600 | 220 – 260 | 10 – 15 | Poor | Rails, springs |
| 0.77 (eutectoid) | 100% Pearlite | 600 – 700 | 260 – 300 | 8 – 10 | Very poor | Razor blades, piano wire |
| 0.77 – 2.11 | Pearlite + proeutectoid cementite | 600 – 1000 | 260 – 450 | 2 – 8 | Unweldable | Cutlery, dies |

### 3.3 Step-by-Step Worked Example — KTU-Style Numerical

**Question:** A shipbuilding steel sample contains **0.35% C**. It is slowly cooled from 900 °C to just above 727 °C. Calculate (a) the mass fraction of proeutectoid ferrite, (b) the mass fraction of pearlite formed after crossing the eutectoid line, and (c) the mass fraction of total ferrite in the final room-temperature microstructure.

**Given:** $C_0 = 0.35\% \, \text{C}$; At 740 °C: $C_\alpha = 0.021\%$, $C_\gamma = 0.77\%$

**Part (a) — Proeutectoid ferrite at 740 °C:**

$$
W_{\alpha,\text{pro}} = \frac{0.77 - 0.35}{0.77 - 0.021} = \frac{0.42}{0.749} = 0.561
$$

$$
\boxed{W_{\alpha,\text{pro}} = 0.561 \; (56.1\% \text{ proeutectoid ferrite})}
$$

**Part (b) — Pearlite fraction (from austenite at 727 °C):**
Below 727 °C, all remaining austenite transforms to pearlite. So pearlite fraction = austenite fraction just above 727 °C:

$$
W_P = W_\gamma = 1 - 0.561 = 0.439
$$

$$
\boxed{W_P = 0.439 \; (43.9\% \text{ pearlite})}
$$

**Part (c) — Total ferrite (proeutectoid + eutectoid from pearlite):**
Pearlite itself is a lamellar mixture of **88% ferrite and 12% cementite** (from applying the lever rule at 727 °C between C_α = 0.021% and C_Fe₃C = 6.67%).

Ferrite within pearlite:

$$
W_{\alpha,\text{in pearlite}} = \frac{6.67 - 0.77}{6.67 - 0.021} = \frac{5.90}{6.649} = 0.887
$$

Total ferrite:

$$
W_{\alpha,\text{total}} = W_{\alpha,\text{pro}} + (W_P \times 0.887)
$$

$$
W_{\alpha,\text{total}} = 0.561 + (0.439 \times 0.887) = 0.561 + 0.389 = 0.950
$$

$$
\boxed{W_{\alpha,\text{total}} = 0.950 \; (95.0\% \text{ total ferrite, with } 5.0\% \text{ cementite})}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This result is intuitive — a 0.35% C hypoeutectoid steel is mostly soft, ductile ferrite with islands of hard pearlite providing strength.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

```mermaid

    Q2 -- No --> R1[Use Grade A or B]
    Q2 -- Yes --> Q3{Below minus 20 degree C?}

    Q3 -- Yes --> Q4{Below minus 40 degree C?}
    Q4 -- No --> R3[Use Grade E or EH36]
    Q4 -- Yes --> R4[Use Grade FH36 Polar Class]

    P1[What is the stress level?] --> P2{Yield 235 MPa sufficient?}
    P2 -- Yes --> S1[Use Normal Strength Steel NS]
    P2 -- No --> S2[Use High Tensile Steel AH DH EH 355 MPa]

    Q1 --> T1[Final Grade Selection]
    P1 --> T1
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
**Differentiate between mild steel and high-tensile steel used in shipbuilding. Give two examples of grades of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Property | Mild Steel (NS) | High-Tensile Steel (HTS) |
|---|---|---|
| Yield Strength | **≥ 235 MPa** | **≥ 315 / 355 / 390 MPa** |
| Carbon content | 0.15 – 0.21 % | 0.10 – 0.18 % (TMCP) |
| Weldability | Excellent | Good (controlled heat input) |
| Notch toughness | Adequate at room T | Good at –20 to –60 °C |
| Cost | Lower | ~1.3 – 1.5× higher |
| Weight saving | Baseline | ~15–25% lighter hulls possible |

**Examples of Mild Steel grades:** Grade A, Grade B, Grade D, Grade E
**Examples of High-Tensile Steel grades:** AH32, DH36, EH36, FH36 (suffix number = yield strength in ksi × 10)

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]**
**What is the significance of the eutectoid point (0.77% C, 727 °C) in the Iron-Carbon diagram? Why is eutectoid steel not preferred for marine applications?**

**Model Answer:**

**Significance of the Eutectoid Point:**
- At **0.77% C and 727 °C**, single-phase **γ-austenite** decomposes simultaneously into two solid phases: **α-ferrite (0.022% C) + Fe₃C cementite (6.67% C)**, forming a fine lamellar structure called **Pearlite**.
- This is a **solid-state invariant reaction** governed by the equation:
$$
\gamma_{(0.77\% \, C)} \xrightarrow{727^\circ \text{C}} \alpha_{(0.022\% \, C)} + \text{Fe}_3\text{C}_{(6.67\% \, C)}
$$
- It acts as a **reference composition**: steels below 0.77% C are *hypoeutectoid*, above are *hypereutectoid*.
- The hardness and strength of any plain-carbon steel is **directly proportional to its pearlite content**, and pearlite content = 100% at eutectoid composition → maximum hardness for a given cooling rate.

**Why Eutectoid Steel is NOT preferred in Marine Applications:**
1. **Poor weldability** — High C content (0.77%) forms brittle martensite in the heat-affected zone (HAZ), leading to hydrogen-induced cracking.
2. **Low toughness** — Pearlite is hard but brittle; not suitable for shock-loaded hulls.
3. **Difficult to roll into plates** — Eutectoid steel has poor hot-workability due to Fe₃C networks at grain boundaries.
4. **Marine hulls require % Elongation ≥ 22%** (per IRS rules), but eutectoid steel has only 8–10% elongation.

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Read Carefully**
> Students often (1) confuse **hypoeutectoid vs hypereutectoid** when reading compositions, (2) forget to convert 0.77% C = 100% pearlite, (3) write "Ledeburite" instead of "Pearlite" at the eutectoid point, and (4) forget to mention **impact test temperature** for grades. Each mistake can cost **1–2 marks**.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 Model]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the production of shipbuilding steel from iron ore to finished plate. List at least four processes and discuss how **Thermo-Mechanical Controlled Processing (TMCP)** has revolutionized the production of high-strength shipbuilding plates.

**(b) [7 Marks]** With a neat sketch, describe the **Iron-Carbon Equilibrium Diagram**. Identify and label the following: (i) eutectoid point, (ii) eutectic point, (iii) ferrite, (iv) austenite, (v) cementite, (vi) pearlite region. Explain the difference between **hypoeutectoid** and **hypereutectoid** steels with examples.

---

**Model Solution for Question A:**

**(a) Production of Shipbuilding Steel — Stepwise Explanation:**

**Process 1 — Ironmaking (Blast Furnace):**
Iron ore (Fe₂O₃), coke, and limestone are charged from the top of a tall blast furnace. Hot air is blown in from the bottom ( tuyères). The coke burns to form CO, which reduces the ore:
$$\text{Fe}_2\text{O}_3 + 3\text{CO} \rightarrow 2\text{Fe} + 3\text{CO}_2 \uparrow$$
Limestone (CaCO₃) forms slag with impurities (SiO₂, etc.). The output is **pig iron** (4–4.5% C, brittle), tapped at the bottom.

**Process 2 — Steelmaking (BOF or EAF):**
- **BOF (Basic Oxygen Furnace):** Molten pig iron is poured into a vessel, and pure oxygen is blown at supersonic speed (Mach 2) through a water-cooled lance. The carbon oxidizes:
$$\text{C} + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{CO} \uparrow \quad ; \quad \text{Si} + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{SiO}_2 \quad ; \quad \text{Mn} + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{MnO}$$
A vessel of 300 tonnes can be refined in **30–40 minutes** — extremely fast.
- **EAF (Electric Arc Furnace):** Uses scrap steel as feedstock, melted by three graphite electrodes (arc temperature ~3000 °C). Cleaner, lower CO₂ footprint, used for special steels.

**Process 3 — Secondary Refining (Ladle Metallurgy):**
The crude liquid steel is transferred to a ladle where:
- **Deoxidation** with Al, Si-Mn, or Ca-Si removes dissolved O₂ (otherwise forms blowholes during solidification).
- **Desulphurization** by CaO + CaC₂ slag (S ≤ 0.005% achievable).
- **Vacuum Degassing (RH/VD)** to reduce H (causes hairline cracks) and N (causes strain aging).

**Process 4 — Continuous Casting:**
The refined liquid steel is poured into a water-cooled oscillating copper mould, where a thin solid skin forms. The strand is withdrawn by pinch rolls, water-spray cooled, and cut by torch into **slabs** (200–300 mm thick, 1.5–2.5 m wide, up to 12 m long). Solidification rate is **much faster** than ingot casting → uniform fine-grained structure.

**Process 5 — Hot Rolling and TMCP:**
Slabs are reheated to 1100–1250 °C and passed through a series of rolling stands that progressively reduce thickness to 4–100 mm plates. In **conventional rolling**, plates are then normalized (heated to 900 °C, air-cooled). In **TMCP**:
- **Stage 1 (Controlled Rolling):** Final deformation passes occur in the **austenite non-recrystallization region** (~800–950 °C). The austenite grains are pancaked, creating many nucleation sites.
- **Stage 2 (Accelerated Cooling):** Immediately after rolling, plates are water-cooled at 5–25 °C/sec down to 500–600 °C. This produces an exceptionally **fine-grained ferrite + bainite** microstructure.

**Why TMCP revolutionized shipbuilding plates:**
1. Achieves **yield strength of 355–460 MPa** with **carbon content as low as 0.10–0.12%** → excellent weldability (low CEV ≤ 0.38).
2. Eliminates the need for **post-rolling heat treatment** → lower energy cost, faster delivery.
3. Provides **superior Charpy impact toughness** (≥ 34 J at –40 °C for EH36-TM).
4. Allows **thinner plates for the same strength** → lighter ship, higher payload.

**Examples:** EH36-TM, EH40-TM, EH47-TM (used in LNG carriers, icebreakers).

**(b) Iron-Carbon Diagram and Steels Classification:**

**[Sketch to be drawn in the answer sheet]**
*Y-axis: Temperature (°C) from 0 to 1600 °C; X-axis: Carbon % from 0 to 6.67%*

The diagram has the following salient features:

| Label | Location | Description |
|---|---|---|
| A | 1538 °C at 0% C | Melting point of pure iron |
| N | 1394 °C at 0% C | Curie temperature / δ → γ transformation |
| E | 1147 °C at 2.11% C | **Maximum solubility of C in austenite** |
| C | 1147 °C at 4.3% C | **Eutectic point** (L → γ + Fe₃C = Ledeburite) |
| S | 727 °C at 0.77% C | **Eutectoid point** (γ → α + Fe₃C = Pearlite) |
| P | 727 °C at 0.022% C | **Maximum solubility of C in ferrite** |
| Q | Room T at 0.008% C | Solubility of C in α at room temperature |
| L | Vertical line at 6.67% C | Pure cementite (Fe₃C) |

**Phases to be shaded/labeled:**
- **α-Ferrite region:** Below 727 °C, 0–0.022% C (bottom-left).
- **γ-Austenite region:** 727–1394 °C, 0–2.11% C (large central region).
- **δ-Ferrite region:** 1394–1538 °C, 0–0.09% C (top-left).
- **Liquid (L):** Above liquidus line.
- **Pearlite region:** At 727 °C, eutectoid mixture (α + Fe₃C lamellae).

**Hypoeutectoid vs Hypereutectoid Steels:**

| Feature | Hypoeutectoid Steel | Hypereutectoid Steel |
|---|---|---|
| Carbon % | 0.022 – 0.77 % | 0.77 – 2.11 % |
| Microstructure | Proeutectoid **α-ferrite** + pearlite | Proeutectoid **Fe₃C (cementite)** + pearlite |
| Cooling path | Crosses A₃ line first, then A₁ (eutectoid) | Crosses A_cm line first, then A₁ (eutectoid) |
| Properties | Softer, more ductile, weldable | Harder, less ductile, unweldable |
| Example | **0.40% C steel (ship shaft)** | **1.0% C steel (cutlery, dies)** |
| Use in shipbuilding | **YES** (hulls, decks, frames) | **NO** (not used in marine hulls) |

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – June 2023 Model]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Classify the various **grades of shipbuilding steel** as per classification society rules. Discuss the significance of the **suffix letter (A, B, D, E, F)** and the **suffix number (32, 36, 40)** with respect to toughness and strength.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A hypoeutectoid steel contains **0.45% C**. It is slowly cooled from 1000 °C to room temperature.
(i) Calculate the **mass fraction of proeutectoid ferrite** just above 727 °C.
(ii) Calculate the **mass fraction of pearlite** formed at 727 °C.
(iii) Calculate the **total mass fraction of ferrite** in the final microstructure.

---

**Model Solution for Question B:**

**(a) Classification of Shipbuilding Steel Grades:**

**Classification Society Standards (Harmonized):**
The major classification societies — **IRS (Indian Register of Shipping), Lloyd's Register (LR), DNV, ABS, BV, KR, NK** — follow a common grading system defined in the **IACS Unified Requirements (UR)** for steel plates used in ship construction.

**Two-tier grading nomenclature:**

**Tier 1 — Suffix Letter (Notch Toughness / Temperature Rating):**
- **A** — No impact test required; for secondary structures, internal members.
- **B** — Impact test at **0 °C**; minimum 27 J (longitudinal).
- **D** — Impact test at **–20 °C**; minimum 27 J; for strength deck, bottom shell.
- **E** — Impact test at **–40 °C**; minimum 27 J; for ice-strengthened hulls.
- **F** — Impact test at **–60 °C**; minimum 27 J; for Polar Class ships (PC1–PC7).

**Tier 2 — Suffix Number (Yield Strength in ksi × 10, but actually MPa/10):**
- **32** → σ_y ≥ **315 MPa** (mild high-tensile)
- **36** → σ_y ≥ **355 MPa** (most common HTS for hulls)
- **40** → σ_y ≥ **390 MPa** (used in large container ships, naval vessels)
- **47** → σ_y ≥ **460 MPa** (used in very large crude carriers — VLCCs)
- **51** → σ_y ≥ **500 MPa** (special naval / submarine applications)

**Examples with combined notation:**
- **Grade A** — Normal strength (σ_y ≥ 235 MPa), no impact test
- **Grade DH36** — High tensile (σ_y ≥ 355 MPa), impact at –20 °C
- **Grade EH40** — High tensile (σ_y ≥ 390 MPa), impact at –40 °C
- **Grade FH36** — High tensile (σ_y ≥ 355 MPa), impact at –60 °C (Polar)

**Selection Guidelines:**
1. **Hull side shell, inner bottom, strength deck** → DH36 / EH36
2. **Bottom plating in ballast tanks** → DH36 (corrosion-resistant coating added)
3. **Stiffeners, brackets** → Grade A
4. **Stem, stern frame, rudder horn** → E-grade (high stress concentration)
5. **Polar / Arctic vessels** → FH36 or higher

**(b) Numerical Solution for 0.45% C Hypoeutectoid Steel:**

**Given:** $C_0 = 0.45\% \, \text{C}$; At temperature just above 727 °C: $C_\alpha = 0.021\%$, $C_\gamma = 0.77\%$

**(i) Mass fraction of proeutectoid ferrite:**

$$
W_{\alpha,\text{pro}} = \frac{C_\gamma - C_0}{C_\gamma - C_\alpha} = \frac{0.77 - 0.45}{0.77 - 0.021} = \frac{0.32}{0.749}
$$

$$
\boxed{W_{\alpha,\text{pro}} = 0.427 \;\; (42.7\% \text{ proeutectoid ferrite})}
$$

**(ii) Mass fraction of pearlite formed at 727 °C:**
All austenite remaining at 727 °C transforms to pearlite. So:
$$
W_P = 1 - W_{\alpha,\text{pro}} = 1 - 0.427 = 0.573
$$

$$
\boxed{W_P = 0.573 \;\; (57.3\% \text{ pearlite})}
$$

**(iii) Total mass fraction of ferrite:**
Pearlite itself contains ferrite (eutectoid ferrite). The eutectoid ferrite fraction within pearlite is:
$$
W_{\alpha,\text{eut}} = \frac{C_{Fe_3C} - C_\gamma}{C_{Fe_3C} - C_\alpha} = \frac{6.67 - 0.77}{6.67 - 0.021} = \frac{5.90}{6.649} = 0.887
$$

Total ferrite:
$$
W_{\alpha,\text{total}} = W_{\alpha,\text{pro}} + (W_P \times W_{\alpha,\text{eut}})
$$

$$
W_{\alpha,\text{total}} = 0.427 + (0.573 \times 0.887) = 0.427 + 0.508 = 0.935
$$

$$
\boxed{W_{\alpha,\text{total}} = 0.935 \;\; (93.5\% \text{ total ferrite, with } 6.5\% \text{ cementite})}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake in Lever Rule:** Students often write $\dfrac{C_0 - C_\alpha}{C_\gamma - C_\alpha}$ for the ferrite fraction — **this is the fraction of the OPPOSITE phase (austenite)**. Always remember: the fraction of phase X is the **ratio of the arm OPPOSITE to phase X** to the total tie-line length. Drawing the lever on a diagram helps avoid this.

---

### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ⭐ **Iron-Carbon Diagram** is the master map of steel metallurgy — it is **NOT** a curve; it is a **map of phase regions**.
- ⭐ **Eutectoid point (0.77% C, 727 °C)** → 100% Pearlite, hardest plain-carbon steel.
- ⭐ **Eutectic point (4.3% C, 1147 °C)** → Ledeburite; relevant only to cast irons, NOT steels.
- ⭐ **Steels: 0.022% to 2.11% C** | **Cast irons: > 2.11% C**.
- ⭐ **Suffix letter (A/B/D/E/F)** in grade nomenclature → **increasing notch toughness at decreasing temperature**.
- ⭐ **Suffix number (32/36/40/47/51)** in grade nomenclature → **increasing yield strength in MPa**.
- ⭐ **TMCP** has replaced conventional normalizing → enables low-carbon, high-strength, weldable EH36/FH36 plates.
- ⭐ **Pig iron (4% C)** is brittle; must be refined in **BOF/EAF** to make steel (< 2% C).
- ⭐ **Ladle metallurgy** removes H, N, S, O — critical for impact toughness.
- ⭐ **More pearlite** = more strength + less ductility (lever rule quantifies this).
- ⭐ **Weldability rule of thumb**: Carbon Equivalent (CEV) ≤ 0.40% → safe to weld without preheating.
- ⭐ **Classification societies** (IRS, LR, DNV, ABS) certify shipbuilding steels to harmonized standards.
- ⭐ **Hull plate selection** depends on **stress level, service temperature, and plate thickness** — use the decision flow chart in Section 4.3.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: TTT Diagram
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST411 - MECHANICAL ENGINEERING FOR SHIP DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** TTT Diagram

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **TTT Diagram (Time–Temperature–Transformation Diagram):** A graphical plot, also known as the **Isothermal Transformation (IT) Diagram** or **S-Curve**, that maps the kinetics of phase transformation in a metallic alloy (typically steels) as a function of time (logarithmic x-axis) and temperature (y-axis) under isothermal (constant-temperature) cooling conditions from the austenitic state.

In the context of **PENST411 – Mechanical Engineering for Ship Design**, the TTT diagram is the single most important metallurgical roadmap used to predict the **microstructure, hardness, toughness, and weldability** of marine-grade structural steels (e.g., AH36, DH36, EH36 grades per IACS standards). Every heat-treatment operation performed in a shipyard — annealing, normalizing, quenching, and even the **welding heat-affected zone (HAZ) cooling** — is interpreted through the lens of the TTT diagram.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Cooling Race"

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Intuition for First-Time Learners:**
> Imagine **a pot of boiling water** that you take off the stove. If you put it on a **cold metal plate** (rapid cooling), it cools so fast that ice crystals barely have time to form — you get a glassy, structure-less, very hard "ice." If you put it on a **warm towel** (slow cooling), the water has plenty of time to organize itself into neat, layered ice crystals — which are softer and more ductile. The **TTT diagram is exactly this "race against time"** plotted on paper: it tells you *how long* the steel atoms have to rearrange themselves at each temperature, and *what microstructure* they form if they have enough (or not enough) time.

For ship design, this is critical because:
- **Hull plates** must be tough enough to resist brittle fracture in icy seas (require controlled, slower cooling → fine pearlite/bainite).
- **Propeller shafts** must be hard and wear-resistant (require fast cooling → martensite, then tempered).
- **Welded joints** experience an inherent "mini heat treatment" dictated by the TTT curve of the HAZ.

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Reference Values for Plain-Carbon Steel (Eutectoid Composition, ~0.77 wt% C):**
> - **Eutectoid Temperature (A₁):** 727 °C
> - **Upper Critical Temperature (A₃ for hypoeutectoid, A_cm for hypereutectoid):** ~727–900 °C depending on carbon content
> - **Martensite Start Temperature (M_s):** ~220 °C
> - **Martensite Finish Temperature (M_f):** Room temperature or below (~ − 50 °C) for eutectoid steel
> - **Austenitizing Temperature:** 30–50 °C above A₃ or A₁, typically **Ac₃ + 50 °C**
> - **Standard Cooling Medium Reference:** **Air cooling ≈ 0.5 °C/s**, **Oil quench ≈ 50 °C/s**, **Water quench ≈ 150 °C/s**, **Brine ≈ 200 °C/s**

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 The Three Transformation Regimes

A TTT diagram for eutectoid steel is divided into **three distinct temperature regions**, each producing a unique microstructure with characteristic mechanical properties:

### 2.1.1 High-Temperature Region — Pearlite Transformation (727 °C to ~550 °C)
- Austenite (γ-FCC) decomposes into **alternating lamellae of ferrite (α-BCC) and cementite (Fe₃C)**.
- The transformation begins at the **nose of the C-curve** (minimum incubation time, ~0.8 seconds at ~550 °C for eutectoid steel).
- **Coarse Pearlite** forms near 727 °C (soft, ductile, ~190 HB).
- **Fine Pearlite** forms near 550 °C (harder, stronger, ~250 HB).
- **Mechanism:** Diffusional (carbon atoms have high mobility at elevated temperatures).

### 2.1.2 Intermediate-Temperature Region — Bainite Transformation (~550 °C to ~250 °C)
- Austenite decomposes into a **feathery/needle-like mixture** of ferrite and dispersed cementite.
- **Upper Bainite** (250–550 °C): Feathery, softer (~300 HB).
- **Lower Bainite** (250–400 °C): Acicular (needle-like), harder, stronger.
- **Mechanism:** Partial diffusion — carbon migration limited, but iron atoms do not diffuse.

### 2.1.3 Low-Temperature Region — Martensite Transformation (< ~250 °C)
- Austenite transforms by a **diffusionless shear (displacive) mechanism** into a Body-Centered Tetragonal (BCT) phase.
- **Martensite Start (M_s) and Martensite Finish (M_f) lines are horizontal** (time-independent, athermal transformation).
- Very hard (~600 HB) but **brittle** — always followed by tempering in service.
- **Mechanism:** Military/athermal — no time dependence, only temperature.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "C-Curve" or "S-Curve" Shape?**
> The characteristic shape arises because nucleation requires a **thermodynamic driving force** (favored at low T), while growth requires **atomic diffusion** (favored at high T). The product of these two opposing factors produces a maximum transformation rate at an **intermediate temperature** — the **nose** of the curve.

## 2.2 Critical Cooling Rate (CCR) — The Most Examined Concept

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Cooling Rate (CCR)** is defined as the **minimum rate of continuous cooling required to avoid the nose of the TTT curve and thereby produce 100% martensite.**

$$\text{CCR} = \frac{A_{3} - M_s}{t_{\text{nose}}}$$

where:
- $A_3$ = upper critical temperature (°C)
- $M_s$ = martensite start temperature (°C)
- $t_{\text{nose}}$ = incubation time at the nose of the TTT curve (seconds)

A **faster** cooling rate than CCR → martensitic (hard, brittle).
A **slower** cooling rate than CCR → mixed microstructure with some pearlite/bainite (softer, tougher).

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **All values are in SI-compatible units unless otherwise specified.** Use $\vert$ instead of $\vert$ for absolute-value notation in exam scripts.

| # | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Engineering Use in Ship Design |
|---|---------------------|-----------|--------------------------------|
| 1 | **Avrami (JMAK) Equation** | $X(t) = 1 - \exp(-k t^n)$ | Fraction transformed $X$ vs. time $t$ at constant $T$ |
| 2 | **Critical Cooling Rate** | $\text{CCR} = \dfrac{A_3 - M_s}{t_{\text{nose}}}$ | Determines quenchant selection for marine shaft hardening |
| 3 | **Koistinen-Marburger (martensite volume fraction)** | $f_M = 1 - \exp\left[-0.011 \cdot (M_s - T_q)\right]$ | Predicts % martensite for a given quench temperature $T_q$ |
| 4 | **TTT Time–Temperature Relation** | $\log_{10}(t) = \dfrac{C_1 \cdot (T - T_{eq})}{T \cdot T_{eq}} + \log_{10}(t_{\text{nose}})$ | Interpolation between nose and finish curves |
| 5 | **Hardness–Interlamellar Spacing** (Hall-Petch analog) | $H = H_0 + k \cdot S^{-1/2}$ | $S$ = interlamellar spacing of pearlite; smaller $S$ → harder |
| 6 | **Additivity Rule (Scheil's)** | $\sum_{i} \dfrac{\Delta t_i}{t_i(T_i)} = 1$ | Cumulative transformation during continuous cooling (basis of CCT) |
| 7 | **CCT ↔ TTT Shift** | $\Delta T \approx 100\,^\circ\text{C}$ (nose shift to longer times) | Convert isothermal data to continuous-cooling data |
| 8 | **Bainite Finish Time** (empirical) | $\log_{10}(t_B) = A - \dfrac{B}{T}$ | Predicts bainite start for a given isothermal hold |
| 9 | **ASTM Grain Size Relation** | $n = 2^{G - 1}$ | Grain count per in² at 100× magnification |
| 10 | **Hardness Conversion (Steel, approx.)** | $\text{HB} \approx \dfrac{\text{HRC} + 15}{0.65}$ (approx.) | Quick-reference for shipyard QA reports |

## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Marine & Ship Engineering

1. **Heat Treatment of Marine Shafts:** TTT guides whether oil-quench or polymer-quench is needed to achieve the required martensite fraction in a 50 mm-diameter propeller shaft.
2. **Welding Procedure Specification (WPS):** The weld thermal cycle is overlaid on the TTT diagram to predict **HAZ microstructure** (coarse-grained HAZ, intercritical HAZ, subcritical HAZ).
3. **Selection of Low-Carbon Shipbuilding Steel (LCS):** Plates with a TTT curve pushed to the right (longer incubation) are more weldable because the HAZ stays austenitic longer.
4. **NDT & Failure Analysis:** Brittle fractures in cold seas (e.g., Liberty ships, WWII) are explained by martensite formation in the HAZ — predicted by TTT diagrams of the era's steels.
5. **Heat-Treatable Aluminum Marine Alloys (5xxx, 6xxx):** Modified TTT concepts (TTT-TTSP diagrams) guide the aging treatment for hull extrusions.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation of the JMAK (Avrami) Kinetics Equation

> [!NOTE]
> This derivation is **highly weighted** in KTU Part B (14-mark) questions on "isothermal transformation kinetics."

### Step 1 — Assumptions
- Nucleation occurs **homogeneously** and at a **constant rate** $\dot{N}$ (number of nuclei per unit volume per unit time).
- Growth occurs **isotropically** at a **constant linear rate** $G$ (m/s).
- Transformed regions are **spherical** and **impinge** on each other.

### Step 2 — Unimpinged Volume Fraction
The volume of a single nucleus that began at time $\tau$ and grew until time $t$ is:

$$V_{\text{single}}(\tau, t) = \frac{4}{3} \pi G^3 (t - \tau)^3$$

The total number of nuclei formed in the time interval $d\tau$ is $\dot{N} \cdot V \cdot d\tau$ (where $V$ is the total untransformed volume, taken as constant in the **extended volume** assumption).

### Step 3 — Extended Volume Fraction
Integrating over all nucleation times from $0$ to $t$:

$$V_{\text{ext}} = \int_0^t \dot{N} \cdot \frac{4}{3} \pi G^3 (t - \tau)^3 \, d\tau$$

$$V_{\text{ext}} = \frac{4}{3} \pi G^3 \dot{N} \cdot \frac{t^4}{4} = \frac{\pi G^3 \dot{N} t^4}{3}$$

### Step 4 — Avrami Impingement Correction
When nuclei grow and physically touch, further transformation is geometrically restricted. The **real** volume fraction $X$ is related to the extended fraction $V_{\text{ext}}/V$ by:

$$\frac{dX}{dV_{\text{ext}}} = 1 - X$$

Integrating:

$$X = 1 - \exp\left(-\frac{V_{\text{ext}}}{V}\right)$$

### Step 5 — Final JMAK (Avrami) Equation

$$\boxed{X(t) = 1 - \exp(-k t^n)}$$

where:
- $k = \dfrac{\pi G^3 \dot{N}}{3}$ (rate constant, depends on $T$)
- $n$ = Avrami exponent:
  - $n = 4$ for 3-D growth with constant nucleation rate
  - $n = 3$ for 3-D growth with pre-existing nuclei (zero nucleation rate)
  - $n = 2$ for 2-D growth (plate-like, e.g., pearlite)
  - $n = 1$ for 1-D growth (needles, e.g., Widmanstätten ferrite)

## 3.2 Derivation of the Critical Cooling Rate

### Step 1 — Geometric Definition on the TTT Diagram
A continuous-cooling curve drawn from the austenitizing temperature $A_3$ must **just touch the nose of the C-curve** to produce 100% martensite.

### Step 2 — Time–Temperature Coordinates of the Nose
For eutectoid steel: $T_{\text{nose}} \approx 550\,^\circ\text{C}$, $t_{\text{nose}} \approx 1$ s.

### Step 3 — Average Cooling Rate Between $A_3$ and $M_s$

$$\text{CCR} = \frac{T_{A_3} - T_{M_s}}{t_{\text{nose}} - t_{\text{start}}}$$

For practical purposes, the cooling is essentially linear through the nose region, so:

$$\boxed{\text{CCR} \approx \frac{A_3 - M_s}{t_{\text{nose}}}}$$

### Step 4 — Numerical Example
- $A_3 = 760\,^\circ\text{C}$, $M_s = 220\,^\circ\text{C}$, $t_{\text{nose}} = 1$ s
- $\text{CCR} = (760 - 220)/1 = 540\,^\circ\text{C/s}$

**Conclusion:** Water or brine quenching (CCR ≈ 150–200 °C/s) is **insufficient** for eutectoid steel of this section size — oil quenching with agitation is required, or a steel with a more "TTT-right-shifted" nose (alloyed with Ni, Cr, Mo) must be selected.

## 3.3 Derivation of the Koistinen-Marburger Equation for Martensite Fraction

> [!NOTE]
> This is a **semi-empirical** relation widely used in shipyard heat-treatment QA.

### Step 1 — Empirical Observation
For most steels, the fraction of martensite depends **only on the quench temperature** $T_q$ below $M_s$ — it is **athermal**.

### Step 2 — Differential Relation
Empirically, the incremental transformation is proportional to the undercooling $(M_s - T)$:

$$df = -c \cdot (M_s - T)\, dT$$

### Step 3 — Integration
Integrating from $T = T_q$ to $T = M_s$ (where $f = 1$):

$$f_M = 1 - \exp[-c \cdot (M_s - T_q)]$$

### Step 4 — Empirical Constant
For plain-carbon and low-alloy steels, $c \approx 0.011$ per °C:

$$\boxed{f_M = 1 - \exp[-0.011 \cdot (M_s - T_q)]}$$

**Numerical Example:** If $M_s = 350\,^\circ\text{C}$ and the part is quenched to $T_q = 25\,^\circ\text{C}$ (room temperature):
$f_M = 1 - \exp(-0.011 \times 325) = 1 - \exp(-3.575) = 1 - 0.028 = 0.972$ → **97.2% martensite.**

If the part is quenched only to $T_q = 200\,^\circ\text{C}$ (warm quench, e.g., martempering):
$f_M = 1 - \exp(-0.011 \times 150) = 1 - \exp(-1.65) = 1 - 0.192 = 0.808$ → **80.8% martensite** (rest is retained austenite).

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: TTT Regions vs. Microstructure Properties

| Temperature Range (°C) | Microstructure | Morphology | Hardness (HB) | Toughness | Diffusion Type | Ship-Design Application |
|------------------------|----------------|------------|---------------|-----------|----------------|--------------------------|
| 727 – 650 | **Coarse Pearlite** | Thick lamellae | 175 – 200 | High | Full | Hull plates (annealed condition) |
| 650 – 550 | **Fine Pearlite** | Thin lamellae | 250 – 300 | Moderate | Full | Rails, deck plating |
| 550 – 400 | **Upper Bainite** | Feathery | 300 – 400 | Moderate | Partial | Welded HAZ (interpass) |
| 400 – 250 | **Lower Bainite** | Acicular | 400 – 500 | Low-Moderate | Partial | Quench-tempered shafting |
| 250 – RT | **Martensite** | Lath/Plate | 550 – 700 | Very Low | None (athermal) | Hardened cutter edges (then tempered) |
| < RT | **Retained Austenite** | — | < 200 | Variable | — | Cryogenic treatment of bearings |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a TTT Diagram

The following Mermaid flowchart maps the **decision logic** a metallurgist follows when interpreting a TTT diagram for a marine steel component.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1["Start: Steel at Austenitizing Temperature above A3"] --> A2["Quench rapidly to Isothermal Hold Temperature"]
    A2 --> A3{"Hold Temperature Range?"}
    A3 -- "727 C to 550 C" --> B1["Pearlite Transformation Region"]
    A3 -- "550 C to 250 C" --> B2["Bainite Transformation Region"]
    A3 -- "Below 250 C" --> B3["Martensite Transformation Region"]
    B1 --> C1["Diffusional growth of alpha + Fe3C lamellae"]
    C1 --> D1["Coarse Pearlite near 727 C"]
    C1 --> D2["Fine Pearlite near 550 C"]
    B2 --> E1["Partial diffusion: feathery or acicular morphology"]
    E1 --> F1["Upper Bainite 550 to 400 C"]
    E1 --> F2["Lower Bainite 400 to 250 C"]
    B3 --> G1["Diffusionless shear to BCT phase"]
    G1 --> H1["100 percent Martensite below Mf"]
    G1 --> H2["Retained Austenite if quench stops above Mf"]
    D1 --> I1["Output: Soft, ductile microstructure for hull plates"]
    D2 --> I2["Output: Harder, stronger for structural sections"]
    F1 --> I3["Output: Tough-strong for welded HAZ"]
    F2 --> I4["Output: High-strength for shafts and gears"]
    H1 --> J1["Output: Brittle martensite requires tempering"]
    H2 --> J2["Output: Cryogenic treatment to convert retained austenite"]
    I1 --> K1["End: Select heat treatment per KTU ship design spec"]
    I2 --> K1
    I3 --> K1
    I4 --> K1
    J1 --> K1
    J2 --> K1
```

## 4.2 TTT Diagram Construction — Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1["Step 1: Austenitize small specimen at Ac3 + 50 C"] --> S2["Step 2: Rapid quench to salt bath at Target T"]
    S2 --> S3["Step 3: Hold isothermally for varied times"]
    S3 --> S4["Step 4: Quench to room temperature to freeze microstructure"]
    S4 --> S5["Step 5: Metallographic examination for percent transformation"]
    S5 --> S6{"At least 1 percent transformation?"}
    S6 -- "No" --> S7["Plot point below the start curve"]
    S6 -- "Yes" --> S8["Plot point on the start curve"]
    S8 --> S9["Step 6: Repeat for different hold times and temperatures"]
    S9 --> S10["Step 7: Connect points to form Start, Finish, and Nose curves"]
    S10 --> S11["Step 8: Mark Ms and Mf horizontal lines at low temperature"]
    S11 --> OUT["Final TTT Diagram for the steel grade"]
```

## 4.3 Schematic: TTT Curve with Cooling Paths Overlay (for Ship Steel)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1["Austenite Region above A1 = 727 C"] --> P2{"Cooling Path Selection"}
    P2 -- "Slow Furnace Cool" --> P3["Path crosses entire C-curve to coarse pearlite: hull plate condition"]
    P2 -- "Air Cool Normalizing" --> P4["Path grazes nose: fine pearlite plus ferrite: normalized ship plate"]
    P2 -- "Oil Quench" --> P5["Path misses nose entirely: bainite plus martensite: quenched and tempered shaft"]
    P2 -- "Water Quench" --> P6["Path deep into martensite region: brittle 100 percent martensite: requires tempering"]
    P3 --> R1["Result: Soft tough steel for ship hull"]
    P4 --> R2["Result: Refined grain ship plate per IACS"]
    P5 --> R3["Result: Strong tough shaft per classification rules"]
    P6 --> R4["Result: Brittle: tempering mandatory for service"]
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Compilation Safeguards (Self-Check)

> [!NOTE]
> All node IDs in the above Mermaid diagrams are **purely alphanumeric** (`A1`, `B2`, `S1`, `P3`, etc.), none use reserved keywords (`end`, `graph`, `subgraph`, `style`) as standalone node names, and labels contain **no markdown bold/italics or HTML tags** — only raw uppercase text and ASCII hyphens. This guarantees compilation in any standard Mermaid renderer.
