# 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model]
**Define a TTT diagram. Why is it also called an S-curve or C-curve?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A Time–Temperature–Transformation (TTT) diagram is a plot of **transformation start and finish times** for austenite decomposition as a function of temperature under **isothermal** conditions, typically on a **logarithmic time axis** and a **linear temperature axis** (1 mark). It is called an S-curve or C-curve because the start and finish lines, when plotted, resemble the letter **"C"** (or "S" when read from start to finish) due to the competing effects of thermodynamic driving force (favoring low $T$) and atomic diffusion (favoring high $T$) (1 mark). For eutectoid steel, the minimum transformation time (~0.8 s) occurs at ~550 °C, known as the **nose of the curve** (1 mark).

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model]
**What is the critical cooling rate? How is it related to the TTT diagram?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **Critical Cooling Rate (CCR)** is the **minimum continuous-cooling rate** required to **bypass the nose of the TTT curve** and obtain **100% martensite** (1 mark). It is calculated as $\text{CCR} = (A_3 - M_s)/t_{\text{nose}}$, where $A_3$ is the upper critical temperature, $M_s$ is the martensite start, and $t_{\text{nose}}$ is the time at the nose (1 mark). A higher CCR is required for steels with a nose shifted to the left (short incubation), and alloying elements (Ni, Cr, Mo) shift the nose right, lowering the CCR and improving hardenability (1 mark).

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Model Paper, 2024 Scheme]**

> **OR**

### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Model Paper, 2024 Scheme]**

---

#### **Question A:**

**Q. (a)** With a neat sketch, explain the construction of a TTT diagram for eutectoid steel. Label all important regions, temperatures, and microstructures. **[7 marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Construction Procedure (4 marks):**
1. Cut small specimens (typically 10–15 mm spheres or thin discs) of eutectoid steel.
2. Austenitize each specimen at ~760 °C (Ac₃ + 30 °C) for sufficient time to obtain 100% austenite.
3. Rapidly transfer the specimen to a **molten salt bath** held at the desired isothermal temperature (e.g., 700 °C, 650 °C, 600 °C, 550 °C, 500 °C, 400 °C, 300 °C).
4. Hold for progressively increasing times.
5. Quench each specimen to **room temperature** to freeze the high-temperature microstructure.
6. Perform **metallographic examination** to determine whether transformation has started (1%), is in progress (50%), or is complete (99%).
7. Plot the start (1%), half (50%), and finish (99%) times on a **log time** vs. **linear temperature** graph.
8. Connect similar-percentage points to form three C-shaped curves.
9. Mark horizontal lines at $M_s$ and $M_f$ at the bottom of the diagram (these are athermal).

**Description of the Diagram (3 marks):**
- The diagram is divided by a **horizontal line at 727 °C (A₁)**. Above this, only austenite is stable.
- The **C-curve** has its **nose at ~550 °C** (incubation time ~0.8 s).
- Above the nose (727–550 °C): **Pearlite region** — coarse at 727 °C, fine at 550 °C.
- Below the nose to 250 °C: **Bainite region** — upper bainite (550–400 °C), lower bainite (400–250 °C).
- Below ~250 °C: **Martensite region** — athermal transformation between $M_s$ and $M_f$.

**Diagram Reference (textual schematic):**

```
T(°C)
  A3
   |    AUSTENITE
727|---A1-- (727 °C eutectoid line) ----------
   |   /  start
   |  / /
   | / /  (PEARLITE region)
   |/ /
   *  (Nose at 550 °C, t = 0.8 s)
   |\ \
   | \ \  (BAINITE region)
   |  \ \
   |   \  finish
250|---Ms (horizontal line ~220 °C)
   |   \  (MARTENSITE region)
 RT|---Mf
   +---------------------------> log t (s)
```

**Q. (b)** A eutectoid steel has the following TTT data: $A_3 = 760\,^\circ\text{C}$, $M_s = 200\,^\circ\text{C}$, time at nose = 1 s. Determine the **critical cooling rate**. If a 30 mm diameter shaft of this steel is cooled in oil (cooling rate ~30 °C/s) and in water (~150 °C/s), predict the microstructure obtained in each case. **[7 marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Critical Cooling Rate (3 marks):**
$$\text{CCR} = \frac{A_3 - M_s}{t_{\text{nose}}} = \frac{760 - 200}{1} = 560\,^\circ\text{C/s}$$

**Step 2 — Comparison with Cooling Rates (2 marks):**
- Oil cooling: 30 °C/s $\ll$ 560 °C/s → cooling curve **crosses the nose** → **no martensite**; mixture of **fine pearlite + bainite**.
- Water cooling: 150 °C/s $\ll$ 560 °C/s (still less) → cooling curve **still crosses the nose** for a 30 mm diameter bar (mass effect reduces effective surface cooling rate). Microstructure: **predominantly bainite + some martensite** near the surface.

**Step 3 — Conclusion (2 marks):**
For this plain-carbon eutectoid steel, **even water quenching may not produce 100% martensite** in a 30 mm section. To achieve full hardening, one must either:
- Use a steel with **higher hardenability** (alloyed with Ni, Cr, Mo) which shifts the nose to the right, or
- Use a more aggressive quench medium (brine, polymer quench with agitation), or
- Reduce the section size.

---

#### **Question B (Alternative Choice for Internal Option):**

**Q. (a)** Differentiate between **TTT** and **CCT** diagrams. Explain why the CCT nose is shifted to longer times and lower temperatures compared to the TTT nose, with a neat sketch. **[7 marks]**

**Model Solution:**

| Aspect | TTT Diagram | CCT Diagram |
|--------|-------------|-------------|
| Cooling Mode | Isothermal (constant T) | Continuous (linear T vs. t) |
| Transformation | Occurs at a single T | Occurs over a range of T |
| Nose Position | Reference | Shifted **right & down** |
| Practical Use | Lab/theoretical | Industrial heat treatment |

**Why the CCT Nose is Shifted (4 marks):**
1. **Finite time spent at each temperature** — In a continuous-cooling experiment, the specimen spends only a brief interval at each temperature, not enough time to complete transformation at the TTT nose temperature.
2. **Lower effective undercooling** — Continuous cooling does not allow equilibration; the transformation is delayed and pushed to lower temperatures where the driving force is higher.
3. **Additivity (Scheil's rule)** — The cumulative transformation fraction follows $\sum \Delta t_i / t_i(T_i) = 1$, which is reached at **longer total times** than the isothermal nose.
4. **Typical shift magnitude:** ~50–100 °C lower and **~10× longer time** at the nose.

**Sketch Description (2 marks):**
- Draw two C-curves on the same axes: TTT (solid line) nose at ~550 °C, 1 s; CCT (dashed line) nose at ~500 °C, 10 s.
- Overlay several linear cooling paths: very slow → coarse pearlite; fast → martensite.
- Mark the **critical cooling rate** line tangent to the CCT nose.

**Q. (b)** A marine propeller shaft of 100 mm diameter is made of a 0.4 % C steel. Using the **Koistinen-Marburger relation**, find the **% martensite** formed if the surface is quenched from 850 °C austenitizing temperature to (i) 25 °C and (ii) 180 °C. Take $M_s = 330\,^\circ\text{C}$. Comment on the implications for ship-service performance. **[7 marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Koistinen-Marburger Equation:**
$$f_M = 1 - \exp[-0.011 \cdot (M_s - T_q)]$$

**Case (i): Quench to 25 °C (3 marks):**
$$f_M = 1 - \exp[-0.011 \cdot (330 - 25)] = 1 - \exp[-0.011 \cdot 305]$$
$$= 1 - \exp(-3.355) = 1 - 0.0349 = 0.9651$$
$$\boxed{f_M = 96.51\% \text{ martensite}}$$

**Case (ii): Quench to 180 °C — Martempering (3 marks):**
$$f_M = 1 - \exp[-0.011 \cdot (330 - 180)] = 1 - \exp[-0.011 \cdot 150]$$
$$= 1 - \exp(-1.65) = 1 - 0.1920 = 0.8080$$
$$\boxed{f_M = 80.80\% \text{ martensite}}$$

**Implications for Ship Service (1 mark):**
- Quenching to 25 °C gives **higher martensite %** but with **higher residual stresses** and **risk of quench cracking** in a 100 mm shaft.
- Quenching to 180 °C (martempering) gives **80% martensite** with **significantly lower thermal gradients**, reducing distortion and crack risk — preferred for marine shafts to meet IACS dimensional tolerances.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Mixing up TTT and CCT:** Students often draw the cooling curve on a TTT diagram and use it as a CCT — **these are different**; CCT nose is shifted right and down. **(−1 to −2 marks)**
> 2. **Forgetting the athermal nature of martensite:** Writing "martensite forms over time at low T" is **wrong** — it forms **instantaneously** as $T$ falls below $M_s$, and time is irrelevant. **(−1 mark)**
> 3. **Unit errors in CCR:** Always use **°C/s** for cooling rate, not °C/min. **(−0.5 mark)**
> 4. **Wrong $M_f$ for eutectoid steel:** Many students write $M_f = 0\,^\circ\text{C}$ — it is actually **below 0 °C** for eutectoid plain-carbon steel, and full transformation requires sub-zero treatment. **(−1 mark)**
> 5. **Forgetting the nose temperature:** Always mention **~550 °C, 0.8 s** for eutectoid steel in any descriptive answer — examiners expect this landmark value. **(−1 mark)**
> 6. **Avrami exponent confusion:** Pearlite = $n = 2$ (or 3), not $n = 4$ — write the correct one for the microstructure in question. **(−1 mark)**

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (high-density bullet summary):**
> - ☐ **TTT = Time–Temperature–Transformation** = Isothermal diagram = S/C-curve.
> - ☐ **Austenitizing T** = Ac₃ + 30 to 50 °C; held until 100% γ.
> - ☐ **Nose of eutectoid steel:** **~550 °C, ~0.8 s** (most-tested landmark).
> - ☐ **Three product regions:** Pearlite (727–550 °C), Bainite (550–250 °C), Martensite (< 250 °C).
> - ☐ **Pearlite** is lamellar (α + Fe₃C), **Bainite** is feathery/acicular, **Martensite** is BCT laths/plates.
> - ☐ **M_s and M_f are horizontal lines** — **athermal, time-independent**.
> - ☐ **Eutectoid composition** = **0.77 wt% C**; **Eutectoid T** = **727 °C (A₁)**.
> - ☐ **Critical Cooling Rate (CCR)** = $(A_3 - M_s)/t_{\text{nose}}$; > CCR → 100% martensite.
> - ☐ **JMAK / Avrami:** $X = 1 - \exp(-k t^n)$; $n$ depends on nucleation and growth dimensionality.
> - ☐ **Koistinen-Marburger:** $f_M = 1 - \exp[-0.011 (M_s - T_q)]$.
> - ☐ **CCT nose** is **shifted right & down** vs. TTT (~50–100 °C lower, ~10× longer time).
> - ☐ **Alloying elements (Ni, Cr, Mo, Mn)** shift TTT/CCT nose to the right → **higher hardenability** → preferred for marine shafts.
> - ☐ **Martempering** = quench to just above $M_s$, hold, then air-cool → less distortion, ~80% martensite.
> - ☐ **Austempering** = quench into 250–400 °C salt bath, hold to complete bainite transformation → no separate tempering needed.
> - ☐ **TTT for ship design:** guides WPS, HAZ prediction, heat-treated shaft hardness, and brittle-fracture avoidance in cold seas.
> - ☐ **Hardness order (steel):** Martensite > Lower Bainite > Upper Bainite > Fine Pearlite > Coarse Pearlite.
> - ☐ **Scheil's additivity rule:** $\sum \Delta t_i / t_i(T_i) = 1$ — basis for converting TTT → CCT data.
> - ☐ **Draw cooling curves as straight lines on T-T diagram** with slope = cooling rate (°C/s).
> - ☐ **Welding HAZ = "mini-thermal cycle"** — overlay on TTT to predict whether martensite forms in coarse-grained HAZ.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Heat Treatment
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PENST411 - MECHANICAL ENGINEERING FOR SHIP DESIGN > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Heat Treatment # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Aligned) **Heat Treatment** is a precisely controlled sequence of heating and cooling operations applied to metals and alloys in the solid state to obtain desired mechanical, physical, and chemical properties *without altering the overall shape or chemical composition* of the component. In the context of **ship design and marine engineering**, heat treatment is indispensable for tailoring the properties of: - **Hull structural steel** (grades AH36, DH36, EH36, AH40 – per IACS standards) - **Propeller shafts** (forged bronze, monel, or maraging steels) - **Marine crankshafts and connecting rods** - **Anchor chains and mooring fittings** - **Marine boiler plates and pressure vessels** - **Turbine blades and gears in ship propulsion systems** > [!NOTE] > **Key Definition (KTU Board Standard):** > *"Heat treatment is an operation or combination of operations involving controlled heating and cooling of a metal or alloy in the solid state for the purpose of obtaining desired properties."* > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why Heat Treatment Matters in Ship Design:** > A 200,000 DWT bulk carrier may consume **~25,000 tonnes of shipbuilding steel**. Heat treatment ensures the hull plates possess adequate **yield strength (≥355 MPa for AH36)**, **impact toughness at sub-zero temperatures (Charpy V-notch test at -40°C for polar vessels)**, and **weldability**. Sub-standard heat treatment of marine steel is a leading cause of brittle fracture failures at sea. ## 1.2 The Four Universal Stages of Every Heat Treatment Every heat treatment process — regardless of the specific type — follows these four fundamental stages: 1. **Heating Stage:** The component is heated from room temperature to a predefined *austenitizing temperature* at a controlled rate (to avoid thermal shock and distortion). 2. **Soaking Stage (Holding/Austenitizing):** The component is held isothermally at the target temperature to allow complete transformation of the microstructure into a uniform phase (usually **austenite – γ-iron**). 3. **Cooling Stage:** The component is cooled at a *specific controlled rate* (furnace, air, oil, water, brine, or polymer quench) that determines the final microstructure. 4. **Post-Cooling Operations:** May include tempering, stress relieving, or straightening. ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "Cooking a Steak" 🍳 Imagine you are a chef preparing a steak (your workpiece), and your goal is to control its **tenderness, juiciness, and texture** (mechanical properties): | Cooking Step | Heat Treatment Equivalent | Purpose | |--------------|--------------------------|---------| | Pre-heating the pan slowly | Slow heating to austenitizing temperature | Avoid thermal shock / cracking | | Searing at high heat for 1 min each side | Soaking at austenitizing temperature | Achieve uniform internal "doneness" (austenite) | | Plunging into ice water | Quenching in brine/oil/water | Lock in the desired "texture" (martensite) | | Resting in foil for 10 min | Tempering at 200–650°C | Reduce internal stresses, improve toughness | - A **rare steak** (cooled slowly in air) = **Coarse pearlite** (soft, ductile) → analogous to **annealing**. - A **well-done steak seared hard and plunged in cold water** (rapid quench) = **Hard martensite** (very hard, brittle) → analogous to **hardening**. - A steak **rested and held at low heat for some time** = **Tempered martensite** (hard + tough) → the **balanced, ship-grade** result we want. > [!TIP] > **Student Mnemonic: "HSSC"** → **H**eating, **S**oaking, **C**ooling, **C**onditioning. Memorize this — it is the universal template examiners expect in any "explain the steps of heat treatment" question. ## 1.4 Critical Reference Temperatures (Bold Constants to Memorize) For plain carbon steel, the following reference points on the **Iron-Carbon (Fe-Fe₃C) Equilibrium Diagram** are essential: - **A₁ (Lower Critical Temperature) = 727°C** — The eutectoid transformation temperature (ferrite + austenite ↔ austenite on heating). - **A₃ (Upper Critical Temperature)** — Temperature at which ferrite (α) fully transforms to austenite (γ) for hypoeutectoid steels (C < 0.77%). Depends on carbon content. - **A_cm** — Temperature at which cementite (Fe₃C) fully dissolves into austenite for hypereutectoid steels (C > 0.77%). - **M_s (Martensite Start)** ≈ 200–250°C for eutectoid steel. - **M_f (Martensite Finish)** ≈ -50°C for eutectoid steel. - **Eutectoid Composition = 0.77% Carbon** — the most reactive composition. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Classification of Heat Treatment Processes Heat treatment processes are systematically classified based on the **phase transformation induced** and the **rate of cooling** employed. ### 2.1.1 Annealing (Softening Process Family) Annealing involves heating the steel to (or near) the A₃ or A_cm temperature, soaking, and **cooling very slowly** (typically inside the furnace itself — *furnace cooling*). The objective is to: - Soften the steel for subsequent machining - Refine the grain structure - Relieve internal residual stresses from prior working - Improve ductility and electrical/magnetic properties **Sub-types of Annealing:** | # | Type | Heating Temperature | Cooling Method | Primary Purpose | |---|------|--------------------|----------------|----------------| | 1 | **Full Anneal** | 30–50°C above A₃ (for hypo), A₁ + 20–30°C (for hyper) | Very slow furnace cooling | Maximum softness, complete recrystallization | | 2 | **Process Anneal** | Just below A₁ (≈ 650–700°C) | Air cool | Stress relief after cold working | | 3 | **Stress-Relief Anneal** | 550–650°C | Slow cool | Remove residual stresses from welding/machining | | 4 | **Spheroidizing Anneal** | Just above A₁, hold long, then slow cool | Very slow | Globular cementite → best machinability | | 5 | **Isothermal Anneal** | Above A₃, then rapid cool to just below A₁, hold | Air cool after isothermal hold | Shorter cycle than full anneal | | 6 | **Diffusion (Homogenizing) Anneal** | 1100–1200°C, hold 10–20 h | Slow cool | Eliminate dendritic segregation in ingots | ### 2.1.2 Normalizing - Heat to **30–50°C above A₃** (or A_cm for hyper-eutectoid). - Hold to achieve uniform austenite. - Cool in **still air** (faster than furnace cooling, slower than oil quenching). - Result: **Fine, uniform pearlite + ferrite** with higher strength and hardness than annealed state. - **Application in Shipbuilding:** Normalized shipbuilding steel plates (ASTM A36 normalized, ABS Grade A normalized) for hull structures requiring consistent impact resistance. ### 2.1.3 Hardening (Quenching) - Heat to **austenitizing temperature** (30–50°C above A₃ for hypo-eutectoid). - Soak for sufficient time. - Quench rapidly in water, brine (10% NaCl), oil, or polymer (polyalkylene glycol — *PAG*). - Result: **Martensitic transformation** (BCT — body-centered tetragonal structure) — extremely hard but brittle. - **Quenching Media Severity (Grossmann's H-value, decreasing order):** 1. Caustic soda + water (H ≈ 2.0) 2. Brine (H ≈ 1.5) 3. Water (H ≈ 1.0) 4. Oil (H ≈ 0.3) 5. Air (H ≈ 0.02) ### 2.1.4 Tempering (Re-heating After Hardening) Re-heating the hardened (martensitic) steel to a temperature **below A₁**, holding, then cooling. Three stages of tempering occur as temperature rises: | Stage | Temperature Range | Microstructural Change | Result | |-------|-------------------|------------------------|--------| | Stage I | 100–250°C | ε-carbide precipitation, reduction of C supersaturation | Slight hardness drop, slight toughness gain | | Stage II | 200–300°C | Decomposition of retained austenite | Hardness may temporarily rise | | Stage III | 250–650°C | Cementite (Fe₃C) formation + coarsening, α-ferrite recovery | Significant hardness drop, large toughness gain | - **Low-temperature tempering (150–250°C):** Retains high hardness, used for cutting tools, gears. - **Medium-temperature tempering (350–500°C):** "**Troostite**" — good balance of hardness and toughness, used for **marine propeller shafts, crankshafts, connecting rod bolts**. - **High-temperature tempering (500–650°C):** "**Sorbite**" — optimum combination of strength and ductility. When done after normalizing, the process is called **"Quenching and Tempering (Q&T)"** — the **gold standard for marine grade steel**. ### 2.1.5 Surface Hardening (Case Hardening) Used when the surface must resist wear/abrasion while the core must remain tough. The surface layer (case) is enriched with carbon or nitrogen, then hardened. | Process | Temperature | Hardening Agent | Case Depth | |--------|------------|-----------------|------------| | **Carburizing** | 850–950°C | Carbon (solid/liquid/gas) | 0.5 – 2 mm | | **Nitriding** | 500–550°C | Nitrogen (NH₃ gas) | 0.1 – 0.7 mm | | **Cyaniding** | 800–870°C | Carbon + Nitrogen (NaCN) | 0.1 – 0.5 mm | | **Carbonitriding** | 700–900°C | C + N (gas mixture) | 0.1 – 0.75 mm | | **Induction Hardening** | Surface (skin) heating | Electromagnetic induction | 1 – 6 mm | | **Flame Hardening** | Surface (skin) heating | Oxy-acetylene flame | 2 – 6 mm | | **Laser / Electron Beam Hardening** | Surface (skin) heating | Focused beam | 0.3 – 1.5 mm | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Marine Application Highlight:** > **Nitriding** is widely used for **marine diesel engine cylinder liners, crankshafts, and gear teeth** because nitrided surfaces retain hardness at elevated temperatures (up to 500°C) and have excellent corrosion resistance in salt-water environments. ## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Examination-Critical) | # | Formula / Relation | Physical Meaning | Units / Notes | |---|--------------------|------------------|---------------| | 1 | $HB = \dfrac{2F}{\pi D \left( D - \sqrt{D^2 - d^2} \right)}$ | Brinell Hardness Number (BHN) | F in kgf; D, d in mm | | 2 | $HV = 1.854 \cdot \dfrac{F}{d^2}$ | Vickers Hardness Number (VHN) | F in kgf; d = diagonal in mm | | 3 | $HRC \approx 10 \cdot \log_{10} \left( \dfrac{HV}{10} \right) - 0.0$ (approx.) | Approx. Vickers → Rockwell C conversion | Empirical | | 4 | $\dfrac{d\gamma}{dt} = -k \left( T(t) - T_{\infty} \right)$ | Newton's Law of Cooling during quench | T in K, k = cooling rate constant | | 5 | $T(t) = T_{\infty} + (T_0 - T_{\infty}) \cdot e^{-kt}$ | Temperature vs. time during cooling | t in seconds | | 6 | $P = T \cdot \left( \log_{10} t + C \right)$ | **Hollomon–Jaffe Tempering Parameter** | T in Kelvin, t in hours, C ≈ 20 | | 7 | $HRC_{J} = f(\text{ distance from quenched end })$ | **Jominy End-Quench Test** (hardenability curve) | Jominy distance in 1/16 inch (J) | | 8 | $D_I = D_C \cdot \left( \dfrac{H_{sample}}{H_{Jominy\,at\,D_I}} \right)$ | Grossmann's ideal critical diameter relation (qualitative) | Hardenability | | 9 | $Ac_1 = 727^{\circ}C$ | Lower critical temperature (eutectoid) | Constant for plain carbon steel | | 10 | $\% \text{Martensite} = e^{-0.011 \cdot (M_s - T_q)}$ | Koistinen-Marburger relation | M_s = Martensite start; T_q = Quench temperature | > [!NOTE] > **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE:** Whenever you write absolute values, modulus, or "such that" conditions in your KTU answer sheet, NEVER use the bare pipe symbol $\vert$ inside markdown table cells — always wrap with `\vert` or `\mid` to keep the table parser happy. ## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Hull Plates (ABS/DNV/LR Standards):** Quenched & Tempered (Q&T) steel grades like **EH36** achieve yield strength ≥ 355 MPa with Charpy impact ≥ 34 J at -40°C, mandatory for ice-class vessels. - **Propeller Material:** Manganese–aluminum bronze or Ni-Al bronze propellers may be **heat-treated (homogenization + quench)** to eliminate coring segregation and prevent dezincification in seawater. - **Anchor Chain (Grade U2/U3, R3/R4 per IACS UR W22):** Requires **normalizing + tempering** to achieve specified impact toughness at -20°C. - **Diesel Engine Cylinder Cover / Piston Crown:** Induction hardened for wear resistance. - **Marine Gearbox Pinion & Bull Gear:** Case-carburized or nitrided depending on load profile. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1: Temperature–Time Relationship During Quench (Newton's Law of Cooling, Modified for Quench Severity) When a hot steel component is plunged into a quenching medium, the surface heat is extracted at a rate that determines whether the steel transforms to pearlite, bainite, or martensite. The lumped-capacitance form of Newton's cooling law models this. **Statement:** The rate of change of component temperature is proportional to the temperature difference between the component and the quenching bath. $$ \frac{dT(t)}{dt} = -h \cdot \frac{A}{m \cdot c_p} \left[ T(t) - T_{\infty} \right] $$ **Step-by-step integration to obtain $T(t)$:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Let } \theta(t) &= T(t) - T_{\infty} \\ \text{Then } \frac{d\theta}{dt} &= -h \cdot \frac{A}{m c_p} \cdot \theta(t) \\ \text{Separate variables:} \quad \frac{d\theta}{\theta} &= -\left(\frac{hA}{m c_p}\right) dt \\ \text{Integrate from } t=0 \text{ to } t: \quad \int_{\theta_0}^{\theta} \frac{d\theta}{\theta} &= -\frac{hA}{m c_p} \int_0^t dt \\ \ln \left( \frac{\theta}{\theta_0} \right) &= -\frac{hA}{m c_p} \cdot t \\ \frac{\theta}{\theta_0} &= \exp \left( -\frac{hA}{m c_p} \cdot t \right) \end{aligned} $$ Substitute back $\theta = T - T_{\infty}$ and $\theta_0 = T_0 - T_{\infty}$: $$ \boxed{\, T(t) = T_{\infty} + (T_0 - T_{\infty}) \cdot \exp \left[ -\frac{hA}{m c_p} \cdot t \right] \,} $$ **Where:** - $T(t)$ = component temperature at time $t$ (°C) - $T_0$ = initial austenitizing temperature (°C) - $T_{\infty}$ = quench bath temperature (°C) - $h$ = convective heat transfer coefficient at the surface (W/m²K) — **higher for brine, lower for oil** - $A$ = surface area of component (m²) - $m$ = mass of component (kg) - $c_p$ = specific heat capacity of steel ≈ **0.486 kJ/(kg·K) = 486 J/(kg·K)** **Engineering Insight:** Larger $h$ (e.g., brine quench) → steeper exponential decay → faster cooling → favors **martensite** formation. Smaller $h$ (e.g., oil quench) → gradual decay → favors **bainite or fine pearlite**. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2: Koistinen–Marburger Equation for Martensite Fraction This is the **empirical equation** universally used in KTU Board problems to calculate the percentage of martensite formed at a given quench temperature. **Starting empirical form:** $$ f_M = 1 - \exp \left[ -\alpha \cdot (M_s - T_q) \right] $$ **Where:** - $f_M$ = volume fraction of martensite (0 to 1) - $M_s$ = martensite start temperature (°C) - $T_q$ = quench temperature (°C) — temperature at which the steel is interrupted from cooling - $\alpha$ = rate constant ≈ **0.011 per °C** (for most carbon and low-alloy steels) **Step-by-step simplification to the standard form:** $$ \begin{aligned} 1 - f_M &= \exp \left[ -\alpha (M_s - T_q) \right] \\ \ln(1 - f_M) &= -\alpha (M_s - T_q) \\ (M_s - T_q) &= -\frac{1}{\alpha} \ln(1 - f_M) \\ T_q &= M_s + \frac{1}{\alpha} \ln(1 - f_M) \end{aligned} $$ **Worked Numerical Example:** Given: A plain carbon steel with $M_s = 200^{\circ}C$ is quenched to a temperature $T_q = 50^{\circ}C$. Find the % martensite formed using $\alpha = 0.011$. $$ \begin{aligned} f_M &= 1 - \exp \left[ -0.011 \cdot (200 - 50) \right] \\ &= 1 - \exp \left[ -0.011 \cdot 150 \right] \\ &= 1 - \exp(-1.65) \\ &= 1 - 0.1920 \\ &= 0.808 \end{aligned} $$ Therefore, **80.8% martensite** has formed when the steel reaches 50°C. --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3: Hollomon–Jaffe Tempering Parameter and its Use in Estimating Hardness After Tempering **Concept:** The combined effect of tempering temperature $T$ (absolute) and time $t$ on the resulting hardness can be expressed through a single **tempering parameter** $P$, also called the **Hollomon–Jaffe parameter**: $$ P = T \cdot \left[ \log_{10}(t) + C \right] $$ **Where:** - $T$ = tempering temperature in **Kelvin** (i.e., $T_{^{\circ}C} + 273$) - $t$ = tempering time in **hours** - $C$ = constant ≈ **20** for most steels **Step-by-step derivation of equivalent tempering conditions:** Suppose steel is tempered at $T_1$ for $t_1$ hours and we wish to find the equivalent time $t_2$ at a different temperature $T_2$ that yields the same hardness (i.e., same $P$). $$ \begin{aligned} P_1 &= P_2 \\ T_1 \left[ \log_{10}(t_1) + C \right] &= T_2 \left[ \log_{10}(t_2) + C \right] \\ \log_{10}(t_2) &= \frac{T_1}{T_2} \left[ \log_{10}(t_1) + C \right] - C \\ t_2 &= 10^{\left\{ \frac{T_1}{T_2} \left[ \log_{10}(t_1) + C \right] - C \right\}} \end{aligned} $$ **Worked Numerical Example:** A marine propeller shaft is tempered at $600^{\circ}C$ for 2 hours. If we wish to use $500^{\circ}C$ instead, what time is required to obtain the same hardness? (Use $C = 20$.) $$ \begin{aligned} T_1 &= 600 + 273 = 873 \text{ K} \\ T_2 &= 500 + 273 = 773 \text{ K} \\ t_1 &= 2 \text{ h} \\ \log_{10}(t_2) &= \frac{873}{773} \left[ \log_{10}(2) + 20 \right] - 20 \\ &= (1.1294) \left[ 0.3010 + 20 \right] - 20 \\ &= (1.1294)(20.3010) - 20 \\ &= 22.9294 - 20 \\ &= 2.9294 \\ t_2 &= 10^{2.9294} \approx 850 \text{ hours} \end{aligned} $$ **Interpretation:** Lowering the tempering temperature by 100°C extends the required time from 2 hours to ~850 hours — a vivid demonstration of why **tempering temperature is the dominant variable**, not time. --- ## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — "Which Heat Treatment for Which Marine Component?" | Marine Component | Required Properties | Recommended Heat Treatment | Why This Treatment? | |------------------|--------------------|---------------------------|---------------------| | Hull plate (AH36) | High strength + weldability + impact toughness | **Normalizing** or **Q&T** | Uniform fine-grain pearlite gives strength without HAZ cracking | | Marine propeller shaft | High fatigue strength + toughness | **Quench + Medium-Temp Tempering** | Troostite structure (350–500°C temper) = ideal fatigue strength | | Anchor chain (studless) | High impact at -20°C | **Normalizing** | Eliminates coarse Widmanstätten structure | | Diesel engine gear | Hard surface + tough core | **Carburizing + Quench + Temper** | Case ~60 HRC, core ~30 HRC | | Cylinder liner | Wear resistance + seawater corrosion | **Nitriding** | Nitrided layer retains hardness at high T and resists corrosion | | Boiler pressure vessel | Creep resistance + toughness | **Normalizing + Tempering** | Stable ferrite-pearlite + stress relief | | Stainless steel pipe (sea water system) | Corrosion resistance | **Solution Annealing** (1050°C + water quench) | Dissolves Cr carbides → restores passivity | > [!TIP] > **Student Tip for 14-Mark Answers:** Always close such a matrix-based answer with a sentence like *"In shipbuilding practice, Q&T steel grades account for over 60% of high-toughness marine structural applications (ABS Grade EH36, EH40, FH36)."* # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Decision Tree for Selecting a Heat Treatment Process ```mermaid flowchart TD startA[Start: Desired Property] --> q1{What property
is required?} q1 -->|Softness for machining| annealRoute[ANNEALING Route] q1 -->|Uniform strength| normRoute[NORMALIZING Route] q1 -->|Maximum hardness| hardRoute[HARDENING Route] q1 -->|Hard surface + Tough core| surfRoute[SURFACE HARDENING] q1 -->|Stress relief only| stressRoute[STRESS RELIEF] annealRoute --> annealSub{Type?} annealSub --> anneal1[Full Anneal 30-50C above A3] annealSub --> anneal2[Process Anneal below A1] annealSub --> anneal3[Spheroidizing Anneal] normRoute --> normStep[Heat 30-50C above A3 then air cool] hardRoute --> hardSub{Quench medium?} hardSub --> h1[Brine: H = 1.5] hardSub --> h2[Water: H = 1.0] hardSub --> h3[Oil: H = 0.3] hardSub --> h4[Polymer: H = 0.3 to 1.0] h1 --> mart[Austenite transforms to MARTENSITE] h2 --> mart h3 --> mart h4 --> mart mart --> tempStep[Must TEMPER afterwards] tempStep --> tempSub{Tempering
temperature?} tempSub --> t1[Low T 150-250C Cutting tools] tempSub --> t2[Medium T 350-500C Marine shafts] tempSub --> t3[High T 500-650C Q and T marine plates] surfRoute --> surfSub{Process?} surfSub --> s1[Carburizing - C diffusion 850-950C] surfSub --> s2[Nitriding - N diffusion 500-550C] surfSub --> s3[Induction Hardening] surfSub --> s4[Flame Hardening] stressRoute --> stressStep[Heat to 550-650C hold then slow cool] t2 --> endNodeA[Component Ready] t3 --> endNodeA s1 --> endNodeB[Case Hardened Component Ready] s2 --> endNodeB endNodeA --> finalA[Marine component delivered] endNodeB --> finalB[Marine component delivered] ``` ## 4.2 Mermaid Schematic — Iron-Carbon (Fe-Fe₃C) Diagram Functional Map ```mermaid flowchart LR fePure[Pure Iron Fe
0 percent C] --> a1[Room T: Ferrite alpha BCC] a1 --> a2[912C: alpha to gamma transformation] a2 --> a3[912 to 1394C: Austenite gamma FCC] a3 --> a4[Above 1394C: delta Ferrite BCC] fePure --> a5[Add Carbon up to 0.022 percent] a5 --> a6[At 727C: Ferrite + Cementite mixture] fePure --> eutPoint{Eutectoid point
0.77 percent C at 727C} eutPoint --> eut1[100 percent PEARLITE on slow cool] fePure --> hypereut[HYPEREUTECTOID 0.77 to 2.14 percent C] hypereut --> hypereut1[Proeutectoid Cementite + Pearlite] fePure --> hypoEut[HYPoeutectoid 0.022 to 0.77 percent C] hypoEut --> hypoEut1[Proeutectoid Ferrite + Pearlite] a1 --> acmLine[Ac3 line for hypo steels] a1 --> acmLine2[Acm line for hyper steels] acmLine --> hypoEut acmLine2 --> hypereut eutPoint --> acmLine eutPoint --> acmLine2 ``` ## 4.3 Mermaid Flowchart — TTT (Time–Temperature–Transformation) Diagram Interpretation ```mermaid flowchart TD timeAxis[Time axis logarithmic scale] --> tempAxis[Temperature axis from high to low] tempAxis --> a1Line[Upper critical Ac1 at 727C] a1Line --> a3Line[Ac3 line for hypo steels] a3Line --> msLine[Ms line Martensite start 200 to 250C] msLine --> mfLine[Mf line Martensite finish near 50C] a1Line --> noseBend[C-curve nose region maximum nucleation rate around 550C] noseBend --> aboveNose[Pearlite transformation above nose] noseBend --> belowNose[Bainite transformation below nose] msLine --> martZone[Martensite zone instantaneous diffusionless] noseBend --> critCool[CRITICAL COOLING RATE tangent to nose] a3Line --> startCool[Cooling curve from austenitizing T] startCool --> check1{Cooling rate relative to critical?} check1 -->|Faster than critical| goMart[Pure Martensite formed] check1 -->|Slower than critical| goMix[Pearlite and Ferrite mixture] check1 -->|Interrupted hold between Ms and nose| goBain[Bainite formation] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading Tip for Students:** The TTT diagram is the "**secret decoder**" of heat treatment. Every quench, every isothermal hold, and every cooling strategy can be plotted on it to predict the final microstructure. In KTU 14-mark questions, always draw a representative TTT diagram and overlay the cooling curve — full marks are awarded for the diagram, not the text. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question A1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Define heat treatment. List the various heat treatment processes commonly used in marine engineering.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** Heat treatment is a controlled sequence of heating and cooling operations applied to a metal or alloy in the solid state to alter its mechanical, physical, or chemical properties without changing its shape or chemical composition. (1.5 marks) In marine engineering, the commonly used heat treatment processes are: (1) **Annealing** (full, process, stress-relief, spheroidizing), (2) **Normalizing**, (3) **Hardening (quenching)**, (4) **Tempering**, (5) **Surface hardening** (carburizing, nitriding, induction hardening), and (6) **Solution treatment** for stainless steels and non-ferrous marine alloys. (1.5 marks) --- ### Question A2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **What is the eutectoid composition in the Fe-Fe₃C diagram? What is its significance?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The eutectoid composition in the Fe-Fe₃C equilibrium diagram corresponds to **0.77% Carbon** at a temperature of **727°C (A₁)**. (1 mark) Significance: (a) At this composition, on slow cooling, a single-phase austenite transforms completely to **100% pearlite** (a lamellar mixture of ferrite and cementite). (1 mark) (b) The eutectoid steel exhibits the **maximum hardness** among plain carbon steels for a given cooling rate and is the most responsive to heat treatment, making it ideal for marine cutting tools and high-strength components. (1 mark) --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice ### ➤ QUESTION A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]` **Q. (a)** Explain in detail the various **types of annealing** processes used in shipbuilding, with their temperatures, cooling methods, and applications. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution — Part (a) — 7 marks:** Annealing is a softening heat treatment involving heating to a specific temperature, soaking, and slow cooling. Various types used in shipbuilding: 1. **Full Annealing (1.5 marks):** Hypoeutectoid shipbuilding steel (e.g., AH36) is heated **30–50°C above the A₃ temperature** (≈ 850–880°C for 0.2% C steel), held for sufficient time to achieve uniform austenite, then **furnace cooled** very slowly (~ 30°C/hour). The resulting microstructure is **coarse pearlite + ferrite**, giving minimum hardness, maximum ductility, and best machinability. Used for ship propeller pre-machining operations and large forged marine shaft blanks before final Q&T. 2. **Process Annealing (1 mark):** Conducted at a temperature **just below A₁** (≈ 650–700°C), used to relieve work-hardening from cold forming of marine sheet steel and plates. The steel is held, then air cooled. Cheaper than full annealing. 3. **Stress-Relief Annealing (1 mark):** Performed at **550–650°C** to relieve residual stresses from welding of hull sections and from cold straightening of plates. Critical for welded ship hulls to prevent stress-corrosion cracking (SCC) in service. 4. **Spheroidizing Annealing (1 mark):** Used for high-carbon steels (e.g., marine cutting tool steel, anchor chain studs). Steel is heated to **just above A₁** and held for a long duration (4–12 hours), then cooled very slowly. Cementite transforms into **globular spheroidite** — the softest possible structure, best for machining. 5. **Isothermal Annealing (1 mark):** Steel is heated above A₃, then rapidly transferred to a separate furnace held at **just below A₁** (~ 680°C). It is held isothermally until transformation is complete, then air cooled. Used in batch production of marine fasteners and bolts. 6. **Diffusion / Homogenizing Annealing (0.5 mark):** Performed at **1100–1200°C** for 10–20 hours to eliminate dendritic segregation in large marine propeller and shaft ingots. Slow furnace cooling follows. 7. **Conclusion (1 mark):** The choice of annealing depends on steel composition, prior history, and final required property — softer for machining, harder for strength. In shipbuilding, stress-relief and full annealing are the most commonly specified per IACS UR W-series guidelines. --- **Q. (b)** With a neat sketch, describe the **Iron-Carbon (Fe-Fe₃C) equilibrium diagram** and discuss the **critical temperatures** A₁, A₃, and A_cm. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution — Part (b) — 7 marks:** **Sketch (3 marks):** A well-labeled Fe-Fe₃C diagram should show: - Y-axis: Temperature (0°C to ~1600°C) - X-axis: Carbon % (0% to 6.67%) - Three single-phase regions: **α (Ferrite, BCC), γ (Austenite, FCC), Fe₃C (Cementite)** - Two-phase regions: α+γ, α+Fe₃C, γ+Fe₃C, L+γ, L+Fe₃C - The **eutectic point (4.3% C at 1147°C)** and **eutectoid point (0.77% C at 727°C)** - Critical lines: A₁ (727°C horizontal), A₃ (curved upper boundary of γ), A_cm (curved solubility limit of C in γ) - The lines Ac₁, Ac₃, Ar₁, Ar₃ to denote *heating* and *cooling* transformations (Ac = chauffage, Ar = refroidissement) **Description of Critical Temperatures (4 marks):** 1. **A₁ — Lower Critical Temperature (727°C):** This is the **eutectoid isotherm** below which austenite (γ) is thermodynamically unstable in plain carbon steel. On slow cooling, austenite transforms to **pearlite** (α + Fe₃C lamellae). On heating, the reverse reaction (pearlite → austenite) occurs. This temperature is *invariant* for plain carbon steels and forms the base of all heat treatment cycles. 2. **A₃ — Upper Critical Temperature (hypoeutectoid):** This is the temperature at which **ferrite (α) completely dissolves into austenite (γ)** on heating. A₃ depends on carbon content: for 0% C, A₃ ≈ 912°C; for 0.77% C, A₃ coincides with A₁ at 727°C. Heat treatment processes like normalizing, full annealing, and hardening specify temperatures 30–50°C above A₃ to ensure complete austenitization. 3. **A_cm — Upper Critical Temperature (hypereutectoid):** This is the temperature at which **cementite (Fe₃C) completely dissolves into austenite (γ)** on heating. It applies to hypereutectoid steels (C > 0.77%). A_cm decreases as C increases, reaching 727°C at 0.77% C. For spheroidizing annealing, the temperature is held just above A_cm to allow globular cementite formation. 4. **Subscript Distinction (1 mark):** Ac₁, Ac₃, Ac_cm denote *heating* (chauffage) critical temperatures; Ar₁, Ar₃, Ar_cm denote *cooling* (refroidissement) critical temperatures. Ac values are always slightly higher than Ar values due to **thermal hysteresis** (~ 10–30°C). This hysteresis is the fundamental reason for the **hysteresis loop in TTT diagrams**. 5. **Engineering Significance:** Knowledge of these critical temperatures is the *sine qua non* of every heat treatment specification. Incorrect austenitizing temperature (e.g., heating a hypereutectoid steel above A₁ but below A_cm) results in incomplete austenitization and uneven hardness — a common shipbuilding failure mode. --- ### ➤ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Q. (a)** Describe the various **surface hardening processes** used for marine components. Compare carburizing and nitriding in detail. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution — Part (a) — 7 marks:** **Surface Hardening Processes (3.5 marks):** Surface hardening is used when a marine component must have a **wear-resistant, hard surface (case)** while retaining a **tough, shock-resistant core**. The processes are: 1. **Carburizing:** Heating low-carbon steel (0.1–0.2% C) at **850–950°C** in a carbonaceous environment (solid — charcoal + BaCO₃; liquid — NaCN bath; gas — propane/butane). Carbon diffuses into the surface to a depth of **0.5–2 mm**. After carburizing, the component is **quenched** to harden the case. Used for **marine gears, camshafts, piston pins**. 2. **Nitriding:** Heating medium-carbon steel (containing Al, Cr, Mo) at **500–550°C** in ammonia gas (NH₃). Nitrogen dissociates and diffuses into the surface forming hard **iron nitrides (Fe₂–₃N, Fe₄N)**. Case depth: **0.1–0.7 mm**. No quenching required. Used for **marine diesel cylinder liners, crankshafts, gear teeth**. 3. **Cyaniding:** Heating in a **sodium cyanide (NaCN) bath** at 800–870°C. Provides carbon + nitrogen in a thin case (0.1–0.5 mm). Less common today due to environmental hazards of cyanide. 4. **Carbonitriding:** Gas-phase process using a mixture of carburizing gas and ammonia at 700–900°C. Case depth 0.1–0.75 mm. Lower temperature than carburizing → less distortion. 5. **Induction Hardening:** Surface is rapidly heated by electromagnetic induction (10–500 kHz), then quenched by water spray. Case depth 1–6 mm. Used for **marine crankshaft journals, large gear teeth**. 6. **Flame Hardening:** Surface heated by oxy-acetylene torch, then quenched. Case depth 2–6 mm. Used for large marine components like kingposts and rudder pintles. 7. **Laser / Electron Beam Hardening:** Localized, precise surface heating with minimal distortion. Emerging for high-precision marine components. **Comparison: Carburizing vs Nitriding (3.5 marks):** | Parameter | Carburizing | Nitriding | |-----------|-------------|-----------| | Temperature | 850–950°C | 500–550°C | | Element diffused | Carbon (C) | Nitrogen (N) | | Steel composition | Low-carbon steel | Alloy steel with Al, Cr, Mo | | Quenching required | Yes (must quench after) | No (diffusion hardening in situ) | | Case depth | 0.5–2 mm | 0.1–0.7 mm | | Case hardness | ~ 60 HRC | ~ 60–70 HRC surface, very hard | | Distortion | High (high T, quenching) | Very low (low T, no quench) | | Fatigue strength | Good | Excellent (residual compressive stresses) | | Corrosion resistance | Moderate | Superior (nitrides resist corrosion) | | Hot hardness | Lost above ~ 200°C | Retained up to ~ 500°C | | Marine application | Gears, pinions | Cylinder liners, crankshafts | | Process duration | 4–8 hours | 20–50 hours (very slow) | | Equipment cost | Moderate | High (requires ammonia gas system) | --- **Q. (b)** Explain the **Jominy End-Quench Test** for determining **hardenability**. Why is hardenability more important than hardness in marine component selection? **(7 marks)** **Model Solution — Part (b) — 7 marks:** **The Jominy End-Quench Test (4 marks):** The Jominy end-quench test, standardized under **ASTM A255**, is used to measure the **hardenability** of a steel — i.e., the depth to which the steel will harden when quenched. **Procedure:** 1. A standard cylindrical specimen (25 mm diameter × 100 mm long) is **austenitized** at the specified temperature (typically 845–870°C) for a fixed soaking time. 2. The specimen is then quickly transferred to a fixture, where **only the bottom end face** is sprayed with a column of water at room temperature. 3. This produces a continuously **decreasing cooling rate** along the length of the specimen — the bottom end cools fastest, the top end cools slowest. 4. After the specimen has fully cooled, two parallel flats (~ 0.4 mm deep) are ground on opposite sides. 5. **Rockwell C hardness** is measured at 1/16 inch (1.5875 mm) intervals starting from the quenched end, denoted as **J₁, J₂, J₃, ..., J₅₀**, etc. 6. A plot of **Hardness (HRC) vs. Jominy distance (1/16 inch)** is drawn, called the **Jominy curve** or **hardenability curve**. **Interpretation:** - A **steep drop** in the Jominy curve indicates **low hardenability** (shallow hardening, plain carbon steel). - A **gradual drop** indicates **high hardenability** (deep hardening, alloy steel with Cr, Ni, Mo, Mn). **Why Hardenability > Hardness in Marine Component Selection (3 marks):** 1. **Hardness** is a *local* property at the surface, but marine components (e.g., 200 mm diameter propeller shaft) experience stress throughout the section, not just the surface. A shaft that is surface-hard but has a soft core will fail by **torsional fatigue** from the core outward. 2. **Hardenability** describes how deeply the hardening effect penetrates during quenching. A steel with high hardenability (e.g., **AISI 4340, EN25, 30CrNiMo8** — common marine shaft alloys) will harden throughout the entire cross-section, ensuring **uniform strength from surface to core**. 3. Marine shafts of large cross-section **cannot** be through-hardened using low-hardenability steels because the cooling rate at the center is too slow to form martensite — only bainite or pearlite forms, giving inadequate strength. 4. The IACS UR W22 specification for marine anchor chains and the DNV GL rules for marine shafts require **specified Jominy end-quench hardness values at specific distances** (e.g., HRC ≥ 35 at J₂₀ for grade R4 chain). Compliance is impossible without high hardenability. 5. Therefore, marine engineers select **alloy steels** (Cr, Ni, Mo additions) over plain carbon steels not for surface hardness, but for the **deep, uniform hardening** that high hardenability provides — ensuring safety against brittle fracture, fatigue, and impact loading in harsh marine service. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Read Carefully Before Writing Your Exam:** > > 1. **The "Four Steps" Trap:** Many students write "heating, soaking, cooling, tempering" and stop. The fourth step is **NOT always tempering** — for annealing and normalizing, the fourth step is *furnace cooling* or *air cooling*. Examiners specifically deduct 1 mark if the steps are listed generically without process-specific customization. > > 2. **Drawing the Fe-Fe₃C Diagram Without Axis Labels:** A diagram without °C on the Y-axis and %C on the X-axis loses **1 full mark** instantly. Labeling subscripts A₁, A₃, A_cm, marking the eutectoid point (0.77% C, 727°C) is mandatory. > > 3. **Confusing Hardness with Hardenability:** Hardness = material property at a point. Hardenability = depth of hardening. This is the most common 14-mark question. Examiners award 0 marks to students who use the words interchangeably. > > 4. **Forgetting Ac vs Ar:** Examiners love to ask "why is Ac₁ > Ar₁?" Students who do not mention **thermal hysteresis** and the **diffusionless nature of cooling transformations** lose 2 marks. > > 5. **Calculation Errors in Hollomon–Jaffe:** Forgetting to **convert °C to Kelvin** (add 273) is the #1 numerical error. Always re-state the unit conversion explicitly in the solution. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) > [!TIP] > **Final 60-Second Revision — Memorize Before Entering the Exam Hall:** - [ ] **Heat Treatment Definition:** Controlled heating + cooling in solid state; property change without shape change. - [ ] **Universal Four Stages:** Heating → Soaking → Cooling → Post-conditioning. (Mnemonic: **HSSC**) - [ ] **Critical Temperatures:** **A₁ = 727°C** (eutectoid, fixed); **A₃** (hypo, upper, variable); **A_cm** (hyper, upper, variable). - [ ] **Eutectoid composition = 0.77% C**; **Eutectic composition = 4.3% C** at 1147°C. - [ ] **Cooling rate increases hardness** (anneal → normalize → oil quench → water quench → brine quench). - [ ] **TTT Diagram:** C-curve; nose at ~550°C; above nose = pearlite; below nose = bainite; below M_s = martensite. - [ ] **Martensite = hard + brittle**; needs tempering for toughness. - [ ] **Tempering Temperature Ranges:** Low (150–250°C), Medium (350–500°C), High (500–650°C). - [ ] **Marine Q&T Steel (EH36, EH40, FH36, AH40):** Yield ≥ 355 MPa, Charpy ≥ 34 J at -40°C (polar). - [ ] **Jominy Test = hardenability, not hardness** — measured in 1/16 inch (J) from quenched end. - [ ] **Carburizing vs Nitriding:** Carburizing needs quench, higher T (850–950°C), deeper case; Nitriding is in-situ, lower T (500–550°C), harder case, no distortion. - [ ] **Quench Severity (Grossmann H):** Brine > Water > Oil > Air. - [ ] **Newton's Law of Cooling:** $T(t) = T_{\infty} + (T_0 - T_{\infty})e^{-hAt/(mc_p)}$ - [ ] **Koistinen–Marburger:** $f_M = 1 - \exp[-\alpha(M_s - T_q)]$ with $\alpha = 0.011/^{\circ}C$ - [ ] **Hollomon–Jaffe:** $P = T \cdot [\log_{10}(t) + C]$, $C = 20$, $T$ in Kelvin. - [ ] **Hull Plate Standards:** ASTM A36, A131; ABS Grades A, B, D, E, AH36, DH36, EH36, FH36. - [ ] **Common Marine Alloys for Heat Treatment:** AISI 1040, 4140, 4340, EN25, EN26, 30CrNiMo8, 17-4 PH (stainless). - [ ] **Propeller Alloys:** Manganese bronze, Ni-Al bronze, Monel — usually homogenized at 700–800°C. - [ ] **Marine Anchor Chain Grades:** U1, U2, U3 (studless); R3, R3S, R4 (studded) per IACS UR W22.

topics.title: Protection from Corrosion: Ferrous and Non-Ferrous Alloys, Non-Metallic Materials - Composites
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST411 - MECHANICAL ENGINEERING FOR SHIP DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Protection from Corrosion: Ferrous and Non-Ferrous Alloys, Non-Metallic Materials - Composites

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Corrosion in the Marine Context?

**Corrosion** is the irreversible deterioration of a metal or alloy caused by an electrochemical reaction with its surrounding environment. In the context of **ship design**, corrosion is not merely a maintenance nuisance — it is a **primary design driver** that governs material selection, structural scantlings, lifecycle cost, and seaworthiness. A ship is essentially a floating electrochemical cell where dissimilar metals, an aggressive electrolyte (seawater), and varying oxygen concentrations conspire continuously to destroy the structure.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PENST411 Module 1):**
> *"Corrosion in marine environment is the degradation of metallic components of a ship due to electrochemical reactions, accelerated by the presence of dissolved salts (typically ~3.5% NaCl), elevated humidity (often >80% RH), galvanic coupling between dissimilar metals, microbiological activity, and stray currents. Protection from corrosion encompasses material selection, surface treatments, cathodic protection, and the use of non-metallic composites."*

### 1.2 Types of Marine Corrosion Encountered on Ships

| Type | Mechanism | Common Location on Ship |
|---|---|---|
| **Uniform / General Corrosion** | Even material loss across exposed surface | Hull plates, ballast tanks, weather decks |
| **Pitting Corrosion** | Localized deep cavities, often under deposits | Stainless steel piping, propellers |
| **Crevice Corrosion** | Stagnant electrolyte in tight gaps | Behind stiffeners, under gaskets, lap joints |
| **Galvanic Corrosion** | Dissimilar metals coupled in electrolyte | Bronze valve + steel pipe, Monel + steel |
| **Intergranular Corrosion** | Attack along grain boundaries | Sensitized austenitic stainless steel exhausts |
| **Erosion-Corrosion** | Mechanical wear + chemical attack | Pump impellers, propeller blade tips |
| **Cavitation Corrosion** | Vapor bubble collapse damage | Propellers, rudders, pump casings |
| **Stray Current Corrosion** | External DC currents leaking into hull | Near welding machines, impressed current systems |
| **Microbiologically Influenced Corrosion (MIC)** | Bacterial action (SRB) creates localized cells | Ballast tanks, bilges, pipework with sludge |

---

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — The "Ship as a Battery" Concept

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy (for intuitive understanding):**
> Imagine a ship as a giant, slow-motion, leaking battery. The seawater acts as the **electrolyte** (like the wet paste inside a battery), the hull acts as one **electrode**, and the propeller shaft (bronze) acts as the other **electrode**. Just as in a battery, electrons flow from the more reactive metal (hull steel — anode) to the less reactive metal (propeller — cathode), and the steel literally dissolves into the sea to supply those electrons. **Corrosion protection, therefore, is the art of either (a) sealing the metals off from the electrolyte (coatings), (b) replacing the hull with a "sacrificial" metal that dissolves first (sacrificial anodes), or (c) forcing electrons back into the hull using an external power supply (impressed current).** The designer must think of the ship not as inert steel, but as a living electrochemical system from day one of design.

## 1.4 Why Corrosion is the #1 Threat to Ship Integrity

The marine environment is uniquely aggressive because seawater combines **four** corrosion-accelerating factors simultaneously:

1. **High electrical conductivity** of seawater (~**50,000 µS/cm**) vs fresh water (~200 µS/cm) — promotes galvanic cells.
2. **High dissolved oxygen** — drives cathodic reduction reactions.
3. **Chloride ions (Cl⁻)** — break down passive films on stainless steel and aluminum.
4. **Biological organisms** — barnacles, mussels, algae create differential aeration cells.

> [!TIP]
> **Key Engineering Metric (KTU High-Yield):**
> Unprotected mild steel corrodes in seawater at approximately **0.1 to 0.5 mm/year** — meaning a 10 mm hull plate could be perforated in just **20 to 100 years**, but with localized pitting, perforation can occur in **less than 5 years** if unprotected. This is why classification societies (DNV, Lloyd's, ABS, IRS) mandate corrosion protection systems as a **rule-level requirement**, not an option.

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Electrochemistry of Marine Corrosion

Corrosion of iron in aerated seawater follows the fundamental **anodic** and **cathodic** half-reactions:

**Anodic Reaction (Oxidation — metal dissolves):**
$$\text{Fe} \rightarrow \text{Fe}^{2+} + 2e^{-}$$

**Cathodic Reaction (Reduction — in aerated neutral seawater):**
$$\text{O}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} + 4e^{-} \rightarrow 4\text{OH}^{-}$$

**Overall Corrosion Product Formation:**
$$\text{Fe}^{2+} + 2\text{OH}^{-} \rightarrow \text{Fe(OH)}_2 \rightarrow \text{FeOOH (rust)}$$

The corrosion rate is governed by **Faraday's Law**, which links current (electron flow) directly to mass loss.

## 2.2 Faraday's Law of Corrosion (Derivation Foundation)

The mass of metal lost through corrosion is directly proportional to the total electric charge passed:

$$m = \frac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$$

where:
- $m$ = mass of metal corroded (grams)
- $I$ = corrosion current (Amperes)
- $t$ = time (seconds)
- $M$ = molar mass of the metal (g/mol)
- $n$ = number of electrons transferred per atom (valence)
- $F$ = Faraday's constant = **96,485 C/mol** (≈ **96,500 C/mol** for engineering use)

### 2.2.1 Corrosion Rate (CR) Engineering Form

By substituting $I = i \cdot A$ (current density × area) and converting units, the widely-used **corrosion rate expression** is:

$$CR = \frac{K \cdot i \cdot M}{n \cdot \rho}$$

where:
- $CR$ = corrosion rate (mm/year)
- $i$ = corrosion current density (µA/cm²)
- $\rho$ = density of metal (g/cm³)
- $K$ = unit conversion constant = **0.00327** (when $i$ is in µA/cm² and rate in mm/year)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conversion Constant Origin (K = 0.00327):**
> $K = \frac{31.5 \times 10^6 \text{ (s/year)} \times 10^{-6} \text{ (A/µA)} \times 10^2 \text{ (cm²/m²)} \times 10^{-3} \text{ (mm/µm)}}{10^4 \text{ (cm²/m²)}}$ 
> This is a derived constant — examiners love asking this in Part A!

## 2.3 Cathodic Protection (CP) — The Designer-Controlled Solution

Cathodic Protection is the **most important corrosion control method in ship design**, mandated by IACS and Classification Societies for hulls, ballast tanks, and sea-water systems. There are two distinct methods:

### 2.3.1 Sacrificial Anode Cathodic Protection (SACP)

A more electrochemically active metal (Zn, Al, Mg) is electrically connected to the hull. The anode "sacrifices" itself by corroding preferentially, protecting the hull.

**Key Design Equation — Anode Life:**

$$T = \frac{m \cdot Q_{\text{util}}}{I_{\text{req}}}$$

where:
- $T$ = effective life of anode (years)
- $m$ = mass of anode (kg)
- $Q_{\text{util}}$ = practical electrochemical capacity (Ah/kg) — **Zn ≈ 780 Ah/kg, Al ≈ 2500 Ah/kg, Mg ≈ 1230 Ah/kg**
- $I_{\text{req}}$ = total current requirement (A)

### 2.3.2 Impressed Current Cathodic Protection (ICCP)

An external DC power source forces current from inert anodes (Pt-coated Ti, mixed metal oxide) through seawater to the hull, making the hull cathodic. Used on large commercial vessels, tankers, naval ships.

**Required Current Density Values (from DNV-RP-B401 / NACE):**

| Ship Region | Bare Steel | Coated Steel |
|---|---|---|
| Hull (underwater) | 100–150 mA/m² | 10–30 mA/m² |
| Ballast tanks | 100–120 mA/m² | 20–30 mA/m² |
| Internals (coated) | — | 5–15 mA/m² |

## 2.4 Marine Coatings — The First Line of Defense

Modern ship coating systems are **multi-layer composites** themselves, typically comprising:

1. **Shop Primer** (15–20 µm) — for blast-cleaned plates before fabrication.
2. **Anti-Corrosive Epoxy Primer** (100–200 µm) — barrier + chemical resistance.
3. **Anti-Fouling / Tie Coat** (75–100 µm) — for underwater hull, prevents marine growth.
4. **Top Coat** (50–75 µm) — UV resistance, color, cosmetic finish.

**Total Dry Film Thickness (DFT)** for ballast tanks per IMO PSPC: **320 µm minimum**.

## 2.5 The Galvanic Series in Seawater (Critical for Material Pairing)

When two dissimilar metals are coupled in seawater, the **more anodic (active) member corrodes**, and the **more cathodic (noble) member is protected**. Ship designers must consult this series when selecting coupled components.

| Position | Metal / Alloy | Potential (V vs Ag/AgCl) |
|---|---|---|
| **Most Active (Anodic)** | Magnesium | −1.60 V |
| ↓ | Zinc | −1.05 V |
| ↓ | Aluminum alloy | −0.75 to −0.95 V |
| ↓ | Mild Steel | −0.60 to −0.70 V |
| ↓ | Cast Iron | −0.55 V |
| ↓ | Stainless Steel (active) | −0.45 V |
| ↓ | Copper | −0.30 V |
| ↓ | Brass (Cu-Zn) | −0.28 V |
| ↓ | Bronze (Cu-Sn) | −0.25 V |
| ↓ | Monel (Ni-Cu) | −0.20 V |
| ↓ | Titanium | −0.05 V |
| **Most Noble (Cathodic)** | Platinum | +0.20 V |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Shipboard Mistake (KTU Pitfall):**
> Direct coupling of **stainless steel propellers to mild steel shafts** or **bronze valves in steel pipework** without isolating flanges (non-conductive gaskets + sleeves) is the #1 cause of accelerated hull pitting near fittings. **Always specify dielectric isolation kits** for dissimilar metal joints in piping class specifications.

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Quick Reference)

| # | Formula | Application | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $m = \frac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$ | Mass of metal lost | grams |
| 2 | $CR = \frac{0.00327 \cdot i \cdot M}{n \cdot \rho}$ | Corrosion rate | mm/year |
| 3 | $T_{\text{anode}} = \frac{m_{\text{anode}} \cdot Q_{\text{util}}}{I_{\text{req}}}$ | Sacrificial anode life | years |
| 4 | $I_{\text{req}} = j_{\text{req}} \cdot A_{\text{wetted}}$ | Total CP current | Amperes |
| 5 | $R_{\text{coat}} = \frac{\rho_{\text{coat}} \cdot t_{\text{film}}}{A}$ | Coating resistance (Ohm's law analog) | Ohms |
| 6 | $N_{\text{anodes}} = \frac{I_{\text{req}}}{I_{\text{single}}}$ | Number of anodes | dimensionless |
| 7 | $A_{\text{anode}} = \frac{I_{\text{req}} \cdot T \cdot 8760}{Q_{\text{util}} \cdot U_z}$ | Anode mass with utilization factor | kg |
| 8 | $E_{\text{cell}} = E_{\text{cathode}} - E_{\text{anode}}$ | Driving voltage for galvanic cell | Volts |

> [!NOTE]
> **Where:** $F$ = 96,485 C/mol, $U_z$ = utilization factor (0.5–0.9 for Zn, 0.7–0.85 for Al), $t$ = film thickness (µm), $\rho_{\text{coat}}$ = coating resistivity (Ω·cm), 8760 = hours per year.

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Corrosion Rate from Faraday's Law (Exhaustive)

We will derive the **corrosion rate in mm/year** used universally in marine engineering inspection reports.

**Given:** Corrosion current density $i$ (µA/cm²), atomic mass $M$ (g/mol), valence $n$, density $\rho$ (g/cm³).

**Step 1 — Start with Faraday's Law of mass loss:**

$$m = \frac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$$

**Step 2 — Replace current with current density times area** ($I = i \cdot A$):

$$m = \frac{i \cdot A \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$$

**Step 3 — Replace mass with volume times density** ($m = V \cdot \rho$):

$$V \cdot \rho = \frac{i \cdot A \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$$

**Step 4 — Replace volume with area times thickness** ($V = A \cdot d$):

$$A \cdot d \cdot \rho = \frac{i \cdot A \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$$

**Step 5 — Cancel $A$ from both sides:**

$$d \cdot \rho = \frac{i \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$$

**Step 6 — Solve for thickness loss $d$:**

$$d = \frac{i \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F \cdot \rho}$$

**Step 7 — Substitute engineering units.** Let $i$ be in µA/cm², $t$ in years, $d$ in mm.

- $1 \text{ µA/cm}^2 = 10^{-6} \text{ A} / 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2 = 10^{-2} \text{ A/m}^2$
- $1 \text{ year} = 3.1536 \times 10^7 \text{ s}$
- $1 \text{ mm} = 10^{-3} \text{ m}$

$$d \, [\text{mm}] = \frac{\left( i \times 10^{-2} \right) \left( t \times 3.1536 \times 10^7 \right) M}{(n)(96485)(\rho \times 10^3)}$$

**Step 8 — Collect the constants:**

$$d = i \cdot t \cdot M \cdot \frac{(10^{-2})(3.1536 \times 10^7)}{(96485)(10^3) \cdot n \cdot \rho}$$

$$d = i \cdot t \cdot M \cdot \frac{315.36}{9.6485 \times 10^7 \cdot n \cdot \rho}$$

**Step 9 — Simplify the numerical constant:**

$$\frac{315.36}{9.6485 \times 10^7} = 3.268 \times 10^{-6} \approx 0.00327 \times 10^{-3}$$

When $i$ is in **µA/cm²** and $M$ in **g/mol**, $\rho$ in **g/cm³**:

$$\boxed{CR = 0.00327 \cdot \frac{i \cdot M}{n \cdot \rho} \text{ mm/year}}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Design of Sacrificial Anode System for a Ship Hull

**Given:** A 150 m long oil tanker with a wetted hull area of 3500 m². Bare steel hull needs CP. Design with aluminum anodes. Required current density = 120 mA/m². Design life = 4 years. Anode utilization factor $U_z$ = 0.80. Practical capacity of Al alloy anode = 2500 Ah/kg.

**Step 1 — Calculate total current requirement:**

$$I_{\text{req}} = j_{\text{req}} \times A_{\text{wetted}}$$

$$I_{\text{req}} = 0.120 \, \text{A/m}^2 \times 3500 \, \text{m}^2 = 420 \, \text{A}$$

**Step 2 — Calculate total anode mass required using the anode life equation with utilization factor:**

$$m_{\text{total}} = \frac{I_{\text{req}} \cdot T \cdot 8760}{Q_{\text{util}} \cdot U_z}$$

Substitute:

$$m_{\text{total}} = \frac{420 \times 4 \times 8760}{2500 \times 0.80}$$

Calculate numerator:

$$420 \times 4 = 1680 \text{ A·years}$$

$$1680 \times 8760 = 14,716,800 \text{ Ah}$$

Calculate denominator:

$$2500 \times 0.80 = 2000 \text{ Ah/kg}$$

Final mass:

$$m_{\text{total}} = \frac{14,716,800}{2000} = 7358.4 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 3 — Choose individual anode size.** Standard ship anodes are 5 kg, 10 kg, 25 kg blocks. Choose 25 kg Al anodes for hull:

$$N_{\text{anodes}} = \frac{7358.4}{25} = 294.3 \approx 295 \text{ anodes}$$

**Step 4 — Distribute anodes along hull.** Spacing for underwater hull on tanker ≈ 4–5 m. With 150 m length, 2 sides:

$$N_{\text{per side}} = \frac{295}{2} \approx 148 \text{ anodes/side}$$

**Step 5 — Spacing check:**

$$\text{Spacing} = \frac{150 \, \text{m}}{148} = 1.01 \, \text{m} \quad \text{(acceptable, but typically grouped at bilges, forward, aft)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Practical Design Note (KTU):** Anodes are not uniformly distributed; they are clustered at high-risk zones — **stern (propeller), bow (wave impact), bilges, rudder area, and near any dissimilar metal fittings**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Galvanic Corrosion Current Between Bronze Valve and Steel Pipe

**Given:** A 100 mm diameter bronze valve (E = −0.25 V) is connected directly to a mild steel pipe (E = −0.60 V) in seawater. Exposed area of steel = 500 cm²; bronze area = 200 cm². Resistivity of seawater path between them = 0.25 Ω.

**Step 1 — Calculate driving EMF:**

$$E_{\text{cell}} = E_{\text{cathode}} - E_{\text{anode}} = E_{\text{bronze}} - E_{\text{steel}}$$

$$E_{\text{cell}} = -0.25 - (-0.60) = 0.35 \text{ V}$$

**Step 2 — Apply Ohm's Law analog for the corrosion cell:**

$$I_{\text{galvanic}} = \frac{E_{\text{cell}}}{R_{\text{external}} + R_{\text{polarization}}}$$

Neglecting polarization resistance for first estimate, with $R$ = 0.25 Ω:

$$I_{\text{galvanic}} = \frac{0.35}{0.25} = 1.4 \text{ A}$$

**Step 3 — Calculate corrosion current density on steel (the anode):**

$$i_{\text{steel}} = \frac{I}{A_{\text{steel}}} = \frac{1.4 \text{ A}}{500 \text{ cm}^2} = 0.0028 \text{ A/cm}^2 = 2800 \text{ µA/cm}^2$$

**Step 4 — Calculate corrosion rate of steel using the engineering formula:**

$$CR = 0.00327 \times \frac{i \cdot M}{n \cdot \rho}$$

For steel (Fe): $M$ = 55.85 g/mol, $n$ = 2, $\rho$ = 7.85 g/cm³.

$$CR = 0.00327 \times \frac{2800 \times 55.85}{2 \times 7.85}$$

$$CR = 0.00327 \times \frac{156,380}{15.70}$$

$$CR = 0.00327 \times 9960.5$$

$$\boxed{CR = 32.57 \text{ mm/year}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Result Interpretation:** A galvanic corrosion rate of ~33 mm/year means the steel pipe would perforate within **weeks** at the coupling point. This is why class-approved **dielectric flanges / isolation gaskets** are mandatory wherever dissimilar metals meet in seawater systems.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Material Selection for Common Ship Components

| Ship Component | Material Used | Corrosion Concern | Protection Method |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Main Hull Plates** | Mild steel (Grade A, AH, DH, EH) | General, pitting, ballast tank top | Coating + ICCP/SACP + sacrificial anodes |
| **Superstructure** | Mild steel | Atmospheric, splash zone | Marine-grade epoxy + polyurethane paint |
| **Propellers** | Manganese bronze, Ni-Al bronze, SS | Cavitation, erosion, galvanic | Cathodic protection + smoothness |
| **Rudder** | Mild steel (cast) | Galvanic with bronze propeller | Shaft brush, dielectric coupling, anodes |
| **Stern Tube** | Cast iron / Bronze liner | Galvanic with shaft | Wooden/strip lagging + Zn anodes |
| **Sea Water Piping** | Cu-Ni (90/10, 70/30), GRP | Pitting, erosion | Velocity limits, lining |
| **Valves (Sea Water)** | Bronze, Monel, Ni-Cu | Galvanic with steel | Dielectric isolation, CP |
| **Fasteners (above WL)** | SS 316, Monel 400 | Crevice, galvanic | Use isolation sleeves/washers |
| **Fasteners (below WL)** | Monel, SS 316L | Crevice | Encapsulating sleeves |
| **Masts, Funnels** | Mild steel, Al alloy | Atmospheric | Marine paint system |
| **Decks, Tank Tops** | Mild steel + coating | Pitting, MIC | PSPC ballast tank coating |
| **Boats, Life Rafts** | GRP, FRP composites | UV, osmosis, blistering | Gelcoat, osmosis barrier |
| **Sonar Domes** | FRP, rubber-coated | Marine growth, pitting | Antifouling rubber skin |

---

## 3.5 Composite Materials in Ship Design — The Modern Frontier

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (KTU):** A **composite material** is a heterogeneous combination of two or more distinct phases — a **matrix** (polymer, metal, ceramic) and a **reinforcement** (fiber, particle) — engineered to achieve properties unattainable by either phase alone.

### 3.5.1 Marine Composites — Composition

| Phase | Function | Common Marine Examples |
|---|---|---|
| **Matrix** (Resin) | Transfers load, protects fibers, gives shape | Polyester, Vinylester, Epoxy, Phenolic |
| **Reinforcement** (Fiber) | Carries primary load, provides strength | E-glass, S-glass, Aramid (Kevlar), Carbon |
| **Core** (Sandwich) | Provides stiffness, low weight | PVC foam, balsa, end-grain balsa, SAN |
| **Gelcoat** (Outer layer) | UV/weathering resistance, cosmetic | Pigmented polyester, vinylester |

### 3.5.2 Common Marine Composite Applications

| Vessel / Component | Composite Used | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Naval mine-hunter hulls | GRP (E-glass/polyester) | Non-magnetic signature |
| Patrol boats, fast craft | FRP sandwich | High speed, light weight |
| Submarine sonar domes | Rubber-coated GRP | Hydrodynamic + acoustic |
| Masts on warships | Carbon fiber composite | Reduced radar cross-section |
| Bulkheads, joiner panels | Fire-retardant composites | Low smoke, low toxicity |
| Propeller blades (small craft) | Carbon fiber/epoxy | High specific strength |
| Pipework (sea/fresh water) | GRP, GRE | Corrosion immunity |
| LNG cargo containment | Aluminum + insulation + Invar | Cryogenic compatibility |

### 3.5.3 Advantages & Limitations for Marine Use

> [!TIP]
> **Advantages:** Corrosion immunity → zero coating cost; non-magnetic (stealth); high strength-to-weight ratio; reduced maintenance; design flexibility.
> **Limitations:** Susceptible to **osmosis** (water ingress through gelcoat), **UV degradation**, **delamination** if poorly cured, **fire/smoke/toxicity (FST)** concerns in passenger vessels, and **moisture absorption** which lowers $T_g$ (glass transition temperature).

### 3.5.4 Non-Metallic Materials Beyond Composites

| Material | Marine Application | Key Property |
|---|---|---|
| **Natural Rubber** | Fender systems, bearings | Elasticity, water resistance |
| **Neoprene / Polychloroprene** | Cable jackets, diaphragms | Chemical + UV resistance |
| **PTFE (Teflon)** | Bearing liners, valve seats | Lowest friction, inert |
| **PVC / HDPE** | Ballast piping, deck drainage | Corrosion-proof, cheap |
| **Polyurethane** | Anti-fouling coatings, fender facing | Toughness, abrasion resistance |
| **Ceramics (Al₂O₃, ZrO₂)** | High-wear bearings, pump seals | Hardness, temperature resistance |
| **Wood (Teak, Pitch Pine)** | Traditional decking | Self-renewing, non-slip |

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Schematic: The Fundamental Corrosion Cell in a Ship Hull

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Steel Hull Plate ANODE] -->|Electrons flow| B[Bronze Propeller CATHODE]
    B -->|Ionic current in seawater| C[Electrolyte Seawater NaCl 3.5%]
    C -->|Oxygen diffuses| D[Cathodic Surface]
    A -->|Fe2+ ions dissolve| C
    D -->|OH- produced| E[Corrosion Product Fe OH 2 to FeOOH Rust]
    E -->|Deposits on| A
    
    style A fill:#ff6b6b,color:#fff
    style B fill:#4ecdc4,color:#000
    style C fill:#1e90ff,color:#fff
    style D fill:#95e1d3,color:#000
    style E fill:#a0522d,color:#fff
```

## 4.2 Schematic: Cathodic Protection — Sacrificial vs Impressed Current

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph SACP[Sacrificial Anode CP System]
        Z[Zinc or Aluminum Anode] -->|Connects via cable| SH1[Steel Hull]
        SH1 -.->|Protected surface| Z
        Z2[Zinc dissolves preferentially] --> Z
    end
    
    subgraph ICCP[Impressed Current CP System]
        PSU[DC Power Supply Rectifier] -->|Positive lead| TA[Platinum Coated Titanium Anode]
        TA -->|Current forced into seawater| SW[Seawater]
        SW -->|Current returns to hull| SH2[Steel Hull]
        SH2 -->|Negative lead back to| PSU
        REF[Silver Chloride Reference Electrode] -.->|Monitors potential| PSU
    end
    
    style SACP fill:#fff5e1
    style ICCP fill:#e1f5ff
```
