## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Style]**
*Define Destructive Testing (DT). List any four common destructive testing methods with their governing ASTM standards.*

**Model Answer:**

**Destructive Testing (DT)** is a category of material evaluation in which the test specimen is subjected to controlled loading or environmental conditions until it **fails or is permanently deformed**, thereby rendering the specimen unusable.

**Four common DT methods with ASTM standards:**

| # | Method | Standard | Property Measured |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Tensile Test | ASTM E8/E8M | Yield strength, UTS, ductility |
| 2 | Brinell Hardness | ASTM E10 | Hardness number |
| 3 | Charpy Impact | ASTM E23 | Impact toughness (Joules) |
| 4 | Fatigue Test | ASTM E466 | Endurance limit, S-N curve |

**Key insight:** DT is the **only** way to obtain true quantitative design data (yield, UTS, elongation) required by classification societies for ship steel approval.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Style]**
*Distinguish between Radiographic Testing (RT) and Ultrasonic Testing (UT) in terms of principle, defect type detected, and safety considerations.*

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Radiographic Testing (RT) | Ultrasonic Testing (UT) |
|---|---|---|
| **Principle** | Penetration of X-rays / Gamma rays through material; defects absorb differentially and create a film image | High-frequency sound waves (1–25 MHz) reflect from defect interfaces |
| **Defect Type** | Volumetric defects: porosity, slag, voids, lack of fusion | Planar and volumetric defects: cracks, laminations, delaminations |
| **Record Type** | Permanent radiographic film (analog) or DDA (digital) | A-scan, B-scan, C-scan (real-time or recorded) |
| **Safety** | ⚠️ **Radiation hazard**; requires controlled area, dosimeters, lead shielding | ✅ Generally safe; couplant (gel) needed for sound coupling |
| **Sensitivity** | Better for volumetric defects (~1–2% of thickness) | Excellent for planar defects (cracks parallel to beam) |
| **Material Suitability** | All metals and even non-metals (using proper isotope) | Conductors, with limitations on rough/coarse-grained materials |
| **Cost** | High (film, isotopes, X-ray equipment) | Moderate (transducer + flaw detector) |

### 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — Destructive Testing Focus

**Q3(A). (a) [7 Marks]** Describe the **Tensile Test** in detail. Draw a typical stress-strain curve for mild steel and clearly mark the following points: Proportional Limit, Elastic Limit, Yield Point (Upper and Lower), Ultimate Tensile Strength, and Fracture Point. Explain the significance of each.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A mild steel specimen of gauge diameter $12 \text{ mm}$ and gauge length $50 \text{ mm}$ is subjected to a tensile test. The maximum load recorded is $52 \text{ kN}$ and the fracture load is $38 \text{ kN}$. After fracture, the gauge length is $65 \text{ mm}$ and the diameter at the neck is $8.5 \text{ mm}$. Calculate: (i) Ultimate Tensile Strength, (ii) Percentage Elongation, (iii) Percentage Reduction in Area.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Description of Tensile Test [7 Marks]:**

The **Tensile Test** is performed on a **Universal Testing Machine (UTM)** in accordance with **ASTM E8/E8M**. A standardized specimen (round or flat) with precisely machined gauge length (typically $L_0 = 5.65\sqrt{A_0}$ for proportional specimens) is gripped between the crossheads of the UTM. A monotonically increasing axial tensile load is applied at a controlled rate (within the elastic strain rate range of $0.015\%/\text{s}$ as per ISO 6892-1) until fracture.

**Stress-Strain Curve for Mild Steel (with marked points):**

```
Stress
  ↑
  │           ┌──── Ultimate Tensile Strength (Point C)
  │          ╱│
  │         ╱ │  Fracture
  │        ╱  │╲  Point E
  │       ╱   │ ╲___
  │      ╱    │     ╲
  │     ╱     │      ╲___
  │    ╱      │          ╲
  │   ╱       │           
  │  ╱ Upper Yield Point A1
  │ ╱         │
  │╱ Proportional╲─────────  Lower Yield Point A2
  │  Limit / Elastic Limit
  │  
  └──────────────────────────────→ Strain
  0    ε
```

| Point | Name | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **A** (lower) | Proportional Limit | Maximum stress at which stress is still proportional to strain (Hooke's Law valid) |
| **A** (upper) | Elastic Limit | Maximum stress at which the material returns to its original shape upon unloading |
| **A1** | Upper Yield Point | Stress at which plastic deformation first begins (sudden drop) |
| **A2** | Lower Yield Point | Stress at which plastic deformation continues at nearly constant stress |
| **B–C** | Strain Hardening | Plastic region; material strengthens due to dislocation entanglement |
| **C** | Ultimate Tensile Strength (UTS) | Maximum stress the material can withstand; beyond this, necking begins |
| **D–E** | Necking & Fracture | Local cross-sectional area reduces rapidly until failure |

**(b) Numerical Solution [7 Marks]:**

**Given Data:**
- Original gauge diameter $d_0 = 12 \text{ mm}$
- Original gauge length $L_0 = 50 \text{ mm}$
- Maximum load $F_{max} = 52 \text{ kN} = 52{,}000 \text{ N}$
- Final gauge length $L_f = 65 \text{ mm}$
- Final diameter at neck $d_f = 8.5 \text{ mm}$

**Step 1 — Calculate Original Cross-Sectional Area $A_0$:**

$$
A_0 = \frac{\pi d_0^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \cdot (12)^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \cdot 144}{4} = 36\pi = 113.097 \text{ mm}^2
$$

> *Logic:* The original cross-section is circular. $A_0 = \pi d^2 / 4$ is the standard circle area formula. $36\pi$ is left symbolic for clarity, then evaluated.

**Step 2 — Ultimate Tensile Strength (UTS):**

$$
\sigma_u = \frac{F_{max}}{A_0} = \frac{52{,}000 \text{ N}}{113.097 \text{ mm}^2} = 459.78 \text{ N/mm}^2
$$

$$
\boxed{\sigma_u \approx 459.78 \text{ MPa}}
$$

> *Logic:* UTS is the maximum stress computed at the maximum load, using the original area. The unit N/mm² is equivalent to MPa ($1 \text{ N/mm}^2 = 1 \text{ MPa}$). For mild steel, this is a reasonable value (typical range: 400–550 MPa).

**Step 3 — Percentage Elongation:**

$$
\%EL = \frac{L_f - L_0}{L_0} \times 100 = \frac{65 - 50}{50} \times 100 = \frac{15}{50} \times 100
$$

$$
\boxed{\%EL = 30\%}
$$

> *Logic:* Ductility is measured by how much the specimen stretches before fracture. A value of 30% indicates a **ductile material** (typical for mild steel). High ductility is desirable for ship steel to absorb impact energy without brittle fracture.

**Step 4 — Final Cross-Sectional Area at Neck $A_f$:**

$$
A_f = \frac{\pi d_f^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \cdot (8.5)^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \cdot 72.25}{4} = 18.0625\pi = 56.745 \text{ mm}^2
$$

> *Logic:* The fractured surface is also circular. We use the measured diameter at the neck.

**Step 5 — Percentage Reduction in Area:**

$$
\%RA = \frac{A_0 - A_f}{A_0} \times 100 = \frac{113.097 - 56.745}{113.097} \times 100
$$

$$
\%RA = \frac{56.352}{113.097} \times 100
$$

$$
\boxed{\%RA \approx 49.83\%}
$$

> *Logic:* Reduction in area is a more sensitive ductility indicator than %EL because it directly measures the localized necking. Values of 40–60% are typical for ductile shipbuilding steel.

**Summary of Results:**
| Quantity | Value |
|---|---|
| Ultimate Tensile Strength | **459.78 MPa** |
| Percentage Elongation | **30%** |
| Percentage Reduction in Area | **49.83%** |

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Non-Destructive Testing Focus (Internal Choice)

**Q3(B). (a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **principle, procedure, advantages, and limitations of Ultrasonic Testing (UT)**. How is it applied for the inspection of **ship hull welds**?

**(b) [7 Marks]** Compare **Magnetic Particle Testing (MPT)** and **Dye Penetrant Testing (DPT)** with respect to: (i) physical principle, (ii) material applicability, (iii) types of defects detected, (iv) sensitivity, (v) pre-cleaning requirements, and (vi) shipyard application areas.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Ultrasonic Testing — Principle, Procedure, Advantages, Limitations [7 Marks]:**

**Principle:**
Ultrasonic Testing is based on the propagation of **high-frequency mechanical sound waves** (typically 1 to 25 MHz, well above the audible range of ~20 kHz) through the test material. These waves are generated and detected by a **piezoelectric transducer** (usually lead zirconate titanate, PZT). When the wave encounters an interface (such as a defect, the back wall, or a different medium), a portion of the energy is **reflected** back to the transducer, and the rest continues to propagate. The time-of-flight and amplitude of the reflected signal are analyzed on a **flaw detector** (A-scan) to determine the **location**, **size**, and **nature** of the defect.

**Procedure for Ship Hull Weld Inspection:**
1. **Surface Preparation:** The weld and adjacent parent metal (typically 50–100 mm on each side) are cleaned of rust, paint, oil, and scale. A smooth finish (often by grinding) ensures good couplant contact.
2. **Couplant Application:** A couplant such as **water, gel, oil, or glycerin** is applied to eliminate the air gap between the transducer and the surface. Without couplant, 99.9% of the ultrasonic energy is reflected due to acoustic impedance mismatch.
3. **Calibration:** A calibration block (e.g., **IIW V1 or V2 block** as per AWS/EN ISO 2400) is used to set the reference sensitivity, time-base, and angle.
4. **Scanning:** The transducer is moved systematically along the weld. Two common probe types are used:
   - **Normal probe (0°):** Detects defects parallel to the surface (e.g., delaminations, laminations in plates).
   - **Angle probe (45°, 60°, 70°):** Uses **Snell's law of refraction** to send shear waves into the weld, detecting cracks, lack of fusion, and lack of penetration.
5. **Interpretation:** The A-scan display shows the initial pulse, intermediate defect echoes, and the back-wall echo. Skilled operators classify defects by echo height, position, and dynamic behavior.

**Advantages:**
- High penetration depth (several meters in steel).
- Excellent sensitivity to planar defects (cracks, lack of fusion).
- Instantaneous results; real-time display.
- No radiation hazard (safe for operators).
- Portable equipment (can be carried to remote shipyard areas).

**Limitations:**
- Requires skilled, certified operators (CSWIP/ASNT Level II minimum).
- Difficult on rough, corroded, or coarse-grained surfaces.
- Reference standards and calibration blocks required.
- Couplant needed (cannot inspect hot surfaces easily).
- Complex geometries (e.g., nozzle welds) require special techniques like **TOFD** (Time-of-Flight Diffraction) or **Phased Array UT**.

**(b) Comparison of MPT and DPT [7 Marks]:**

| # | Parameter | Magnetic Particle Testing (MPT) | Dye Penetrant Testing (DPT) |
|---|---|---|---|
| (i) | **Physical Principle** | A magnetic flux is induced in the ferromagnetic specimen. Surface or near-surface defects cause **flux leakage**, which attracts fine iron particles (dry powder or wet suspension) forming a visible indication. | A low-surface-tension liquid penetrant is drawn into surface-breaking defects by **capillary action**. Excess is removed, and a developer absorbs the trapped penetrant, revealing the defect as a colored or fluorescent indication. |
| (ii) | **Material Applicability** | Only **ferromagnetic** materials: mild steel, low-alloy steel, high-strength ship steel, etc. (Cannot be used on aluminum, austenitic stainless steel, copper, brass) | All **non-porous** materials: steels, aluminum alloys, austenitic SS, titanium, ceramics, plastics, glass. |
| (iii) | **Defects Detected** | Surface and **near-surface** defects (typically up to 6 mm deep). Excellent for cracks, seams, laps, weld undercuts. | **Surface-breaking** defects only. Excellent for cracks, porosity, laps, cold shuts, grinding cracks. |
| (iv) | **Sensitivity** | Very high for surface/near-surface in ferromagnetic materials. Can detect defects as small as 1 mm length, 0.1 mm depth. | High for fine, tight surface cracks. Fluorescent DPT can detect cracks as narrow as 1 micron. |
| (v) | **Pre-Cleaning Required** | Critical: surface must be free of oil, paint, dirt that would mask flux leakage. Sometimes requires **demagnetization** after testing. | Critical: surface must be free of oil, contaminants, and (often) pre-etched to open tight cracks. Post-cleaning to remove residual penetrant. |
| (vi) | **Shipyard Applications** | Inspection of **propeller shaft journals, rudder stocks, crankshafts, weld toes, chain links, crane hooks, motor housings**. Common during in-service inspection. | Inspection of **aluminum superstructures, austenitic stainless steel pipe welds, bronze propellers, turbine blades, non-magnetic castings**, and small critical components. |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Read This Carefully!**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert units:** $52 \text{ kN} = 52{,}000 \text{ N}$ (not 52). Common mistake leads to a 10× error in UTS.
> 2. **Confusing %EL with %RA:** %EL uses length change; %RA uses area change. They are NOT the same. Both must be reported for full ductility characterization.
> 3. **In NDT questions, students forget to mention couplant.** Without couplant, UT will not work on land. Always mention couplant as part of UT procedure.
> 4. **Confusing MPT with DPT applicability:** MPT works ONLY on ferromagnetic materials. Don't say "MPT is used for aluminum superstructures" — that's a guaranteed 1–2 mark deduction.
> 5. **Skipping the standard:** KTU examiners love ASTM/ISO numbers. Always cite **ASTM E8** for tensile, **ASTM E23** for Charpy, **ASTM E10** for Brinell, **ASTM E709** for MPT, **ASTM E165** for DPT, **ASTM E114** for UT.
> 6. **Not drawing the stress-strain curve properly:** A hand-drawn but clearly labeled curve earns full marks. A text-only answer without a sketch may lose 1–2 marks.
> 7. **Mixing up "True Stress" with "Engineering Stress":** Use engineering stress for tensile test questions unless explicitly asked for true stress.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Exam:**

- **DT** destroys the specimen; **NDT** preserves it. Remember this as the foundational distinction.
- **ASTM E8/E8M** = Tensile test; **ASTM E23** = Charpy impact; **ASTM E10** = Brinell; **ASTM E18** = Rockwell; **ASTM E384** = Vickers/Knoop; **ASTM E466** = Fatigue.
- **UTS** = $F_{max} / A_0$; **%EL** = $100(L_f - L_0)/L_0$; **%RA** = $100(A_0 - A_f)/A_0$.
- **Brinell** uses a ball (10 mm) and load; **Vickers** uses a diamond pyramid; **Rockwell** gives direct dial reading.
- **Charpy test** reports energy in Joules; for ship steel, **27 J at 0 °C** is a typical minimum (IACS requirement).
- **UT** uses sound waves and needs **couplant**; **RT** uses X-rays/Gamma rays and needs **radiation safety**; **MPT** needs **ferromagnetic** material and **demagnetization**; **DPT** needs thorough **pre-cleaning** and works on **all non-porous** materials.
- **Snell's Law** governs angle-beam UT: $\sin\theta_1 / v_1 = \sin\theta_2 / v_2$. Critical angle gives mode conversion to shear wave.
- Ship hull welds are typically inspected by **RT or UT** (volumetric), with **DPT or MPT** (surface) for toes and root passes.
- **Eddy current** is restricted to **conductive** materials (not plastics); **Acoustic emission** is used for real-time monitoring of pressure vessels, tanks, and offshore platforms.
- **Creep** occurs above $0.4 T_m$; relevant for ship **boilers, superheaters, turbine components**.
- **Fatigue** failures initiate at stress concentrators (weld toes, rivet holes); S-N curve gives the **endurance limit** below which the material has infinite life.

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 2 - Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1. Cold Working of Metals and Alloys

### Formal Definition
**Cold Working (Cold Forming / Cold Deformation)** is the plastic deformation of a metal carried out at a temperature **below its recrystallization temperature** (typically below ~0.4 × T<sub>melt</sub> in Kelvin). Since no significant recrystallization occurs during the process, the metal undergoes *strain hardening*, a permanent change in shape accompanied by an increase in dislocation density, strength, and hardness.

### Conceptual Analogy
Think of cold working like kneading cold clay or repeatedly bending a paperclip. The paperclip does not "heal" itself — each bend makes the wire harder to bend further at the same spot because the internal structure has been disturbed and packed tighter. The metal "remembers" the deformation by becoming stronger but more brittle.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Cold working increases **yield strength, ultimate tensile strength, and hardness** but **decreases ductility and toughness**. The recrystallization temperature for steel is roughly **0.4 × T<sub>melting</sub> (in Kelvin)**, e.g., for pure iron this corresponds to ~450 °C.

### Common Cold Working Processes
- Rolling (cold rolling of plates, sheets)
- Drawing (wire, tubes)
- Extrusion (cold extrusion of rods)
- Forging (cold forging / cold heading)
- Stamping, bending, deep drawing, spinning, coining

---

## 2. Hot Working of Metals and Alloys

### Formal Definition
**Hot Working** is the plastic deformation of a metal performed at a temperature **above its recrystallization temperature**, so that the deformed grains continuously recrystallize and remain soft and ductile during the process. The metal is shaped without significant strain hardening because new strain-free grains replace the deformed ones as fast as they form.

### Conceptual Analogy
Imagine molding warm dough. As you press it, the dough changes shape easily, and even if it tears or roughens on the surface, fresh dough inside flows out. The "soft" structure of warm metal behaves similarly — it reforms its internal grain pattern continuously, allowing large shape changes without cracking.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Hot working **removes** the effects of prior cold work, refines the cast (coarse) grain structure, closes internal porosity/gas pockets, and improves ductility and toughness. The deformation force required is **much lower** than cold working because the metal is softer.

### Common Hot Working Processes
- Hot rolling (plates, sections, rails)
- Hot forging (shafts, crankshafts, propellers)
- Hot extrusion (seamless tubes, structural sections)
- Hot drawing, hot spinning, hot pressing

---

## 3. Failure of Components and Failure Analysis

### Formal Definition
**Failure of a component** is the loss of its functional capability to perform the intended duty under prescribed operating conditions. **Failure Analysis** is the systematic engineering investigation to determine the root cause(s) of failure, including the material, design, manufacturing, service, and environmental factors contributing to it.

### Conceptual Analogy
A failure is like a "symptom" in a patient, and failure analysis is the "diagnosis." A doctor doesn't just treat a broken bone — they investigate *why* it broke (accident, weak bones, disease). Similarly, an engineer doesn't just replace a broken ship shaft — they investigate *why* it broke (fatigue, corrosion, overload, material defect).

> [!NOTE]
> **Three Broad Categories of Failure:**
> 1. **Overload failure** — load exceeds material strength.
> 2. **Fatigue failure** — progressive damage under cyclic loads.
> 3. **Environmental / time-dependent failure** — corrosion, creep, stress-corrosion cracking, hydrogen embrittlement.

### Types of Failure Modes
| Mode | Description |
|---|---|
| Ductile fracture | Significant plastic deformation before fracture |
| Brittle fracture | Little or no plastic deformation, sudden |
| Fatigue | Failure under cyclic/alternating stress |
| Creep | Time-dependent strain at elevated temperature |
| Stress corrosion | Cracking under combined stress + corrosive medium |
| Wear | Material loss due to contact motion |
| Buckling | Geometric instability under compression |

---

## 4. Stress-Strain Curve

### Formal Definition
The **Stress-Strain Curve** is the graphical representation of a material's mechanical response — showing the relationship between applied stress ($\sigma$) and resulting strain ($\epsilon$) under uniaxial loading, typically obtained from a standard tensile test as per **ASTM E8 / E8M** or **IS 1608**.

### Conceptual Analogy
The stress-strain curve is like the "fingerprint" of a mechanical material. Two materials subjected to the same pulling force can stretch very differently, just as two people of the same height may have very different weights. The curve tells the engineer *exactly* how a material behaves from the first instant of loading until it breaks.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Points on the Engineering Stress-Strain Curve (for ductile mild steel):**
> - **Proportional limit** — end of linear (Hookean) region.
> - **Elastic limit** — maximum stress the material can sustain without permanent deformation.
> - **Yield point** — stress at which appreciable plastic deformation begins.
> - **Ultimate Tensile Strength (UTS)** — maximum stress the material can withstand.
> - **Fracture / Breaking point** — point of actual rupture.

---

## 5. Elements of Fracture Mechanics

### Formal Definition
**Fracture Mechanics** is the field of mechanics that studies the propagation of cracks in materials. It quantifies the stress and energy conditions at a crack tip, allowing prediction of when a crack will grow and cause catastrophic failure. Classical (linear-elastic) fracture mechanics is valid when plastic deformation at the crack tip is small compared to the crack length.

### Conceptual Analogy
Think of a sheet of paper with a small tear at the edge. You can pull the paper apart quite easily because all the tension concentrates at the tip of that tear. Fracture mechanics asks: *how much force, with a tear of what size, will tear the paper in two?* In a ship's hull or pressure vessel, the same question becomes critical.

> [!NOTE]
> **Three Core Fracture Mechanics Parameters:**
> 1. **Stress Intensity Factor (K)** — characterizes the crack-tip stress field.
> 2. **Strain Energy Release Rate (G)** — energy available per unit increase in crack area.
> 3. **J-Integral (J)** — characterizes the crack-tip stress-strain field under elastic-plastic conditions.

---

## 6. Griffith's Crack Theory

### Formal Definition
**Griffith's Crack Theory** (1920, A.A. Griffith) is the founding theory of fracture mechanics. It states that a brittle crack will propagate when the **decrease in elastic strain energy** stored in the material is at least equal to (or greater than) the **energy required to create the new crack surface**. This gives a critical stress criterion for an existing crack of length 2a in an infinite plate.

### Conceptual Analogy
Imagine stretching a rubber band and then carefully nicking its edge with a scissor. The rubber band snaps much more easily than an un-notched one. Griffith's theory mathematically explains *why* — the stored energy that wanted to be released finds an "easy exit" through the notch, and the energy cost of forming new surfaces is small for brittle materials.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Griffith Stress (for an infinite plate with a central crack of length 2a):**
> 
> $$\sigma_c = \sqrt{\dfrac{2E\gamma_s}{\pi a}}$$
> 
> where E = Young's modulus, $\gamma_s$ = specific surface energy, a = half-crack length. This is the **KTU board-favorite formula** for Module 2.

---

## 7. Fatigue Crack Propagation

### Formal Definition
**Fatigue Crack Propagation** is the progressive (sub-critical) growth of a pre-existing crack under cyclic (fluctuating) loading, even when each individual load cycle is below the static yield strength of the material. It is the dominant mechanism of failure for ship hulls, propeller shafts, and offshore structures subjected to wave-induced cyclic stresses.

### Conceptual Analogy
Imagine repeatedly bending a paperclip back and forth at the same spot. Each bend causes a tiny, invisible crack to grow a little. After 30, 50, or 100 bends — well below the force that would straight-snap the clip — it suddenly breaks. That is fatigue crack propagation. The ship hull, wave after wave, slowly "bends" a microscopic defect into a fatal crack.

> [!NOTE]
> **Three Stages of Fatigue Life:**
> 1. **Stage I — Crack Initiation** (most of life, in smooth specimens)
> 2. **Stage II — Crack Propagation** (Paris regime, predictable)
> 3. **Stage III — Final fracture** (fast, unstable)

The **Paris Law (KTU-important)** describes Stage II:
$$\dfrac{da}{dN} = C (\Delta K)^m$$
where da/dN = crack growth per cycle, $\Delta K$ = stress intensity factor range, C and m are material constants.

---

## 8. Selection of Materials for Ship Building Applications

### Formal Definition
**Material Selection for Shipbuilding** is the engineering process of choosing the most suitable metallic alloys, composites, and coatings for hulls, superstructures, propulsion systems, and auxiliaries based on mechanical, corrosion, fabrication, weight, cost, and regulatory (Classification Society — DNV, Lloyd's Register, ABS, IRS) requirements.

### Conceptual Analogy
Choosing a material for a ship component is like choosing a tool for a job — you wouldn't use a butter knife to cut steel rods. The hull needs to be tough and weldable, the propeller needs to resist corrosion and cavitation, the shaft needs high fatigue strength — every part of the ship has its own "personality requirement."

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Typical Shipbuilding Material Choices:**
> - **Hull plates / structural sections:** Mild steel (ASTM A131 Grade A/B), high-tensile steel (AH36, DH36, EH36).
> - **Propeller blades:** Manganese–bronze, Ni–Al bronze, stainless steel (duplex).
> - **Propeller shaft:** Forged steel (C-Mn, Ni-Cr-Mo alloys), Monel K-500.
> - **Rudder / stern frame:** Cast steel, Ni-Al bronze.
> - **Boilers, superheaters:** Alloy steels (Cr-Mo).
> - **Piping (sea-water):** Cu-Ni (90/10, 70/30), titanium, GRP.
> - **Superstructure / non-critical:** Mild steel with protective coatings.
> - **Specialized (naval/submarine):** HY-80, HY-100, HSLA steels.

---

# Module 2 - Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 1. Cold Working — Deeper Mechanics

### Operating Principles
- Dislocation motion (slip) is restricted because **dynamic recovery and recrystallization are suppressed** below the recrystallization temperature.
- Dislocation density rises from $\sim 10^6$ lines/cm² to $10^{10}$–$10^{12}$ lines/cm².
- Dislocations tangle, multiply, and obstruct each other → **strain hardening (work hardening)**.
- Macroscopic effects: **Strength ↑, Hardness ↑, Ductility ↓, Toughness ↓, Residual stresses ↑.**

### Quantitative Relationships
- **True stress:** $\sigma_t = \sigma (1 + \epsilon)$
- **True strain:** $\epsilon_t = \ln(1 + \epsilon)$
- **Hollomon's power law (plastic region):** $\sigma_t = K \epsilon_t^n$
- **% Cold Work:** $\%CW = \dfrac{A_0 - A_f}{A_0} \times 100$

where $A_0$ = original cross-section area, $A_f$ = final area, n = strain-hardening exponent (0–0.5), K = strength coefficient.

### Why and How
- **Why strength rises:** more dislocations = more barriers to slip.
- **Why ductility drops:** fewer mobile dislocations = less ability to deform plastically.
- **Why residual stresses appear:** uneven plastic flow between surface and core.

### Real-world Engineering Utility
- Cold-rolled steel sheets for ship superstructure and bulkheads — higher strength allows thinner plates.
- Cold-drawn wires for cables, fasteners, and mooring lines.
- Deep-drawing of stainless steel for galley and tank components.

---

## 2. Hot Working — Deeper Mechanics

### Operating Principles
- Temperature is **above the recrystallization temperature** → new strain-free grains nucleate and grow as fast as deformation occurs.
- No net accumulation of dislocations → **no strain hardening**.
- Coarse cast dendritic structure is broken → **refined equiaxed grains** form on cooling.
- Internal porosity (shrinkage cavities) is welded shut.

### Why and How
- **Why lower force is needed:** yield strength drops sharply with temperature.
- **Why grain refinement occurs:** dynamic recrystallization produces fine, uniform grains.
- **Why defects close:** compressive hydrostatic stresses in the deformation zone collapse internal voids.

### Real-world Engineering Utility
- Hot-rolled hull plates — high ductility for forming and welding.
- Hot-forged propeller shafts and crankshafts — directional grain flow gives superior fatigue strength.
- Hot-extruded structural sections (T-bars, channels, angles) for ship framing.

---

## 3. Failure Analysis — Engineering Logic

### Logic Steps in a Systematic Failure Investigation
1. **Collect background data:** service history, drawings, specifications, environment.
2. **Visual and macroscopic examination:** identify fracture surface, deformation, discoloration, wear patterns.
3. **Nondestructive testing (NDT):** dye-penetrant, magnetic particle, ultrasonic, radiography.
4. **Mechanical testing:** hardness, tensile, impact (Charpy), fracture toughness.
5. **Metallographic examination:** grain size, microstructural anomalies, inclusions, decarburization, weld defects.
6. **Chemical analysis:** bulk composition, surface deposits, corrosive products.
7. **Fractographic analysis (SEM):** dimples (ductile), cleavage facets (brittle), striations (fatigue), beach marks (fatigue).
8. **Reconstruction and root cause identification.**
9. **Recommendations:** design change, material change, process change, maintenance schedule.

### Why and How
- Each step progressively narrows the root cause: **what** failed → **how** it failed → **why** it failed.
- A single missed step can misattribute the cause and allow the failure to repeat.

---

## 4. Stress-Strain Curve — Detailed Engineering Analysis

### Characteristic Regions (Ductile Mild Steel)
1. **Linear elastic region:** $\sigma = E\epsilon$ (Hooke's Law). Slope = Young's modulus E.
2. **Proportional limit:** end of linearity.
3. **Elastic limit:** max stress with no permanent deformation.
4. **Yield point (upper/lower for mild steel):** $f_y$ — design stress for steel structures.
5. **Strain-hardening region:** stress rises with strain to UTS.
6. **Ultimate Tensile Strength (UTS):** $f_u$ — max load / original area.
7. **Necking region:** cross-section reduces locally; engineering stress drops, true stress keeps rising.
8. **Fracture point:** actual separation.

### Key Engineering Quantities
- **Modulus of resilience:** $U_r = \sigma_y^2 / (2E)$ — energy absorbed per unit volume up to yield.
- **Modulus of toughness:** area under entire σ–ε curve up to fracture.
- **Ductility:** % elongation = $\dfrac{L_f - L_0}{L_0} \times 100$.
- **Reduction in area:** $\dfrac{A_0 - A_f}{A_0} \times 100$.

### Real-world Utility
- Yield strength sets the **maximum allowable working stress** (with a factor of safety).
- Toughness guides **fracture-critical design** in Arctic / North-Sea service ships.
- Ductility ensures the material can absorb overload, redistribute stress, and warn before fracture.

---

## 5. Fracture Mechanics — Engineering Framework

### Three Loading Modes
- **Mode I — Opening mode** (tensile, crack separates normal to its plane) — most critical and most studied.
- **Mode II — Sliding / Shear mode** (in-plane shear).
- **Mode III — Tearing mode** (out-of-plane shear).

### Stress Intensity Factor (Mode I)
$$K_I = Y \sigma \sqrt{\pi a}$$
where $\sigma$ = applied stress, a = crack length (or half-length for central crack), Y = dimensionless geometry factor (Y = 1 for through-thickness central crack in infinite plate).

### Fracture Criterion
$$K_I \geq K_{IC}$$
where $K_{IC}$ = **plane-strain fracture toughness** — a material property. The crack propagates catastrophically when K<sub>I</sub> reaches K<sub>IC</sub>.

### Strain Energy Release Rate (G)
$$G = \dfrac{K_I^2}{E'}$$
where $E' = E$ for plane stress, and $E' = E / (1 - \nu^2)$ for plane strain, $\nu$ = Poisson's ratio.

### J-Integral
A path-independent integral that characterizes the crack-tip stress-strain field in **elastic-plastic** fracture mechanics.

### Real-world Utility
- Used in fitness-for-service (FFS) assessments of aged ships, FPSOs, and offshore platforms.
- Used to set inspection intervals based on crack growth calculations.
- Mandated by classification societies for fracture-critical members (e.g., side-shell, hatch corners).

---

## 6. Griffith's Crack Theory — Engineering Logic

### Logical Steps in Griffith's Argument
1. Consider an infinite elastic plate under uniform tension $\sigma$ containing a through-thickness central crack of length 2a.
2. Introduce the crack → elastic strain energy in the plate **decreases** by an amount $U_E$ proportional to $\pi a^2 \sigma^2 / E$.
3. Introducing the crack **creates** two new free surfaces of total area 2(2aB) = 4aB (B = thickness) → **surface energy increases** by $4a B \gamma_s$.
4. The crack will grow spontaneously if the released strain energy per unit crack extension is greater than (or equal to) the surface energy required.
5. Setting up the energy balance and minimizing gives the critical stress.

### Orowan's Modification (for ductile materials)
For metals, plastic work at the crack tip (γ<sub>p</sub>) >> surface energy (γ<sub>s</sub>). Orowan replaced γ<sub>s</sub> with an effective surface energy:
$$\sigma_c = \sqrt{\dfrac{2E(\gamma_s + \gamma_p)}{\pi a}} \approx \sqrt{\dfrac{2E\gamma_p}{\pi a}}$$

### Real-world Utility
- Explains why brittle materials fail at stresses far below their theoretical strength.
- Foundation of all subsequent fracture-mechanics developments (Irwin's K, Rice's J).
- Direct design implication: keep **crack size a** small, use materials with high **fracture toughness K<sub>IC</sub>**.

---

## 7. Fatigue Crack Propagation — Paris Law Deep Analysis

### Stress Intensity Factor Range
$$\Delta K = K_{max} - K_{min} = Y \Delta \sigma \sqrt{\pi a}$$
where $\Delta \sigma = \sigma_{max} - \sigma_{min}$ is the cyclic stress range.

### Paris Law (Stage II Crack Growth)
$$\dfrac{da}{dN} = C (\Delta K)^m$$
- Typical values: $m = 2 - 4$ for steels, $C$ is determined experimentally.
- Stage I (near threshold): $\Delta K_{th}$ — below this, cracks don't grow.
- Stage III (unstable, fast fracture): $K_{max} \to K_{IC}$.

### Fatigue Life by Integration
Integrating Paris law between initial crack a<sub>i</sub> and critical crack a<sub>c</sub>:
$$N_f = \int_{a_i}^{a_c} \dfrac{da}{C (\Delta K)^m} = \int_{a_i}^{a_c} \dfrac{da}{C (Y \Delta \sigma \sqrt{\pi a})^m}$$

For Y and $\Delta \sigma$ constant, m ≠ 2:
$$N_f = \dfrac{a_i^{1 - m/2} - a_c^{1 - m/2}}{C (Y \Delta \sigma \sqrt{\pi})^m \cdot (m/2 - 1)}$$

### S-N Curve (Wöhler Curve) — Alternative Approach
For high-cycle fatigue life estimation in uncracked components:
$$\sigma_a = \sigma'_f (2N_f)^b$$
where $\sigma_a$ = stress amplitude, $\sigma'_f$ = fatigue strength coefficient, b = fatigue strength exponent, N<sub>f</sub> = cycles to failure.

### Miner's Rule (Cumulative Damage)
$$D = \sum_{i} \dfrac{n_i}{N_i} = 1 \text{ (at failure)}$$
where $n_i$ = number of cycles at stress level i, $N_i$ = cycles to failure at that level.

### Real-world Utility
- Predicts inspection intervals for ship hulls (rule-based, e.g., IACS S21).
- Determines retirement criteria for propeller shafts, crank-shafts, crane pedestals.
- Used in design of FPSO turret bearings and mooring chain links.

---

## 8. Material Selection for Shipbuilding — Decision Logic

### Selection Criteria
| Criterion | Why it matters |
|---|---|
| Yield / tensile strength | Carries hull girder loads, hydrostatic pressure |
| Toughness (Charpy V at service T) | Resists brittle fracture in cold waters |
| Weldability (Ceq ≤ 0.45 typically) | Shipbuilding is overwhelmingly welded construction |
| Corrosion resistance | Service in seawater, marine atmosphere |
| Fatigue strength | Wave-induced cyclic loading |
| Density | Lightweighting, stability, payload |
| Cost & availability | Bulk production economics |
| Class society approval | Regulatory compliance (DNV, LR, ABS, IRS) |

### Carbon Equivalent (Ceq) — Welding-friendly Steel
$$C_{eq} = C + \dfrac{Mn}{6} + \dfrac{Cr + Mo + V}{5} + \dfrac{Ni + Cu}{15}$$
- For ship plate steels, Ceq ≤ 0.45 % (typical acceptance for normal welding).

### Why and How (Hull Plate Selection Logic)
- For **general hull plates**: mild steel (ASTM A131 Gr A) — cheap, weldable, adequate strength.
- For **higher strength, weight-critical** vessels (warships, tankers, container ships): AH32/AH36, DH32/DH36, EH32/EH36.
- For **submarines and ice-strengthened hulls**: HY-80, HY-100, HY-130.

---

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\%CW = \frac{A_0 - A_f}{A_0} \times 100$ | Percent cold work | % |
| 2 | $\sigma_t = \sigma(1 + \epsilon)$ | True stress from engineering stress | MPa |
| 3 | $\epsilon_t = \ln(1 + \epsilon)$ | True strain from engineering strain | — |

> [!NOTE]
> Always remember: **MPa √m** is the unit of fracture toughness and stress intensity factor. KTU examiners **deduct marks** for unit mistakes on this topic.

# Module 2 - Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## Derivation 1: Griffith's Critical Stress (Central Crack in Infinite Plate)

### Given
- Infinite elastic plate under uniform uniaxial tension $\sigma$.
- Through-thickness central crack of total length 2a (so half-length = a).
- Plate thickness B, Young's modulus E, specific surface energy $\gamma_s$.

### Step 1: Set Up the Energy Balance
Total potential energy of the cracked plate = (Elastic strain energy stored) + (Surface energy of new crack faces).

Let U<sub>0</sub> = elastic strain energy of uncracked plate, U<sub>E</sub> = released strain energy when crack forms.

$$
\Delta U = U_{\text{surface}} - U_{\text{released}} = 4 a B \gamma_s - U_E
$$

The crack will grow spontaneously if $\Delta U$ decreases (i.e., the released strain energy exceeds the surface energy created).

### Step 2: Released Elastic Strain Energy (Irwin's Result)
For a central crack of half-length a in an infinite plate under uniform stress $\sigma$:

$$
U_E = \dfrac{\pi a^2 \sigma^2 B}{E}
$$

This is the elastic energy released by the formation of the crack. (For plane stress; for plane strain replace E with $E/(1-\nu^2)$.)

### Step 3: Surface Energy Required
A through-thickness crack of length 2a creates **two new surfaces**, each of area (2a) × B:

$$
U_{\text{surface}} = 2 \times (2a B) \times \gamma_s = 4 a B \gamma_s
$$

### Step 4: Total Energy Change
$$
\Delta U = 4 a B \gamma_s - \dfrac{\pi a^2 \sigma^2 B}{E}
$$

### Step 5: Equilibrium Condition (Critical State)
At the critical stress, the system is in unstable equilibrium — the crack is on the verge of growing. Mathematically, the energy change with respect to crack length is zero (minimum of $\Delta U$ vs. a curve):

$$
\dfrac{d(\Delta U)}{da} = 0
$$

$$
\dfrac{d}{da}\left[ 4 a B \gamma_s - \dfrac{\pi a^2 \sigma^2 B}{E} \right] = 0
$$

$$
4 B \gamma_s - \dfrac{2 \pi a \sigma^2 B}{E} = 0
$$

Divide both sides by 2B:

$$
2 \gamma_s - \dfrac{\pi a \sigma^2}{E} = 0
$$

### Step 6: Solve for Critical Stress $\sigma_c$

$$
\dfrac{\pi a \sigma_c^2}{E} = 2 \gamma_s
$$

$$
\sigma_c^2 = \dfrac{2 E \gamma_s}{\pi a}
$$

$$
\boxed{\sigma_c = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 E \gamma_s}{\pi a}}}
$$

### Step 7: Physical Interpretation
- Critical stress is **inversely proportional to the square root of crack length** — a 4× longer crack reduces strength by half.
- Higher surface energy (γ<sub>s</sub>) and higher modulus (E) raise the fracture strength.
- Griffith's prediction for glass was ~ 0.7 GPa, vs. theoretical strength of ~ 7 GPa — a much better match than the **theoretical (Orowan–Polanyi) value**, validating the theory.

---

## Derivation 2: Critical Crack Length in Terms of Fracture Toughness

### Given
- A plate of thickness B contains a central through-thickness crack of half-length a under stress σ.
- Geometry factor Y = 1 (infinite plate approximation).

### Step 1: Stress Intensity Factor
$$
K_I = Y \sigma \sqrt{\pi a}
$$

### Step 2: Fracture Criterion
The crack propagates when K<sub>I</sub> reaches the material's fracture toughness K<sub>IC</sub>:

$$
K_{IC} = \sigma \sqrt{\pi a_c}
$$

### Step 3: Solve for Critical Crack Length a<sub>c</sub>
$$
\sigma^2 \pi a_c = K_{IC}^2
$$

$$
\boxed{a_c = \dfrac{1}{\pi} \left( \dfrac{K_{IC}}{Y \sigma} \right)^2}
$$

### Step 4: Numerical Insight
For a steel with $K_{IC} = 60 \, \text{MPa}\sqrt{m}$, Y = 1, $\sigma = 200$ MPa:

$$
a_c = \dfrac{1}{\pi}\left( \dfrac{60}{200} \right)^2 = \dfrac{0.09}{\pi} \approx 0.0286 \text{ m} = 28.6 \text{ mm}
$$

This means a 28.6 mm half-length (57.2 mm full-length) central crack would cause catastrophic fracture — and **inspection intervals** must be designed to detect cracks well below this size.

---

## Derivation 3: Fatigue Life from Paris Law (m ≠ 2 case)

### Given
- A central through-thickness crack grows from initial size a<sub>i</sub> to critical size a<sub>c</sub> under constant amplitude cyclic stress range $\Delta \sigma$.
- Geometry factor Y is constant; Paris law valid throughout.

### Step 1: Write Paris Law
$$
\dfrac{da}{dN} = C (\Delta K)^m
$$

with $\Delta K = Y \Delta \sigma \sqrt{\pi a}$.

### Step 2: Substitute
$$
\dfrac{da}{dN} = C \left( Y \Delta \sigma \sqrt{\pi a} \right)^m
$$

$$
\dfrac{da}{dN} = C (Y \Delta \sigma)^m (\pi a)^{m/2}
$$

$$
\dfrac{da}{dN} = C (Y \Delta \sigma \sqrt{\pi})^m \, a^{m/2}
$$

### Step 3: Separate Variables
$$
\dfrac{da}{a^{m/2}} = C (Y \Delta \sigma \sqrt{\pi})^m \, dN
$$

$$
a^{-m/2} \, da = C (Y \Delta \sigma \sqrt{\pi})^m \, dN
$$

### Step 4: Integrate
$$
\int_{a_i}^{a_c} a^{-m/2} \, da = C (Y \Delta \sigma \sqrt{\pi})^m \int_0^{N_f} dN
$$

For m ≠ 2:

$$
\left[ \dfrac{a^{1 - m/2}}{1 - m/2} \right]_{a_i}^{a_c} = C (Y \Delta \sigma \sqrt{\pi})^m \, N_f
$$

$$
\dfrac{a_c^{1 - m/2} - a_i^{1 - m/2}}{1 - m/2} = C (Y \Delta \sigma \sqrt{\pi})^m \, N_f
$$

### Step 5: Solve for N<sub>f</sub>
Since $1 - m/2 = -(m/2 - 1)$:

$$
N_f = \dfrac{a_i^{1 - m/2} - a_c^{1 - m/2}}{C (Y \Delta \sigma \sqrt{\pi})^m (m/2 - 1)}
$$

$$
\boxed{N_f = \dfrac{a_i^{1 - m/2} - a_c^{1 - m/2}}{C (Y \Delta \sigma \sqrt{\pi})^m \left( \dfrac{m}{2} - 1 \right)}}
$$

### Step 6: Special Case (m = 2)
If m = 2, the integral gives a logarithmic form:

$$
\int_{a_i}^{a_c} \dfrac{da}{a} = C (Y \Delta \sigma \sqrt{\pi})^2 \int_0^{N_f} dN
$$

$$
N_f = \dfrac{1}{C (Y \Delta \sigma \sqrt{\pi})^2} \ln \left( \dfrac{a_c}{a_i} \right)
$$

---

## Derivation 4: Percent Cold Work and Resulting Strength

### Given
- A cylindrical rod of initial diameter d<sub>0</sub> is cold-drawn through a die to final diameter d<sub>f</sub>.

### Step 1: Compute Areas
$$
A_0 = \dfrac{\pi d_0^2}{4}, \qquad A_f = \dfrac{\pi d_f^2}{4}
$$

### Step 2: Percent Cold Work
$$
\%CW = \dfrac{A_0 - A_f}{A_0} \times 100 = \left(1 - \dfrac{d_f^2}{d_0^2}\right) \times 100
$$

### Step 3: Resulting Yield Strength (Empirical)
For most metals, yield strength rises approximately linearly with % cold work up to ~ 50 %:

$$
\sigma_y = \sigma_{y0} + k (\%CW)
$$

where $\sigma_{y0}$ = annealed yield strength, k = material constant (~ 7 MPa per 1 % CW for low-carbon steel).

### Step 4: Numerical Example
If d<sub>0</sub> = 20 mm, d<sub>f</sub> = 15 mm, then:

$$
\%CW = \left(1 - \dfrac{15^2}{20^2}\right) \times 100 = (1 - 0.5625) \times 100 = 43.75\%
$$

For annealed mild steel with $\sigma_{y0} = 250$ MPa, k = 7 MPa/%:

$$
\sigma_y = 250 + 7 \times 43.75 = 250 + 306.25 = 556.25 \text{ MPa}
$$

> A 43.75 % reduction in area **more than doubles** the yield strength.

---

## Derivation 5: True Stress – True Strain Conversion and Hollomon's Law

### Step 1: From Engineering to True Stress
Engineering stress uses the **original** area A<sub>0</sub>:

$$
\sigma = \dfrac{P}{A_0}
$$

True stress uses the **instantaneous** area A (assuming constant volume during plastic deformation, V = A·L = const):

$$
A = \dfrac{A_0 L_0}{L}, \quad A = \dfrac{A_0}{1 + \epsilon}
$$

$$
\sigma_t = \dfrac{P}{A} = \dfrac{P (1 + \epsilon)}{A_0} = \sigma (1 + \epsilon)
$$

### Step 2: From Engineering to True Strain
Engineering strain: $\epsilon = (L - L_0) / L_0$.

Differential true strain: $d\epsilon_t = dL / L$.

Integrate from L<sub>0</sub> to L:

$$
\epsilon_t = \int_{L_0}^{L} \dfrac{dL}{L} = \ln \left( \dfrac{L}{L_0} \right) = \ln (1 + \epsilon)
$$

### Step 3: Hollomon Equation in Plastic Region
$$
\sigma_t = K \epsilon_t^n
$$

Take log both sides:

$$
\ln \sigma_t = \ln K + n \ln \epsilon_t
$$

A plot of ln σ<sub>t</sub> vs. ln ε<sub>t</sub> gives a straight line of slope n (strain-hardening exponent) and intercept ln K.

### Step 4: n and K — Physical Meaning
- n = 0 → perfectly plastic (no hardening).
- n = 1 → perfectly elastic.
- Typical metals: n = 0.1 – 0.5.
- K (MPa) sets the **strength level** at ε<sub>t</sub> = 1.

---

## Analytical Matrix — Cold vs. Hot Working

| Aspect | Cold Working | Hot Working |
|---|---|---|
| Temperature | Below T<sub>recryst</sub> | Above T<sub>recryst</sub> |
| Recrystallization | No | Yes (dynamic) |
| Strain hardening | Yes | No |
| Strength | ↑ | ↓ (but ductility ↑) |
| Ductility | ↓ | ↑ |
| Force required | High | Low |
| Surface finish | Better | Poorer (oxide scale) |
| Dimensional accuracy | Better | Poorer (thermal contraction) |
| Grain structure | Distorted, elongated | Refined, equiaxed |
| Internal defects | Increased (residual stress) | Reduced (porosity closes) |
| Typical products | Sheets, wires, tubes | Plates, sections, forgings |

---

## Analytical Matrix — Failure Modes vs. Mechanism

| Failure Mode | Mechanism | Typical Surface Feature | Typical Ship Component |
|---|---|---|---|
| Ductile overload | Void nucleation, growth, coalescence | Cup-and-cone, dimples | Mildly-overloaded bracket |
| Brittle fracture | Cleavage, low-energy separation | Facets, river patterns | Cold-service hull plate |
| Fatigue | Cyclic slip, striations | Beach marks, striations | Propeller shaft, hull at hotspot |
| Creep | Grain-boundary sliding | Multiple intergranular cracks | Boiler tube, superheater |
| Stress corrosion | Synergy of stress + corrosion | Branching cracks | Stainless pipe in seawater |
| Hydrogen embrittlement | H atoms + stress | Internal fissures | Welded high-strength steel |
| Corrosion fatigue | Cyclic stress + corrosion | Mixed striations + pitting | Offshore riser |

---

# Module 2 - Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## Diagram 1: Cold Working vs. Hot Working — Block Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    inputA[Raw Metal Billet or Plate] --> route1{Temperature of Deformation}
    route1 -- Below 0.4 Tm --> coldProcess[Plastic Deformation Below Recrystallization]
    coldProcess --> coldMech[Dislocation Density Rises from 10 to the 6 to 10 to the 10 per cm squared]
    coldProcess --> coldOut1[Yield Strength and Hardness Increase]
    coldProcess --> coldOut2[Ductility and Toughness Decrease]
    coldProcess --> coldOut3[Residual Stresses Increase]
    coldProcess --> coldApp[Applications: Cold Rolled Sheets, Drawn Wires, Deep Drawn Parts]
    route1 -- Above 0.4 Tm --> hotProcess[Plastic Deformation Above Recrystallization]
    hotProcess --> hotMech[Dynamic Recrystallization Produces New Strain Free Grains]
    hotProcess --> hotOut1[Grain Refinement and Porosity Closure]
    hotProcess --> hotOut2[No Net Strain Hardening]
    hotProcess --> hotOut3[Improved Ductility and Toughness]
    hotProcess --> hotApp[Applications: Hot Rolled Hull Plates, Forged Shafts, Extruded Sections]
    coldApp --> shipEnd[Ship Component]
    hotApp --> shipEnd
```

## Diagram 2: Stress-Strain Curve Regions (Engineering, Ductile Steel)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    origin[Origin Zero Zero] --> propRegion[Linear Elastic Region Slope equals E]
    propRegion --> propLim[Proportional Limit]
    propLim --> elastLim[Elastic Limit]
    elastLim --> yldPt[Yield Point fy]
    yldPt --> strainHard[Strain Hardening Region]
    strainHard --> utsPeak[Ultimate Tensile Strength fy is at UTS]
    utsPeak --> neck[Necking Region]
    neck --> fracPt[Fracture Point]
    yldPt -. design stress for steel .-> designMark[Allowable Stress with FoS]
    fracPt -. toughness area .-> toughnessMark[Area under curve]
```

## Diagram 3: Fracture Mechanics Logic Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA[Identify Cracked Component] --> ndt[Apply NDT for Crack Detection]
    ndt --> measureA[Measure Crack Length a]
    measureA --> sigmaApp[Determine Applied Stress sigma and Geometry Y]
    sigmaApp --> calcK[Calculate KI equals Y sigma sqrt pi a]
    calcK --> compareK{Compare KI with KIC}
    compareK -- KI less than KIC --> safe[Crack is Subcritical, Safe for Service]
    compareK -- KI greater than or equal to KIC --> fail[Catastrophic Fracture, Immediate Action]
    safe --> schedule[Schedule Inspection]
    schedule --> parisCheck[Apply Paris Law da per dN equals C delta K to the m]
    parisCheck --> predictA[Predict Crack Growth and Life]
    predictA --> nextInsp[Set Next Inspection Interval]
```

## Diagram 4: Fatigue Crack Propagation — Three Stages

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    stage1[Stage I Crack Initiation at Persistent Slip Bands] --> stage2[Stage II Stable Crack Growth Paris Regime]
    stage2 --> stage3[Stage III Unstable Fast Fracture when Kmax approaches KIC]
    stage1 -. threshold .-> thresh[Below delta Kth Crack Does Not Grow]
    stage2 -. paris .-> parisEq[da per dN equals C delta K to the m]
    stage3 -. fracture .-> final[Sudden Catastrophic Failure]
```

## Diagram 5: Griffith's Theory — Energy Balance

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    intactPlate[Infinite Plate Under Uniaxial Stress sigma] --> addCrack[Introduce Central Crack of Half Length a]
    addCrack --> energyChange[Change in Total Energy]
    energyChange --> strainRel[Strain Energy Released equals pi a squared sigma squared B over E]
    energyChange --> surfAdd[Surface Energy Created equals 4 a B gamma s]
    strainRel --> comp[Compare Released Energy with Surface Energy]
    surfAdd --> comp
    comp --> crit{sigma greater than or equal to sigma c}
    crit -- Yes --> growth[Crack Propagates Spontaneously]
    crit -- No --> arrest[Crack is Stable]
```

## Diagram 6: Material Selection Logic for Ship Hull Plates

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    req[Design Requirements for Hull Plate] --> q1{Strength Needed}
    q1 -- Normal Service --> ms[Mild Steel ASTM A131 Grade A or B]
    q1 -- High Strength Lightweight --> hts[High Tensile Steel AH36 or DH36 or EH36]
    q1 -- Naval or Arctic --> hsla[HSLA Steel HY80 or HY100]
    ms --> q2{Weldability Required}
    hts --> q2
    hsla --> q2
    q2 -- Yes --> ceq[Check Carbon Equivalent Ceq less than 0.45 percent]
    q2 -- No --> specialAlloy[Special Alloy or Clad Plate]
    ceq --> q3{Service Environment}
    q3 -- Cold Waters --> impactTest[Charpy Impact Test at Service Temperature]
    q3 -- Tropical --> coat[Protective Coating System]
    q3 -- Corrosive --> cs[Corrosion Resistant Steel or Cu-Ni Cladding]
```

# Module 2 - Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Define cold working of metals. Why does the strength of a metal increase during cold working?**

**Model Answer:**
Cold working is the plastic deformation of a metal carried out at a temperature below its recrystallization temperature (typically below 0.4 × T<sub>melt</sub> in Kelvin). During cold working, the dislocation density increases dramatically (from ~10<sup>6</sup> to ~10<sup>10</sup>–10<sup>12</sup> lines/cm²). The increase in dislocations creates mutual obstacles to further slip, requiring higher applied stress to continue plastic deformation. This phenomenon is known as **strain hardening (work hardening)**, which raises the yield strength, ultimate tensile strength, and hardness of the metal. (3 marks)

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**State Griffith's criterion for crack propagation in brittle materials.**

**Model Answer:**
According to Griffith's theory, a brittle crack will propagate when the **decrease in elastic strain energy** of the body (due to crack formation) is at least equal to the **energy required to create the new crack surfaces**. For an infinite plate of elastic modulus E containing a central crack of half-length a and specific surface energy γ<sub>s</sub>, the critical stress for crack propagation is:
$$\sigma_c = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 E \gamma_s}{\pi a}}$$
A crack will grow spontaneously when the applied stress $\sigma$ ≥ $\sigma_c$. (3 marks)

---

## Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice

> **KTU ESE Format:** Each Part B question has sub-parts (a) and (b) carrying 7 marks each. Students must answer **either full Question A or full Question B**.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 2]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain with neat sketches the difference between **cold working and hot working** of metals. Discuss the effect of cold working on the mechanical properties of a metal.

**Model Answer:**

**Definitions:**
- **Cold working** is plastic deformation performed below the recrystallization temperature (~0.4 × T<sub>melt</sub> in K).
- **Hot working** is plastic deformation performed above the recrystallization temperature, so the metal recrystallizes dynamically during the process.

**Comparison (Tabular form – 3 marks):**

| Aspect | Cold Working | Hot Working |
|---|---|---|
| Temperature | < 0.4 T<sub>melt</sub> | > 0.4 T<sub>melt</sub> |
| Recrystallization | Does not occur | Occurs dynamically |
| Strain hardening | Yes, accumulates | No |
| Strength & hardness | Increases | Decreases |
| Ductility & toughness | Decreases | Increases |
| Force required | High | Low |
| Surface finish | Good (smooth) | Poor (oxide scale) |
| Dimensional tolerance | High | Low (due to thermal contraction) |
| Grain structure | Elongated, distorted | Refined, equiaxed |
| Internal defects | May increase residual stress | Porosity closes |
| Examples | Cold-rolled sheets, wire drawing | Hot-rolled plates, forging |

**Effect of Cold Working on Mechanical Properties (4 marks):**
1. **Yield strength ↑** — due to dislocation pile-ups and tangles.
2. **Ultimate tensile strength ↑** — strain hardening.
3. **Hardness ↑** — resistance to plastic deformation rises.
4. **Ductility ↓** — elongation and reduction in area decrease.
5. **Toughness ↓** — energy absorbed in fracture decreases.
6. **Residual stresses ↑** — uneven plastic flow between surface and core; relieved by annealing.
7. **Fatigue strength** improves initially, then drops if over-cold-worked.
8. **% Cold Work formula:** $\%CW = \frac{A_0 - A_f}{A_0} \times 100$.

> The severity of property change is captured by the empirical relation:
> $$\sigma_y = \sigma_{y0} + k(\%CW)$$
> where k is a material constant.

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** A low-carbon steel rod of 25 mm diameter is cold-drawn to 20 mm diameter. Calculate (i) the percent cold work, and (ii) the new yield strength if the annealed yield strength is 240 MPa and k = 7 MPa per 1 % CW.

**Model Answer:**

**Given:** d<sub>0</sub> = 25 mm, d<sub>f</sub> = 20 mm, σ<sub>y0</sub> = 240 MPa, k = 7 MPa/%.

**(i) Percent cold work:**

$$
A_0 = \dfrac{\pi (25)^2}{4} = 490.87 \text{ mm}^2
$$

$$
A_f = \dfrac{\pi (20)^2}{4} = 314.16 \text{ mm}^2
$$

$$
\%CW = \dfrac{A_0 - A_f}{A_0} \times 100 = \dfrac{490.87 - 314.16}{490.87} \times 100
$$

$$
\%CW = \dfrac{176.71}{490.87} \times 100 = 36.0\%
$$

**(ii) New yield strength:**

$$
\sigma_y = \sigma_{y0} + k (\%CW) = 240 + 7 \times 36.0
$$

$$
\sigma_y = 240 + 252 = 492 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Final Answers:** %CW = **36 %**, σ<sub>y</sub> = **492 MPa**.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 2]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the **Griffith equation for critical crack propagation stress** in a brittle material. State its significance in fracture mechanics.

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Problem Setup:**
Consider an infinite plate of elastic modulus E, thickness B, under uniform uniaxial tension σ. The plate contains a through-thickness central crack of half-length a and specific surface energy γ<sub>s</sub>.

**Step 2 — Released Elastic Strain Energy:**
For a central crack of half-length a, the released elastic energy is:
$$
U_E = \dfrac{\pi a^2 \sigma^2 B}{E}
$$

**Step 3 — Surface Energy Created:**
Two new surfaces of area 2a·B each are formed, so:
$$
U_S = 2 \times 2aB \times \gamma_s = 4aB\gamma_s
$$

**Step 4 — Net Change in Energy:**
$$
\Delta U = U_S - U_E = 4aB\gamma_s - \dfrac{\pi a^2 \sigma^2 B}{E}
$$

**Step 5 — Critical Condition:**
At the critical state, the net energy is at a minimum:
$$
\dfrac{d(\Delta U)}{da} = 0
$$

$$
4B\gamma_s - \dfrac{2\pi a \sigma_c^2 B}{E} = 0
$$

$$
\boxed{\sigma_c = \sqrt{\dfrac{2E\gamma_s}{\pi a}}}
$$

**Step 6 — Significance (3 marks):**
1. First physically consistent theory explaining why brittle materials fail far below theoretical strength.
2. Direct proportionality of strength to $1/\sqrt{a}$ — explains why doubling crack length reduces strength by 30 % only, not 50 %.
3. Foundation for Irwin's Stress Intensity Factor and modern fracture mechanics.
4. For ductile materials, modified by Orowan: $\gamma_s \to (\gamma_s + \gamma_p)$ where $\gamma_p$ is plastic work.
5. Critical design principle: **limit crack size** and select materials with high fracture toughness.

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** A steel plate with a central through-thickness crack of half-length 12 mm is subjected to a tensile stress of 250 MPa. The fracture toughness of the steel is $K_{IC} = 55 \text{ MPa}\sqrt{m}$. Determine the **factor of safety** against brittle fracture. (Y = 1.)

**Model Answer:**

**Given:** a = 12 mm = 0.012 m, σ = 250 MPa, $K_{IC} = 55 \text{ MPa}\sqrt{m}$, Y = 1.

**Step 1 — Stress Intensity Factor:**
$$
K_I = Y \sigma \sqrt{\pi a} = 1 \times 250 \times \sqrt{\pi \times 0.012}
$$

$$
\pi a = \pi \times 0.012 = 0.03770
$$

$$
\sqrt{0.03770} = 0.1942
$$

$$
K_I = 250 \times 0.1942 = 48.55 \text{ MPa}\sqrt{m}
$$

**Step 2 — Critical (Failure) Stress:**
$$
\sigma_c = \dfrac{K_{IC}}{Y \sqrt{\pi a}} = \dfrac{55}{1 \times 0.1942} = 283.2 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Step 3 — Factor of Safety:**
$$
\text{FoS} = \dfrac{\sigma_c}{\sigma_{applied}} = \dfrac{283.2}{250} = 1.13
$$

**Final Answer:** **Factor of Safety = 1.13** — the structure has a 13 % margin against brittle fracture.

> ⚠️ Note: FoS < 1.5 is **inadequate** for fracture-critical marine structures (IACS S21 requires FoS ≥ 2.0). Action: reduce applied stress, or repair/replace the plate.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Unit Mistake #1:** K<sub>I</sub> and K<sub>IC</sub> must be in **MPa√m**, never in MPa. Convert crack length to **metres**, not mm, when substituting.
> 2. **Common Mistake #2:** Forgetting the factor Y in $K_I = Y\sigma\sqrt{\pi a}$. KTU deducts 1 full mark for this.
> 3. **Common Mistake #3:** In the percent cold work question, using diameter ratio instead of area ratio. The correct formula uses **areas**, not diameters.
> 4. **Common Mistake #4:** In Griffith's derivation, omitting the "4aB" (four surfaces) and writing "2aB" — the central crack creates **two** surfaces, each of area 2a·B.
> 5. **Common Mistake #5:** In fatigue life integration, students forget the case **m = 2** (logarithmic form). Always state the case.
> 6. **Common Mistake #6:** Confusing **recrystallization temperature** (≈ 0.4 T<sub>melt</sub> in K) with melting temperature itself.
> 7. **Common Mistake #7:** Writing true strain as $\epsilon_t = \epsilon$ (engineering) — must be $\ln(1+\epsilon)$.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Cold working**: below 0.4 T<sub>melt</sub>; strain hardening; strength ↑, ductility ↓.
- **Hot working**: above 0.4 T<sub>melt</sub>; dynamic recrystallization; grain refinement; porosity closure.
- **%CW** formula uses **areas**; k (MPa/%) gives the linear strength rise.
- **Hollomon equation** $\sigma_t = K\epsilon_t^n$ characterizes plastic flow; n is hardening exponent.
- **Failure analysis** is a step-by-step elimination: visual → NDT → mechanical → metallography → fractography (SEM).
- **Fracture mechanics** quantifies crack-tip stress via K<sub>I</sub>; failure when K<sub>I</sub> ≥ K<sub>IC</sub>.
- **Three fracture modes**: I (opening), II (sliding), III (tearing) — Mode I is critical.
- **Griffith's equation**: $\sigma_c = \sqrt{2E\gamma_s/(\pi a)}$; foundation of fracture mechanics.
- **Orowan's modification** for metals: $\gamma_s \to (\gamma_s + \gamma_p)$.
- **Fatigue** has 3 stages: initiation → Paris propagation → final fracture.
- **Paris law**: $da/dN = C(\Delta K)^m$; integrated to get cycles-to-failure.
- **S-N curve** (Basquin): $\sigma_a = \sigma'_f (2N_f)^b$ — for uncracked high-cycle fatigue.
- **Miner's rule**: $\sum n_i/N_i = 1$ — cumulative damage criterion.
- **Hull plate materials**: mild steel (ASTM A131), high-tensile (AH36, DH36, EH36), HSLA (HY-80, HY-100).
- **Propeller materials**: Ni-Al bronze, Mn-bronze, duplex stainless steel.
- **Shaft materials**: forged C-Mn, Ni-Cr-Mo alloys, Monel K-500.
- **Piping (sea-water)**: Cu-Ni (90/10, 70/30), titanium, GRP.
- **Weldability indicator**: $C_{eq} = C + \frac{Mn}{6} + \frac{Cr+Mo+V}{5} + \frac{Ni+Cu}{15}$ (target ≤ 0.45 %).
- **Always convert crack length to metres** before substituting in MPa√m expressions.
- **Always include Y** in stress intensity factor calculations.
- **Class society rules** (DNV, LR, ABS, IRS) govern material selection, inspection intervals, and FoS.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Modes of Heat Transfer
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST411 - MECHANICAL ENGINEERING FOR SHIP DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Modes of Heat Transfer

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Heat Transfer?

**Heat Transfer** is the branch of engineering thermodynamics and thermal science that deals with the generation, use, conversion, and exchange of thermal (heat) energy between physical systems. According to the **Second Law of Thermodynamics**, heat energy spontaneously flows from a region of higher temperature to a region of lower temperature until thermal equilibrium is reached.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** Heat transfer is the movement of thermal energy from one physical system or region of matter to another, driven by a temperature difference (thermal gradient). The driving potential for all modes of heat transfer is the **temperature difference ($\Delta T$)**, measured in **Kelvin (K)** or **degrees Celsius (°C)**.

In the context of **ship design**, heat transfer plays a pivotal role in:
- Sizing marine boilers and steam generators.
- Designing heat exchangers for lube oil coolers, fresh water generators, and condensers.
- Insulation of engine rooms, cargo holds carrying heated liquids, and exhaust manifolds.
- Thermal management of electrical switchboards and accommodation HVAC systems.

## 1.2 The Three Fundamental Modes of Heat Transfer

Heat can be transferred by **three distinct mechanisms**. The following table provides a bird's-eye view before we dive deep into each.

| Mode | Carrying Mechanism | Medium Required? | Ship Design Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Conduction** | Molecular/electron vibration & collision | Yes (Solid, Liquid, Gas) | Heat passing through a boiler tube wall |
| **Convection** | Bulk fluid motion (advection of molecules) | Yes (Liquid or Gas) | Coolant flowing over an engine cylinder head |
| **Radiation** | Electromagnetic waves (photons) | **No** (Vacuum transparent) | Solar heat gain on the ship's upper deck |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Board-Exam Point:** In most practical shipboard problems, all three modes occur **simultaneously** (combined heat transfer). However, for analytical simplicity, the KTU board examinations typically isolate each mode for individual study and numerical evaluation.

## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogies

### 🧊 Analogy 1: Conduction — "The Hot Spoon in Tea"
Imagine you place a **metallic spoon** into a hot cup of tea. After a minute, even the handle of the spoon (far from the tea) becomes warm. Heat has travelled through the solid metal rod without any movement of the metal itself. This is **conduction** — energy transfer via molecular vibration. Metals conduct heat well because they have **free electrons** that transport energy rapidly.

### 🌊 Analogy 2: Convection — "The Boiling Kettle"
When water boils in a kettle, you see **physical movement** of hot water rising and cool water sinking. This circulating motion (a *convection current*) is what carries heat throughout the fluid. The molecules *physically move* and carry their thermal energy with them. In a ship, this is exactly how seawater in the **box coolers** absorbs heat from the engine's jacket cooling circuit.

### ☀️ Analogy 3: Radiation — "The Warmth of the Sun"
The Sun's heat reaches the Earth across **150 million kilometers of vacuum** through outer space. No medium is required. This is **thermal radiation** — energy carried by **electromagnetic waves** (primarily infrared, $\lambda \approx 0.1$ to $100 \ \mu m$). On a ship, the surface of an exposed **exhaust manifold** glows red and radiates heat to the surrounding engine room even when the air is stationary.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Outcome Mapping:** This topic directly addresses **CO3 (Module 3):** *"Illustrate the various modes of heat transfer relevant to shipboard thermal systems and apply governing equations to marine engineering components."*

## 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following physical constants frequently appear in heat transfer calculations and must be memorized for the KTU board exam:

- **Stefan-Boltzmann constant:** $\sigma = 5.670 \times 10^{-8} \ \text{W/(m}^2\cdot\text{K}^4\text{)}$
- **Universal gas constant:** $R_u = 8.314 \ \text{kJ/(kmol}\cdot\text{K)}$
- **Thermal conductivity of water at 20°C:** $k \approx 0.598 \ \text{W/(m}\cdot\text{K)}$
- **Thermal conductivity of copper:** $k \approx 401 \ \text{W/(m}\cdot\text{K)}$
- **Thermal conductivity of stainless steel (marine grade 316):** $k \approx 16.3 \ \text{W/(m}\cdot\text{K)}$
- **Solar constant (outside Earth's atmosphere):** $G_{sc} = 1367 \ \text{W/m}^2$
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Mode 1: Conduction

### 2.1.1 Mechanism in Detail
Conduction is the transfer of heat through **stationary matter** by physical contact, without bulk movement of the material. At the microscopic level:
- In **solids**, energy is transferred by lattice vibrations (quantized as *phonons*) and by the drift of free electrons.
- In **fluids (liquids/gases)**, conduction occurs via molecular collisions during random thermal motion.
- Metals are the best conductors because of their high free-electron density.

### 2.1.2 Fourier's Law of Heat Conduction (The Governing Equation)
For a **one-dimensional, steady-state** conduction through a plane wall:

$$q_x = -k \frac{dT}{dx}$$

Where:
- $q_x$ = **Heat flux** (heat transfer rate per unit area), $\text{W/m}^2$
- $k$ = **Thermal conductivity** of the material, $\text{W/(m}\cdot\text{K)}$
- $\frac{dT}{dx}$ = **Temperature gradient** in the direction of heat flow, $\text{K/m}$
- The **negative sign** indicates heat flows in the direction of *decreasing* temperature (Second Law compliance).

The total heat transfer rate is obtained by multiplying by the cross-sectional area $A$:

$$Q = -kA\frac{dT}{dx}$$

### 2.1.3 Plane Wall Solution (Most Examined Form)
For a homogeneous plane wall of thickness $L$ with surface temperatures $T_1$ (hot) and $T_2$ (cold):

$$Q = \frac{kA(T_1 - T_2)}{L} = \frac{\Delta T}{R_{th}}$$

Where **thermal resistance** is defined as:

$$R_{th} = \frac{L}{kA}$$

### 2.1.4 Composite (Multi-Layer) Walls — Critical for Ship Insulation
Shipboard insulation (e.g., a fire-resistant duct wall) typically has multiple layers. For series conduction:

$$Q = \frac{T_1 - T_{n+1}}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{L_i}{k_i A}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why is conduction important in ship design?** Boiler tubes, economizer coils, and evaporator shells are sized using Fourier's law. The thermal resistance concept allows designers to **predict heat loss** through composite insulation and select appropriate lagging thicknesses for IACS-compliant surfaces.

### 2.1.5 Thermal Conductivity — Material Property
The value of $k$ depends on:
- **Material type**: Metals (high) > Non-metallic solids (medium) > Gases (low) > Vacuum ($\approx 0$)
- **Temperature**: For most engineering materials, $k$ varies approximately linearly with temperature. KTU board questions often provide a mean $k$ or use $k(T) = k_0(1 + \beta T)$.

## 2.2 Mode 2: Convection

### 2.2.1 Mechanism in Detail
Convection involves heat transfer between a **solid surface** and a **moving fluid**. It is fundamentally a *two-step* process:
1. Conduction from the solid surface to the adjacent fluid particles (in the *boundary layer*).
2. Advection (bulk transport) of these heated particles by the fluid motion.

### 2.2.2 Classification of Convection
- **Forced Convection:** Fluid motion is driven by an external agent (pump, fan, ship's sea-water pump).
- **Natural (Free) Convection:** Fluid motion is driven by **buoyancy forces** arising from density differences caused by temperature gradients (e.g., natural draft in a ship boiler uptake).
- **Phase-Change Convection:** Convection accompanied by boiling (e.g., in a marine steam generator) or condensation (e.g., in a main condenser).

### 2.2.3 Newton's Law of Cooling (The Governing Equation)

$$Q = h A (T_s - T_\infty)$$

Where:
- $h$ = **Convective heat transfer coefficient**, $\text{W/(m}^2\cdot\text{K)}$
- $T_s$ = Surface temperature, K
- $T_\infty$ = Bulk fluid temperature far from the surface, K
- $A$ = Heat transfer area, $\text{m}^2$

The corresponding **convective thermal resistance** is:

$$R_{conv} = \frac{1}{hA}$$

### 2.2.4 The Convective Coefficient $h$ — Order of Magnitude
KTU frequently asks for the *order of magnitude* of $h$ in different situations:

| Situation | Approx. $h$ value, $\text{W/(m}^2\cdot\text{K)}$ |
|---|---|
| Natural convection in air | 5 – 25 |
| Forced convection in air (fan) | 10 – 200 |
| Natural convection in water | 100 – 1,000 |
| Forced convection in water (pump) | 500 – 20,000 |
| Boiling/condensation (phase change) | 2,500 – 100,000 |

### 2.2.5 The Non-Dimensional Numbers (KTU Hot List)
The Nusselt number $Nu$ is the key non-dimensional number governing convection:

$$Nu = \frac{hL_c}{k_f}$$

Where $L_c$ is a characteristic length and $k_f$ is the fluid's thermal conductivity. The Nusselt number is a function of the **Reynolds number** ($Re$, inertia vs. viscous forces) and the **Prandtl number** ($Pr$, momentum vs. thermal diffusivity):

$$Re = \frac{\rho V L_c}{\mu} \quad ; \quad Pr = \frac{\mu c_p}{k} = \frac{\nu}{\alpha}$$

Typical empirical correlations used in ship design:
- **Dittus-Boelter** (turbulent flow in smooth pipes, used for lube oil coolers):

$$Nu = 0.023 \, Re^{0.8} \, Pr^{n}$$

Where $n = 0.4$ for heating and $n = 0.3$ for cooling.

## 2.3 Mode 3: Radiation

### 2.3.1 Mechanism in Detail
Thermal radiation is the emission of **electromagnetic waves** by a body due to its temperature. All matter above absolute zero ($\text{0 K}$) emits thermal radiation. Unlike conduction and convection, radiation:
- Requires **no medium** (it can travel through vacuum).
- Travels at the **speed of light**, $c = 2.998 \times 10^8 \ \text{m/s}$.
- Is governed by **optical** (surface) properties — emissivity, absorptivity, reflectivity, transmissivity.

### 2.3.2 Stefan-Boltzmann Law (The Governing Equation)
The **total emissive power** of a *blackbody* is:

$$E_b = \sigma T^4$$

For a *real (gray) surface*, the emitted power is reduced by the **emissivity** $\varepsilon$ (a dimensionless quantity between 0 and 1):

$$E = \varepsilon \sigma T^4$$

The net radiative heat exchange between a small surface at $T_1$ and a much larger surrounding at $T_2$ is:

$$Q = \varepsilon \sigma A (T_1^4 - T_2^4)$$

### 2.3.3 Absorptivity, Reflectivity, Transmissivity
For radiation incident on a semi-transparent body:
$$\alpha + \rho + \tau = 1$$

- $\alpha$ = **Absorptivity** (fraction absorbed)
- $\rho$ = **Reflectivity** (fraction reflected)
- $\tau$ = **Transmissivity** (fraction transmitted through)

> [!NOTE]
> **Kirchhoff's Law:** For a body in thermal equilibrium, the emissivity of a surface equals its absorptivity at the same wavelength and temperature: $\varepsilon_\lambda = \alpha_\lambda$. This is a frequently tested KTU statement question.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (Single-Page Revision)

| # | Governing Law | Equation | Unit of Result | Key Symbol Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Fourier's Law (1-D steady) | $Q = \dfrac{kA \Delta T}{L}$ | W | $k$ = thermal conductivity |
| 2 | Heat Flux | $q'' = \dfrac{Q}{A} = -k \dfrac{dT}{dx}$ | $\text{W/m}^2$ | $\dfrac{dT}{dx}$ = temperature gradient |
| 3 | Thermal Resistance (conduction) | $R_{cond} = \dfrac{L}{kA}$ | K/W | $L$ = thickness |
| 4 | Composite Wall Resistance | $R_{tot} = \sum \dfrac{L_i}{k_i A}$ | K/W | $i$ = layer index |
| 5 | Newton's Law of Cooling | $Q = hA(T_s - T_\infty)$ | W | $h$ = convective coefficient |
| 6 | Convective Resistance | $R_{conv} = \dfrac{1}{hA}$ | K/W | $T_s$ = surface temp |
| 7 | Nusselt Number | $Nu = \dfrac{hL_c}{k_f}$ | dimensionless | $L_c$ = characteristic length |
| 8 | Reynolds Number | $Re = \dfrac{\rho V L_c}{\mu}$ | dimensionless | $V$ = flow velocity |
| 9 | Prandtl Number | $Pr = \dfrac{\mu c_p}{k}$ | dimensionless | $c_p$ = specific heat |
| 10 | Grashof Number | $Gr = \dfrac{g \beta \Delta T L_c^3}{\nu^2}$ | dimensionless | $\beta$ = thermal expansion coeff. |
| 11 | Stefan-Boltzmann Law | $E_b = \sigma T^4$ | $\text{W/m}^2$ | $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \ \text{W/(m}^2\text{K}^4\text{)}$ |
| 12 | Net Radiation Exchange | $Q = \varepsilon \sigma A (T_1^4 - T_2^4)$ | W | $\varepsilon$ = emissivity |
| 13 | View Factor Relation | $A_i F_{ij} = A_j F_{ji}$ | $\text{m}^2$ | $F_{ij}$ = view factor from $i$ to $j$ |
| 14 | Kirchhoff's Law | $\varepsilon = \alpha$ (at same $\lambda$, $T$) | dimensionless | Applicable in equilibrium |
| 15 | Energy Conservation (1-D SS) | $R_{tot} = R_{cond} + R_{conv} + R_{rad}$ | K/W | For series networks |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** In a real ship, the three modes occur *in series*. The total thermal resistance is summed, and the heat transfer rate is $\Delta T / R_{tot}$. This is the foundation of **heat exchanger design** using the LMTD (Log Mean Temperature Difference) method and the **$\varepsilon$-NTU (Effectiveness-Number of Transfer Units) method**.

## 2.5 The Combined Heat Transfer Coefficient (Ship Engineering Application)

In a typical marine boiler tube, heat flows as follows:
1. From hot combustion gases to the outer tube surface (convection, $h_g$).
2. Through the tube wall (conduction, $k_{wall}$).
3. From the inner tube surface to the feed water (convection, $h_w$).

The total resistance is:

$$R_{tot} = \frac{1}{h_g A_o} + \frac{\ln(D_o/D_i)}{2\pi k L} + \frac{1}{h_w A_i}$$

This is a direct application of the **resistance network** approach taught in Module 3 of PENST411.
