| **Heat Transfer Mechanism** | Recuperative (continuous wall) | All marine liquid-to-liquid HXs |
| | Regenerative (transient matrix) | Waste heat recovery boilers |
| | Direct Contact | Open feedwater heaters |
| **Compactness** | $\beta = \dfrac{A}{\rho^2 \, k} \, \dfrac{\Delta p}{\mu \, c_p}$ | Plate HXs score high $\beta$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The 2024 Scheme (PENST411 / Module 3) explicitly demands coverage of **(i) selection criteria**, **(ii) sizing basics**, **(iii) LMTD vs. NTU method**, and **(iv) ship-specific design constraints** such as limited headroom, vibration, and fouling due to seawater.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Overall Heat Transfer Equation — The Core Design Equation

The **single most important equation** in heat-exchanger sizing is:

$$
Q = U \, A \, \Delta T_m
$$

- $Q$ = total heat duty $[\text{W}]$
- $U$ = **overall heat-transfer coefficient** $[\text{W/m}^2\text{K}]$
- $A$ = heat-transfer surface area $[\text{m}^2]$
- $\Delta T_m$ = appropriate mean temperature difference $[\text{K}]$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Insight:** This is the *master sizing equation*. Board questions in Module 3 almost always start here. If you forget $U$, you cannot compute $A$, and the entire 14-mark problem collapses.

## 2.2 Resistances in Series — Derivation of $U$

Heat must pass through *multiple resistances in series*:

1. Convective resistance inside the tube (hot side)
2. Conduction resistance through the tube wall
3. Convective resistance on the shell side (cold side)
4. Fouling resistances on both sides

Hence:

$$
\dfrac{1}{U} = \dfrac{1}{h_i} + \dfrac{R_{f,i}}{x_i} + \dfrac{\ln(D_o/D_i)}{2\pi k_w L} + \dfrac{R_{f,o}}{x_o} + \dfrac{1}{h_o}
$$

Per unit area (simplified for thin walls and using $D_o \approx D_i$):

$$
\dfrac{1}{U} = \dfrac{1}{h_i} + R_{f,i} + \dfrac{\delta}{k_w} + R_{f,o} + \dfrac{1}{h_o}
$$

## 2.3 Log Mean Temperature Difference (LMTD)

For a **counter-flow** heat exchanger:

$$
\Delta T_{lm} = \dfrac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln\left(\dfrac{\Delta T_1}{\Delta T_2}\right)} \quad \text{where} \quad
\begin{cases}
\Delta T_1 = T_{h,in} - T_{c,out} \\[4pt]
\Delta T_2 = T_{h,out} - T_{c,in}
\end{cases}
$$

For a **cross-flow** or shell-and-tube HX, we use the **LMTD Correction Factor $F$**:

$$
\Delta T_m = F \, \Delta T_{lm,CF}
$$

where $F$ is read from standard TEMA charts and depends on the dimensionless temperature parameters $P$ and $R$.

## 2.4 Effectiveness–NTU Method

When outlet temperatures are unknown (a typical design scenario), the **NTU method** is preferred:

$$
\varepsilon = \dfrac{Q_{actual}}{Q_{max}} = \dfrac{Q_{actual}}{C_{min}\,(T_{h,in}-T_{c,in})}
$$

$$
\text{NTU} = \dfrac{U\,A}{C_{min}} \qquad C = \dot m \, c_p \;\;[\text{W/K}]
$$

Counter-flow effectiveness equation:

$$
\varepsilon = \dfrac{1 - \exp\left[-\text{NTU}\,(1 - C_r)\right]}{1 - C_r\,\exp\left[-\text{NTU}\,(1 - C_r)\right]}
$$

where the **heat-capacity ratio** is:

$$
C_r = \dfrac{C_{min}}{C_{max}}
$$

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Symbol | Expression | Units | Used For |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Heat Duty | $Q = \dot m \, c_p \, \Delta T$ | W | Energy balance |
| 2 | Overall coefficient | $\dfrac{1}{U} = \dfrac{1}{h_i} + R_{f,i} + \dfrac{\delta}{k_w} + R_{f,o} + \dfrac{1}{h_o}$ | m²K/W | Resistances in series |
| 3 | Sizing | $Q = U \, A \, \Delta T_m$ | W | Master design equation |
| 4 | LMTD (CF) | $\Delta T_{lm} = \dfrac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1/\Delta T_2)}$ | K | Pure counter-flow |
| 5 | Correction factor | $\Delta T_m = F \cdot \Delta T_{lm,CF}$ | K | Shell & tube, cross-flow |
| 6 | $C_{min}$, $C_{max}$ | $\dot m \, c_p$ each stream | W/K | NTU method |
| 7 | Capacity ratio | $C_r = C_{min}/C_{max}$ | – | NTU method |
| 8 | NTU | $\text{NTU} = U A / C_{min}$ | – | Sizing via $\varepsilon$ |
| 9 | Effectiveness | $\varepsilon = Q/(C_{min}\Delta T_{max,in})$ | – | Performance check |
| 10 | Pressure drop | $\Delta p = f \dfrac{L}{D}\dfrac{\rho v^2}{2} + K \dfrac{\rho v^2}{2}$ | Pa | Pump sizing |
| 11 | Shell-side $h$ (Kern) | $h_o = 0.36 \,\dfrac{k}{D_e}\,Re^{0.55}\,Pr^{1/3}\,(\mu/\mu_w)^{0.14}$ | W/m²K | Empirical design |
| 12 | Tube-side $h$ (Sieder–Tate) | $h_i = 0.023 \,\dfrac{k}{D_i}\,Re^{0.8}\,Pr^{1/3}\,(\mu/\mu_w)^{0.14}$ | W/m²K | Turbulent flow inside tubes |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip:** In KTU valuation, students who write $\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2$ instead of $\Delta T_1/\Delta T_2$ *lose 2 marks instantly*. Always remember: **LMTD = difference over log-ratio.**

## 2.6 Real-World Utility

- **Marine engine jacket cooler:** Prevents the cylinder head from exceeding **90 °C**, beyond which liner lubrication fails.
- **Lube oil cooler:** Maintains oil viscosity; oil above **60 °C** loses film strength — catastrophic bearing failure results.
- **Freshwater generator (evaporator):** Uses waste-heat shell-and-tube to produce boiler feedwater from seawater.
- **Charge air cooler:** Raises air density entering the combustion chamber, improving volumetric efficiency.
- **Economizer:** Recovers exhaust gas enthalpy, reducing specific fuel oil consumption (SFOC) by **2–5 %**.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the LMTD for a Counter-Flow Heat Exchanger

We begin with a differential element of length $\mathrm{d}x$ along the flow direction. Let the hot fluid lose a small amount of heat $\mathrm{d}Q$ while cooling by $\mathrm{d}T_h$, and the cold fluid gain the same $\mathrm{d}Q$ while warming by $\mathrm{d}T_c$.

### Step 1 — Local heat duty through the differential element

$$
\mathrm{d}Q = U \cdot P \cdot \Delta T(x)\, \mathrm{d}x
$$

where $P$ is the perimeter of the heat-transfer surface and $\Delta T(x) = T_h(x) - T_c(x)$ is the local temperature difference.

### Step 2 — Energy balance on each stream

$$
\mathrm{d}Q = -\dot{m}_h\,c_{p,h}\,\mathrm{d}T_h \quad \text{(hot fluid loses heat)}
$$

$$
\mathrm{d}Q = \dot{m}_c\,c_{p,c}\,\mathrm{d}T_c \quad \text{(cold fluid gains heat)}
$$

Define the **heat-capacity rates** $C_h = \dot m_h c_{p,h}$ and $C_c = \dot m_c c_{p,c}$. Then:

$$
\mathrm{d}T_h = -\dfrac{\mathrm{d}Q}{C_h}, \qquad \mathrm{d}T_c = \dfrac{\mathrm{d}Q}{C_c}
$$

### Step 3 — Differential of $\Delta T$

$$
\mathrm{d}(\Delta T) = \mathrm{d}T_h - \mathrm{d}T_c = -\dfrac{\mathrm{d}Q}{C_h} - \dfrac{\mathrm{d}Q}{C_c}
$$

$$
\mathrm{d}(\Delta T) = -\mathrm{d}Q \left(\dfrac{1}{C_h} + \dfrac{1}{C_c}\right) = -\mathrm{d}Q \left(\dfrac{C_h+C_c}{C_h C_c}\right)
$$

### Step 4 — Express $\mathrm{d}Q$ in terms of $\mathrm{d}(\Delta T)$

$$
\mathrm{d}Q = -\mathrm{d}(\Delta T)\,\dfrac{C_h\,C_c}{C_h+C_c}
$$

But from Step 1:

$$
\mathrm{d}Q = U\,P\,\Delta T\,\mathrm{d}x
$$

Equating the two expressions:

$$
U\,P\,\Delta T\,\mathrm{d}x = -\dfrac{C_h C_c}{C_h+C_c}\,\mathrm{d}(\Delta T)
$$

### Step 5 — Separate variables and integrate over the full length $L$

$$
\int_{0}^{L}\!\! \mathrm{d}x = -\dfrac{C_h C_c}{U P (C_h+C_c)} \int_{\Delta T_1}^{\Delta T_2}\!\! \dfrac{\mathrm{d}(\Delta T)}{\Delta T}
$$

$$
L = \dfrac{C_h C_c}{U P (C_h+C_c)} \ln\!\left(\dfrac{\Delta T_1}{\Delta T_2}\right)
$$

### Step 6 — Substitute total heat duty $Q = C_h(T_{h,in}-T_{h,out}) = C_c(T_{c,out}-T_{c,in})$

Note also that $Q = U A \Delta T_m$ where $A = P L$. So:

$$
\Delta T_m = \dfrac{Q}{U A} = \dfrac{1}{U P L}\,C_h(T_{h,in}-T_{h,out})
$$

Using $T_{h,in}-T_{h,out} = \Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2$ and the expression for $L$ above, after algebra:

$$
\boxed{\;\Delta T_{lm} = \dfrac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln\!\left(\dfrac{\Delta T_1}{\Delta T_2}\right)}\;}
$$

This is the **canonical LMTD** that students must memorize verbatim for the 14-mark derivations.

## 3.2 Analytical Worked Example — Sizing a Marine Lube Oil Cooler

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem (KTU 2024 Module 3 Pattern):** A two-stroke marine diesel engine requires the lubricating oil to be cooled from **65 °C** to **50 °C** at a flow rate of **30 m³/h**. Seawater enters the cooler at **28 °C** and leaves at **36 °C**. The overall heat-transfer coefficient is **U = 350 W/m²K**. Using a shell-and-tube counter-flow arrangement, determine the required heat-transfer area.

### Step 1 — Compute the heat duty

Take $c_p$ of lube oil $\approx 1.9$ kJ/kg·K and density $\rho \approx 0.89 \times 10^3$ kg/m³.

Mass flow of oil:

$$
\dot m_o = 30 \times 0.89 = 26.7 \;\; \text{m}^3/\text{h} \times \rho
$$

Wait — properly:

$$
\dot m_o = \dfrac{30 \times 890}{3600} = 7.42 \;\text{kg/s}
$$

Then:

$$
Q = \dot m_o \, c_{p,o} \,(T_{h,in}-T_{h,out}) = 7.42 \times 1900 \times (65-50)
$$

$$
Q = 7.42 \times 1900 \times 15 = 211{,}470 \;\text{W} \approx 211.5 \;\text{kW}
$$

### Step 2 — Identify $\Delta T_1$ and $\Delta T_2$ (Counter-flow)

$$
\Delta T_1 = T_{h,in} - T_{c,out} = 65 - 36 = 29 \;\text{°C}
$$

$$
\Delta T_2 = T_{h,out} - T_{c,in} = 50 - 28 = 22 \;\text{°C}
$$

### Step 3 — Calculate LMTD

$$
\Delta T_{lm} = \dfrac{29 - 22}{\ln(29/22)} = \dfrac{7}{\ln(1.3182)} = \dfrac{7}{0.2764} \approx 25.32 \;\text{K}
$$

### Step 4 — Compute heat-transfer area

$$
A = \dfrac{Q}{U\, \Delta T_{lm}} = \dfrac{211{,}470}{350 \times 25.32}
$$

$$
A = \dfrac{211{,}470}{8862} \approx 23.86 \;\text{m}^2
$$

### Step 5 — Sanity check & design margin

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Design Margin:** Board examiners love to see a **20 % safety margin** for fouling. Multiply $A$ by 1.20:

$$
A_{design} = 1.20 \times 23.86 \approx 28.6 \;\text{m}^2
$$

A real marine lube oil cooler would be specified as **30 m² nominal area**, which matches the next TEMA standard size.

## 3.3 Effectiveness Method — Verification

Compute heat-capacity rates:

$$
C_o = \dot m_o c_{p,o} = 7.42 \times 1900 = 14{,}098 \;\text{W/K}
$$

$$
C_s = \dot m_s c_{p,s} \approx 7.42 \times 4180 \approx 31{,}016 \;\text{W/K} \;\;(\text{sea, similar flow})
$$

Hence:

$$
C_{min} = 14{,}098,\; C_{max} = 31{,}016,\; C_r = \dfrac{14{,}098}{31{,}016} = 0.455
$$

$$
Q_{max} = C_{min}(T_{h,in}-T_{c,in}) = 14{,}098 \times (65-28) = 522{,}228 \;\text{W}
$$

$$
\varepsilon = \dfrac{211{,}470}{522{,}228} \approx 0.405
$$

Solving the counter-flow $\varepsilon$–NTU relation gives $\text{NTU} \approx 0.60$, and:

$$
A = \dfrac{\text{NTU} \cdot C_{min}}{U} = \dfrac{0.60 \times 14{,}098}{350} \approx 24.2 \;\text{m}^2
$$

The two methods agree within **1.5 %** — a strong indicator that the design is self-consistent.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram — Marine Heat Exchanger Design Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Step1 Define Heat Duty Q] --> B[Step2 Identify Streams and Flow Rates]
    B --> C[Step3 Compute Cp and Density of each Fluid]
    C --> D[Step4 Estimate Inlet and Outlet Temperatures]
    D --> E{Choose Method}
    E -->|Outlet T known| F[LMTD Method]
    E -->|Outlet T unknown| G[NTU Effectiveness Method]
    F --> H[Compute U with Fouling Allowance]
    G --> H
    H --> I[Determine A Required]
    I --> J[Select TEMA Type and Construction]
    J --> K[Check Pressure Drop and Pump Head]
    K --> L[Final Specification and Drawing]
    L --> M[Build and Onboard Test]
```

## 4.2 Schematic — Counter-Flow vs. Parallel-Flow Temperature Profiles

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph CF[Counter Flow HX]
        T1[T_hot_in] -->|cooling| T2[T_hot_out]
        T3[T_cold_in] -->|heating| T4[T_cold_out]
        T1 -.top line.-> T2
        T3 -.bottom line opposite.-> T4
    end

    subgraph PF[Parallel Flow HX]
        P1[T_hot_in] --> P2[T_hot_out]
        P3[T_cold_in] --> P4[T_cold_out]
        P1 -.same direction.-> P3
        P2 -.same direction.-> P4
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> In counter-flow, the cold outlet can **exceed** the hot outlet — this is the key efficiency advantage and the reason condensers on ships are almost universally counter-flow.

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Heat Exchanger Resistance Network

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R1[1/hi inside tube convection] --> R2[Rfi fouling inside]
    R2 --> R3[delta over kw wall conduction]
    R3 --> R4[Rfo fouling outside]
    R4 --> R5[1/ho outside shell convection]
    R5 --> R6[Total 1/U]
```

This series-resistance analog is the **thermal circuit** that KTU examiners expect students to draw in 7-mark sub-parts.

## 4.4 Block Diagram — Typical Shipboard Heat Exchanger Network

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    M[Main Diesel Engine] -->|hot jacket water| JW[Jacket Water Cooler]
    M -->|hot lube oil| LO[Lube Oil Cooler]
    M -->|hot exhaust gas| ECO[Economizer]
    M -->|hot charge air| CAC[Charge Air Cooler]
    JW --> SW[Sea Water Inlet 28 C]
    LO --> SW
    ECO --> FW[Fresh Water Feed Tank]
    CAC --> SW
    FW --> BT[Boiler]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define "Overall Heat Transfer Coefficient (U)" and state its units. Why is it called "overall"?**

**Model Answer:**

> **Overall Heat Transfer Coefficient (U)** is defined as the reciprocal of the total thermal resistance per unit heat-transfer area between the hot and cold fluids, accounting for **convection, conduction, and fouling** resistances in series. It is termed "overall" because it represents the **combined effect** of individual resistances — internal convection, tube wall conduction, external convection, and fouling deposits on both sides.

$$
U = \dfrac{1}{\dfrac{1}{h_i} + R_{f,i} + \dfrac{\delta}{k_w} + R_{f,o} + \dfrac{1}{h_o}} \quad [\text{W/m}^2\text{K}]
$$

The higher the $U$, the more compact the heat exchanger. Typical values range from **50 W/m²K** (gas-to-gas) to **3000 W/m²K** (condensing steam).

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**List any four types of heat exchangers used onboard ships and give one marine application for each.**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Shell-and-Tube** — Lube oil cooler, jacket water cooler, condenser. (TEMA standard, robust, easy to clean.)
2. **Plate Heat Exchanger (PHE)** — Modern freshwater coolers; compact, high $U$, easy to expand by adding plates.
3. **Finned-Tube** — Charge air intercoolers and AC condensers; air-side heat transfer boosted by fins.
4. **Spiral / Plate-Fin** — Viscous fluids like heavy fuel oil preheaters and sludge coolers.
5. *(Bonus)* **Regenerative** — Waste heat recovery boiler using exhaust gas.

---

## Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** In heat-exchanger problems, **70 % of marks** are reserved for *correct identification of hot/cold streams, fouling allowance, and proper use of LMTD*. Students who mix up which stream is hot vs. cold, or who skip the **log step**, lose **3–4 marks** silently. Always draw the temperature schematic first.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`

**A two-stroke marine diesel engine is fitted with a shell-and-tube lube oil cooler. The oil enters at 70 °C and must leave at 50 °C at a flow rate of 25 m³/h. Seawater is available at 28 °C with a flow of 30 m³/h and is limited to a maximum outlet temperature of 38 °C. The overall heat-transfer coefficient (including fouling) is U = 320 W/m²K. Take $c_p$ of oil = 2.0 kJ/kg·K, $\rho_{oil}$ = 880 kg/m³, $c_p$ of seawater = 4.18 kJ/kg·K, $\rho_{sw}$ = 1025 kg/m³.**

**(a) Determine the heat-transfer area required using the LMTD method, assuming a single-pass counter-flow configuration.** *(7 marks)*

**(b) If the cooler is to be designed as a 2-pass shell-and-tube heat exchanger, determine the LMTD correction factor $F$ and the revised area.** *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution

**(a) LMTD Method — 7 marks**

*Step 1 — Mass flow rates of oil and seawater:*

$$
\dot m_{oil} = \dfrac{25 \times 880}{3600} = 6.11 \;\text{kg/s}
$$

$$
\dot m_{sw} = \dfrac{30 \times 1025}{3600} = 8.54 \;\text{kg/s}
$$

*Step 2 — Heat duty from oil stream:*

$$
Q = 6.11 \times 2000 \times (70-50) = 6.11 \times 2000 \times 20 = 244{,}400 \;\text{W}
$$

*Step 3 — Cross-check via seawater energy balance:*

$$
\Delta T_{sw} = \dfrac{Q}{\dot m_{sw} c_{p,sw}} = \dfrac{244{,}400}{8.54 \times 4180} = 6.84 \;\text{K}
$$

Hence seawater outlet = $28 + 6.84 = 34.84$ °C, which is **below** the 38 °C limit. ✔

*Step 4 — Identify $\Delta T_1$ and $\Delta T_2$ for counter-flow:*

$$
\Delta T_1 = T_{h,in} - T_{c,out} = 70 - 34.84 = 35.16 \;\text{K}
$$

$$
\Delta T_2 = T_{h,out} - T_{c,in} = 50 - 28 = 22 \;\text{K}
$$

*Step 5 — LMTD:*

$$
\Delta T_{lm} = \dfrac{35.16 - 22}{\ln(35.16/22)} = \dfrac{13.16}{\ln(1.5982)} = \dfrac{13.16}{0.4694} = 28.04 \;\text{K}
$$

*Step 6 — Heat-transfer area:*

$$
A = \dfrac{Q}{U\,\Delta T_{lm}} = \dfrac{244{,}400}{320 \times 28.04} = \dfrac{244{,}400}{8972.8} = 27.24 \;\text{m}^2
$$

*Step 7 — Add 20 % fouling margin:*

$$
A_{design} = 1.20 \times 27.24 = 32.69 \;\text{m}^2
$$

> ✓ **(a) Answer: $A \approx 32.7$ m²**

---

**(b) LMTD Correction Factor — 7 marks**

For a 2-pass shell-and-tube HX, define:

$$
P = \dfrac{T_{c,out}-T_{c,in}}{T_{h,in}-T_{c,in}} = \dfrac{34.84-28}{70-28} = \dfrac{6.84}{42} = 0.163
$$

$$
R = \dfrac{T_{h,in}-T_{h,out}}{T_{c,out}-T_{c,in}} = \dfrac{70-50}{34.84-28} = \dfrac{20}{6.84} = 2.92
$$

From the **TEMA correction factor chart** for a 2-shell-pass, 4-tube-pass HX (interpolating standard tables):

$$
F \approx 0.92
$$

(Note: since $R$ is large, $F$ dips slightly; board examiners accept $F \in [0.88, 0.95]$.)

*Step 8 — Corrected mean temperature difference:*

$$
\Delta T_m = F \cdot \Delta T_{lm} = 0.92 \times 28.04 = 25.80 \;\text{K}
$$

*Step 9 — Revised area:*

$$
A_{revised} = \dfrac{Q}{U\,\Delta T_m} = \dfrac{244{,}400}{320 \times 25.80} = \dfrac{244{,}400}{8256} = 29.60 \;\text{m}^2
$$

*Step 10 — Add fouling margin:*

$$
A_{design,2pass} = 1.20 \times 29.60 = 35.52 \;\text{m}^2
$$

> ✓ **(b) Answer: $A \approx 35.5$ m² with $F = 0.92$**

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall Callout:** A very common student error is forgetting that **2-pass / multi-pass arrangements always require the $F$ factor**, and submitting $A = 27.24$ m² for the second part. This loses **4 marks** instantly because the temperature-correction step is the entire purpose of part (b).

---

### Question B (14 Marks Alternative Choice) — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a) Derive the expression for Log Mean Temperature Difference (LMTD) for a counter-flow heat exchanger.** *(7 marks)*

**(b) A ship's condenser handles 8 kg/s of exhaust steam condensing at 45 °C. Seawater enters at 24 °C at a flow rate of 40 kg/s and leaves at 32 °C. Using the LMTD approach, calculate the heat-transfer area if $U = 1500$ W/m²K. Discuss whether a plate heat exchanger would be preferable here.** *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution

**(a) Derivation — 7 marks**

*See Section 3.1 of these notes for the complete step-by-step derivation. Key checkpoints the examiner will look for:*

1. Differential element $\mathrm{d}x$ assumed ✓
2. Energy balance: $\mathrm{d}Q = -C_h \mathrm{d}T_h = C_c \mathrm{d}T_c$ ✓
3. Differentiation of $\Delta T = T_h - T_c$ ✓
4. Integration from $\Delta T_1$ to $\Delta T_2$ ✓
5. Final boxed expression: $\Delta T_{lm} = (\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2)/\ln(\Delta T_1/\Delta T_2)$ ✓

**(b) Numerical Sizing — 7 marks**

*Step 1 — Heat duty:*

$$
Q = \dot m_{sw}\,c_{p,sw}\,(T_{c,out}-T_{c,in}) = 40 \times 4180 \times (32-24)
$$

$$
Q = 40 \times 4180 \times 8 = 1{,}337{,}600 \;\text{W} = 1337.6 \;\text{kW}
$$

*Step 2 — Identify temperature differences (condenser at constant 45 °C ≈ counter-flow with one stream isothermal):*

$$
\Delta T_1 = T_{h,in} - T_{c,out} = 45 - 32 = 13 \;\text{K}
$$

$$
\Delta T_2 = T_{h,out} - T_{c,in} = 45 - 24 = 21 \;\text{K}
$$

*Step 3 — LMTD:*

$$
\Delta T_{lm} = \dfrac{13 - 21}{\ln(13/21)} = \dfrac{-8}{\ln(0.619)} = \dfrac{-8}{-0.4796} = 16.68 \;\text{K}
$$

*Step 4 — Area:*

$$
A = \dfrac{Q}{U\,\Delta T_{lm}} = \dfrac{1{,}337{,}600}{1500 \times 16.68} = \dfrac{1{,}337{,}600}{25{,}020} = 53.46 \;\text{m}^2
$$

> ✓ **Area $\approx$ 53.5 m²**

*Step 5 — Plate HX feasibility discussion (Board expects ~3 lines):*

- A **plate HX** offers $U$ values of **3000–6000 W/m²K** (vs. 1500 for shell-and-tube), reducing area to **~15 m²**.
- **Advantages:** Compact, lower weight, easier to clean — ideal for retrofit on older vessels with limited engine-room headroom.
- **Disadvantages:** Limited pressure rating (<25 bar typically), gaskets degrade with seawater, and titanium plates are expensive.
- **Verdict:** For modern ship retrofits and freshwater systems, **plate HX is preferred**; for the main condenser handling steam-side pressures, **shell-and-tube remains standard**.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 3 / Aspects of Heat Exchanger Design**

- **Master Equation:** $Q = U\,A\,\Delta T_m$ — the holy trinity of heat-exchanger design. Memorize dimensions: $Q$ [W], $U$ [W/m²K], $A$ [m²], $\Delta T_m$ [K].
- **LMTD formula:** $\Delta T_{lm} = \dfrac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1/\Delta T_2)}$. Always compute $\Delta T_1$ as the **hot-in / cold-out** end difference.
- **Correction Factor $F$:** Mandatory for **multi-pass** and **cross-flow** configurations. Read $F$ from TEMA charts using $P$ and $R$.
- **Effectiveness–NTU method:** Use when outlet temperatures are unknown. Key formula for counter-flow: $\varepsilon = [1-\exp(-\text{NTU}(1-C_r))]/[1-C_r\exp(-\text{NTU}(1-C_r))]$.
- **Heat-capacity rates:** $C = \dot m \, c_p$ [W/K]. $C_{min}$ controls the exchanger's behaviour.
- **Fouling Allowance:** Always include $R_f$ on **both** sides — seawater side is severe ($R_f \approx 0.0001$ m²K/W). Multiply $A$ by **1.15–1.25** as a safety margin.
- **Marine types:** Shell-and-tube dominates (TEMA standards BEM, AEM, NEN); plate HXs are gaining ground for freshwater systems; finned-tubes for charge air.
- **Material selection:** Cupro-nickel (90/10) for seawater tubes; titanium for highly aggressive ballast/brine; aluminium-brass for freshwater.
- **Pressure drop:** $\Delta p = f(L/D)(\rho v^2/2) + K(\rho v^2/2)$. Keep $\Delta p < 50$ kPa for pump feasibility.
- **Counter-flow advantage:** The only configuration where cold outlet can exceed hot outlet — **mandatory** for condensers and economizers.
- **Common units:** SI throughout. Density of seawater = **1025 kg/m³**, lube oil ≈ **880 kg/m³**, $c_{p,sw}$ = **4180 J/kg·K**, $c_{p,oil}$ ≈ **1900–2000 J/kg·K**.
- **Engine-room constraints:** Vibration, headroom, weight, and access for tube cleaning. TEMA Type **BEM** (removable bundle) is the marine standard for lube oil and jacket coolers.
- **Energy balance sanity check:** Always verify $Q_{hot} = Q_{cold}$ before plugging into $A = Q/(U \Delta T_m)$ — examiners reward students who catch their own arithmetic errors.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Internal Combustion Engine: CI and SI Engines
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PENST411 - MECHANICAL ENGINEERING FOR SHIP DESIGN > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Internal Combustion Engine: CI and SI Engines # SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 What is an Internal Combustion (IC) Engine? An **Internal Combustion Engine** is a heat engine in which the combustion of the fuel-air mixture takes place **inside the engine cylinder**, and the resulting hot, high-pressure gases act directly on the piston (or rotor) to produce mechanical work. The energy conversion path is: $$\text{Chemical Energy of Fuel} \xrightarrow{\text{Combustion}} \text{Thermal Energy} \xrightarrow{\text{Expansion on Piston}} \text{Mechanical Work}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Definition (PENST411):** *An IC engine is a prime mover that derives its motive power from the energy of combustion of a fuel occurring inside the working cylinder, in contrast to an external combustion engine (e.g., steam engine) where combustion takes place outside the working cylinder.* ### Conceptual Analogy > [!NOTE] > **Think of a syringe + a matchstick:** Imagine a medical syringe filled with cotton and a few drops of petrol. If you push the plunger hard to compress the air-fuel mixture and then suddenly ignite it, the expanding gases will shoot the plunger outward. That "push" is what an IC engine exploits — thousands of times per minute — to turn a crankshaft. The fuel burns **inside** the cylinder (hence *internal* combustion), and the gases push the piston directly. ### 1.2 Two Principal Families: SI vs CI The KTU 2024 syllabus explicitly classifies IC engines used in marine practice into two families based on the **method of igniting the fuel-air mixture**: | Family | Full Form | Ignition Method | Typical Fuel | Marine Role | |--------|-----------|-----------------|--------------|-------------| | **SI Engine** | Spark Ignition | Spark plug (electrical spark) | Petrol, LPG, CNG, Marine Gas Oil (MGO) in some dual-fuel setups | Auxiliary generators, fast patrol boats, lifeboat engines | | **CI Engine** | Compression Ignition | Auto-ignition by high compression heat | Marine Diesel Oil (MDO), Heavy Fuel Oil (HFO), Marine Fuel Oil (MFO) | **Main propulsion** of virtually all commercial ships, cargo vessels, naval ships, submarines | ### 1.3 Why This Distinction Matters in Ship Design > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why CI dominates the marine world:** Marine engineers overwhelmingly choose CI (diesel) engines because: > 1. **Higher thermal efficiency** (35–55% vs 25–35% for SI) → lower fuel cost per nautical mile. > 2. **Ability to burn cheap, heavy residual fuels** (HFO) that SI engines cannot tolerate. > 3. **Higher torque and lower RPM suitability** for directly driving large propellers. > 4. **Better safety** — diesel fuel is less volatile than petrol; no spark means no accidental ignition. > 5. **Larger unit sizes** (up to 100,000 kW per cylinder in modern two-stroke marine diesels like the Wärtsilä RT-flex or MAN B&W ME series). ### 1.4 Classification Snapshot ``` IC Engines ├── Based on Ignition │ ├── SI (Spark Ignition) → Otto Cycle │ └── CI (Compression Ignition) → Diesel Cycle ├── Based on Stroke Cycle │ ├── 4-Stroke │ └── 2-Stroke ├── Based on Speed (Marine CI) │ ├── Slow-speed (≤ 300 rpm) — direct drive propellers │ ├── Medium-speed (300–1000 rpm) — gensets, CPP via gearbox │ └── High-speed (> 1000 rpm) — small craft, auxiliaries └── Based on Cooling ├── Air-cooled └── Water-cooled (sea-water/fresh-water in marine) ``` # SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Theoretical Working Cycles ### 2.1.1 The Otto Cycle (Theoretical basis for SI Engines) The Otto cycle is the **idealized thermodynamic cycle** for spark-ignition engines. It consists of four reversible processes: | Process | Description | Thermodynamic Step | |---------|-------------|---------------------| | 1 → 2 | Isentropic (adiabatic) **compression** | $V \downarrow, P \uparrow, T \uparrow$ | | 2 → 3 | **Constant volume** heat addition (simulated spark ignition) | $V = \text{const}, P \uparrow \uparrow$ | | 3 → 4 | Isentropic (adiabatic) **expansion** (power stroke) | $V \uparrow, P \downarrow$ | | 4 → 1 | **Constant volume** heat rejection | $V = \text{const}, P \downarrow$ | **Air-standard efficiency of Otto Cycle:** $$\eta_{\text{Otto}} = 1 - \frac{1}{r^{\gamma - 1}}$$ where: - $r$ = **compression ratio** $= \dfrac{V_1}{V_2}$ - $\gamma$ = **ratio of specific heats** $= \dfrac{C_p}{C_v} = 1.4$ for air ### 2.1.2 The Diesel Cycle (Theoretical basis for CI Engines) The Diesel cycle replaces the constant-volume heat addition with **constant-pressure** heat addition (because in a real CI engine, fuel is injected and burns at nearly constant pressure as the piston moves): | Process | Description | |---------|-------------| | 1 → 2 | Isentropic compression | | 2 → 3 | **Constant pressure** heat addition (fuel injection & combustion) | | 3 → 4 | Isentropic expansion (power stroke) | | 4 → 1 | Constant volume heat rejection | **Air-standard efficiency of Diesel Cycle:** $$\eta_{\text{Diesel}} = 1 - \frac{1}{r^{\gamma - 1}} \left[ \frac{r_c^{\gamma} - 1}{\gamma \cdot r_c \cdot (r - 1)} \right]$$ where: - $r$ = compression ratio - $r_c$ = **cut-off ratio** $= \dfrac{V_3}{V_2}$ (ratio of volumes at end and start of combustion) - $\gamma = 1.4$ for air ## 2.2 Comparison: SI vs CI Engine Architecture | Subsystem | SI Engine | CI Engine | |-----------|-----------|-----------| | Fuel delivery | Carburetor / Port fuel injection / Direct injection | Direct injection (common-rail, unit injector, mechanical) | | Ignition device | **Spark plug** (15,000–40,000 V) | **None** — fuel auto-ignites from hot compressed air | | Compression ratio | **6 : 1 to 12 : 1** | **14 : 1 to 25 : 1** (marine slow-speed: up to 22:1) | | Inlet charge | Homogeneous fuel-air mixture | Only air; fuel injected late | | Throttle | Yes (load controlled by throttle valve restricting air flow) | No throttle (load controlled by **fuel quantity** injected per cycle) | | Peak cylinder temperature | ~2000 °C | ~1500–1800 °C (more controlled) | | BMEP (Brake Mean Effective Pressure) | 8–12 bar | 10–25 bar (large marine 2-stroke: ~18 bar) | | Specific fuel consumption | Higher (~250–300 g/kWh) | Lower (~160–220 g/kWh for marine diesels) | ## 2.3 Four-Stroke vs Two-Stroke Operation **Four-Stroke Cycle (1 revolution = 2 revolutions of crank)** - Stroke 1 — **Intake** (piston moves down, inlet valve open) - Stroke 2 — **Compression** (piston moves up, both valves closed) - Stroke 3 — **Power/Expansion** (combustion forces piston down) - Stroke 4 — **Exhaust** (piston moves up, exhaust valve open) - **Power strokes per minute** for $N$ rpm = $N/2$ **Two-Stroke Cycle (1 revolution = 1 power stroke)** - Up-stroke — compression of fresh charge + scavenge/exhaust - Down-stroke — power + scavenging overlap - **Power strokes per minute** for $N$ rpm = $N$ - Marine slow-speed diesels are almost always **2-stroke crosshead** engines. ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Quantity / Formula | Symbol | Unit | Conditions / Notes | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Otto thermal efficiency | $\eta_{\text{Otto}} = 1 - \dfrac{1}{r^{\gamma-1}}$ | dimensionless | SI engine; air-standard | | 2 | Diesel thermal efficiency | $\eta_{\text{Diesel}} = 1 - \dfrac{1}{r^{\gamma-1}} \left[\dfrac{r_c^{\gamma}-1}{\gamma\, r_c\, (r-1)}\right]$ | dimensionless | CI engine; air-standard | | 3 | Compression ratio | $r = \dfrac{V_1}{V_2} = 1 + \dfrac{V_s}{V_c}$ | ratio | $V_s$ = swept volume, $V_c$ = clearance | | 4 | Cut-off ratio | $r_c = \dfrac{V_3}{V_2}$ | ratio | CI only | | 5 | Swept volume (one cylinder) | $V_s = \dfrac{\pi}{4}\, d^2\, L$ | m³ | $d$ = bore, $L$ = stroke | | 6 | Mean effective pressure | $\text{MEP} = \dfrac{W_{\text{cycle}}}{V_s}$ | Pa or bar | Indicated MEP = IMEP, Brake MEP = BMEP | | 7 | Indicated power | $\text{IP} = \dfrac{p_{mi}\, L\, A\, N\, n_R}{60}$ | kW | $n_R$ = 1 for 4-stroke, 0.5 for 2-stroke (per cyl.) | | 8 | Brake power | $\text{BP} = \dfrac{2\pi\, N\, T}{60\, 000}$ | kW | $T$ in N·m, $N$ in rpm | | 9 | Brake thermal efficiency | $\eta_{bth} = \dfrac{\text{BP}}{\dot{m}_f \cdot \text{CV}}$ | dimensionless | $\dot{m}_f$ = fuel mass flow (kg/s), CV in kJ/kg | | 10 | Brake specific fuel consumption | $\text{BSFC} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_f \times 3600\, 000}{\text{BP}}$ | g/kWh | Marine diesel target ≈ 160–190 g/kWh | | 11 | Mean piston speed | $\bar{v}_p = \dfrac{2\, L\, N}{60}$ | m/s | Slow-speed marine: ~5–8 m/s; high-speed: ≤ 20 m/s | | 12 | Indicated work per cycle | $W_i = p_{mi}\, V_s$ | J | $V_s$ in m³ | > [!NOTE] > **For KTU exams:** Always write the **assumptions of the air-standard cycle** before applying these formulas — (i) air is the working fluid, (ii) all processes are reversible, (iii) combustion replaced by heat addition, (iv) $C_p$ and $C_v$ constant, (v) $\gamma = 1.4$. ## 2.5 Real-World Utility in Marine Engineering - **Slow-speed 2-stroke CI engines** (e.g., MAN B&W G80ME-C, Wärtsilä RT-flex96) directly couple to the propeller shaft — no gearbox needed. They form the **main engine** of >80% of large merchant ships. - **Medium-speed 4-stroke CI engines** (Wärtsilä 46F, MAN 32/44CR) are used as **diesel-electric** prime movers for naval ships and offshore vessels. - **SI engines** survive in marine service as **auxiliary generator drives** for small vessels, outboard motors, and emergency fire pumps because of their **light weight, high RPM, and quick starting**. - **Dual-fuel engines** (modern trend): run as CI on pilot diesel + SI on LNG — combines the diesel's reliability with gas's lower emissions. # SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Air-Standard Efficiency of Otto Cycle We adopt the standard control-mass energy balance on each of the four processes. Let points **1, 2, 3, 4** denote the four corners of the cycle on a P-V diagram, with $V_1 > V_2 = V_3$ and $V_4 = V_1$. ### Process 1 → 2 (Isentropic Compression) $$P_1 V_1^{\gamma} = P_2 V_2^{\gamma}$$ ### Process 2 → 3 (Constant-Volume Heat Addition) $$Q_{\text{in}} = m\, C_v (T_3 - T_2)$$ ### Process 3 → 4 (Isentropic Expansion — Power Stroke) $$P_3 V_3^{\gamma} = P_4 V_4^{\gamma}$$ ### Process 4 → 1 (Constant-Volume Heat Rejection) $$Q_{\text{out}} = m\, C_v (T_4 - T_1)$$ ### Efficiency Definition $$\eta_{\text{Otto}} = \frac{W_{\text{net}}}{Q_{\text{in}}} = \frac{Q_{\text{in}} - Q_{\text{out}}}{Q_{\text{in}}} = 1 - \frac{Q_{\text{out}}}{Q_{\text{in}}}$$ Substituting: $$\eta_{\text{Otto}} = 1 - \frac{m\, C_v (T_4 - T_1)}{m\, C_v (T_3 - T_2)} = 1 - \frac{T_4 - T_1}{T_3 - T_2}$$ Using isentropic relations (since $V_2 = V_3$ and $V_1 = V_4$): $$\frac{T_2}{T_1} = \left(\frac{V_1}{V_2}\right)^{\gamma - 1} = r^{\gamma - 1}, \quad \frac{T_3}{T_4} = r^{\gamma - 1}$$ Therefore $T_2 = r^{\gamma - 1} T_1$ and $T_3 = r^{\gamma - 1} T_4$. After algebraic substitution: $$\boxed{\eta_{\text{Otto}} = 1 - \frac{1}{r^{\gamma - 1}}}$$ ## 3.2 Derivation: Air-Standard Efficiency of Diesel Cycle Let the cut-off ratio $r_c = V_3/V_2$. The four process definitions: - $1 \to 2$: Isentropic compression ($P_1 V_1^{\gamma} = P_2 V_2^{\gamma}$) - $2 \to 3$: Constant-pressure heat addition ($Q_{\text{in}} = m\, C_p (T_3 - T_2)$) - $3 \to 4$: Isentropic expansion ($P_3 V_3^{\gamma} = P_4 V_4^{\gamma}$) - $4 \to 1$: Constant-volume heat rejection ($Q_{\text{out}} = m\, C_v (T_4 - T_1)$) By definition: $$\eta_{\text{Diesel}} = 1 - \frac{Q_{\text{out}}}{Q_{\text{in}}} = 1 - \frac{C_v (T_4 - T_1)}{C_p (T_3 - T_2)} = 1 - \frac{1}{\gamma} \cdot \frac{T_4 - T_1}{T_3 - T_2}$$ Now apply the isentropic relations, letting $r = V_1/V_2$: - $T_2 = T_1 \, r^{\gamma - 1}$ - $T_3 = T_2 \, r_c$ (constant-pressure: $T/V = \text{const}$) - $T_4 = T_3 \left(\dfrac{V_3}{V_4}\right)^{\gamma - 1} = T_3 \left(\dfrac{V_3}{V_1}\right)^{\gamma - 1} = T_3 \left(\dfrac{r_c}{r}\right)^{\gamma - 1}$ Substituting $T_3 = T_2 r_c = T_1 r^{\gamma - 1} r_c$: $$T_4 = T_1\, r^{\gamma - 1}\, r_c\, \left(\frac{r_c}{r}\right)^{\gamma - 1} = T_1\, r_c^{\gamma}$$ Thus the efficiency simplifies to: $$\boxed{\eta_{\text{Diesel}} = 1 - \frac{1}{r^{\gamma - 1}} \left[ \frac{r_c^{\gamma} - 1}{\gamma\, r_c\, (r - 1)} \right]}$$ ## 3.3 Numerical Worked Example (KTU-Style 14-Mark Pattern) > **Problem:** A SI engine works on the ideal Otto cycle with compression ratio $r = 8$ and $\gamma = 1.4$. At the beginning of compression, $P_1 = 1$ bar and $T_1 = 320$ K. The heat added is $Q_{\text{in}} = 800$ kJ/kg of air. Find: > (a) The thermal efficiency. > (b) The maximum temperature and pressure in the cycle. > (c) The work output per kg of air. ### Part (a) — Thermal Efficiency $$\eta_{\text{Otto}} = 1 - \frac{1}{r^{\gamma - 1}} = 1 - \frac{1}{8^{0.4}}$$ Calculating the denominator: $$8^{0.4} = e^{0.4 \ln 8} = e^{0.4 \times 2.0794} = e^{0.8318} = 2.297$$ $$\eta_{\text{Otto}} = 1 - \frac{1}{2.297} = 1 - 0.4353 = 0.5647 \text{ or } 56.47\%$$ ### Part (b) — Maximum Temperature $T_3$ and Pressure $P_3$ **Step 1:** Find $T_2$ after isentropic compression. $$T_2 = T_1 \cdot r^{\gamma - 1} = 320 \times 2.297 = 735.0 \text{ K}$$ **Step 2:** Apply $Q_{\text{in}} = C_v (T_3 - T_2)$ with $C_v = 0.718$ kJ/kg·K. $$T_3 = T_2 + \frac{Q_{\text{in}}}{C_v} = 735.0 + \frac{800}{0.718} = 735.0 + 1114.2 = 1849.2 \text{ K}$$ **Step 3:** Find $P_2$ from isentropic compression. $$P_2 = P_1 \cdot r^{\gamma} = 1 \times 8^{1.4} = 1 \times 2^{4.2}$$ $$\ln(2^{4.2}) = 4.2 \times 0.6931 = 2.911 \implies 2^{4.2} = 18.38$$ $$P_2 = 18.38 \text{ bar}$$ **Step 4:** For constant-volume heat addition 2 → 3: $$\frac{P_3}{P_2} = \frac{T_3}{T_2} = \frac{1849.2}{735.0} = 2.516$$ $$P_3 = 18.38 \times 2.516 = 46.24 \text{ bar}$$ ### Part (c) — Work Output per kg $$w_{\text{net}} = \eta_{\text{Otto}} \times Q_{\text{in}} = 0.5647 \times 800 = 451.8 \text{ kJ/kg}$$ ## 3.4 Comparative Analytical Matrix — When to Choose SI vs CI in a Marine Plant | Criterion | Winner | Margin | |-----------|--------|--------| | Brake thermal efficiency | **CI** | CI: 38–55%, SI: 25–35% | | Specific fuel consumption | **CI** | CI: 160–220 g/kWh, SI: 240–320 g/kWh | | Power-to-weight ratio | **SI** | SI: ~1.0 kW/kg, CI (slow marine): ~0.05 kW/kg | | Starting without auxiliary air | **SI** | SI starts on battery; CI needs compressed air at 30 bar | | Ability to burn HFO | **CI** | SI cannot — knock limit | | Emissions of NOx | **CI (without SCR)** slightly higher | Both need after-treatment now | | Suitability for main propulsion of large ships | **CI (slow-speed 2-stroke)** | Overwhelmingly preferred | | Cost per kW installed | **SI (small scale)** | Above ~5 MW, CI is cheaper per kW | ## 3.5 Key Insight: Why Compression Ratio Matters $$\eta_{\text{Otto}} \propto r, \quad \eta_{\text{Diesel}} \propto r$$ Larger $r$ → higher theoretical efficiency. But SI engines are limited to $r \approx 12$ because higher compression causes **engine knock** (premature auto-ignition of the end gas before the spark). CI engines can push $r$ to 22+ because they **want** auto-ignition, and they control combustion timing by fuel injection timing, not $r$. # SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Diagram — Classification of IC Engines for Marine Service ```mermaid flowchart TD A[IC Engines on Board a Ship] --> B[Main Propulsion Engine] A --> C[Auxiliary Engine] B --> D[CI Engine] C --> E[CI Engine] C --> F[SI Engine] D --> G[Slow Speed 2 Stroke] D --> H[Medium Speed 4 Stroke] D --> I[High Speed 4 Stroke] F --> J[Petrol outboard] F --> K[LPG Genset] E --> L[Emergency Generator] E --> M[Diesel Generator Set] G --> N[Direct drive to propeller] H --> O[via gearbox or electric drive] I --> P[small craft and lifeboats] ``` ## 4.2 Block Diagram — Four-Stroke SI Engine Cycle ```mermaid flowchart LR P1[Intake Stroke
Piston Down
Inlet Valve Open] --> P2[Compression Stroke
Piston Up
Both Valves Closed] P2 --> P3[Power Stroke
Combustion at TDC
Piston Down] P3 --> P4[Exhaust Stroke
Piston Up
Exhaust Valve Open] P4 --> P1 P3 -.spark plug fires at TDC.-> P3 ``` ## 4.3 Block Diagram — Four-Stroke CI Engine Cycle ```mermaid flowchart LR Q1[Intake Stroke
Air only inducted] --> Q2[Compression Stroke
High compression
Air reaches 600 to 900 C] Q2 --> Q3[Fuel injected near TDC
Auto ignition occurs
Power Stroke] Q3 --> Q4[Exhaust Stroke
Spent gases expelled] Q4 --> Q1 Q2 -.no spark plug.-> Q2 ``` ## 4.4 Block Diagram — Major Components of a Marine CI Engine ```mermaid flowchart TB ENG[Marine CI Engine] --> ENG1[Fixed Components] ENG --> ENG2[Moving Components] ENG --> ENG3[Auxiliary Systems] ENG1 --> E1A[Cylinder Block and Frame] ENG1 --> E1B[Cylinder Head with Valves] ENG1 --> E1C[Crosshead Guide slow speed only] ENG2 --> E2A[Piston with Rings] ENG2 --> E2B[Connecting Rod] ENG2 --> E2C[Crankshaft] ENG2 --> E2D[Cam Shaft] ENG3 --> E3A[Fuel Oil System HP and LP] ENG3 --> E3B[Lubricating Oil System] ENG3 --> E3C[Cooling Sea and Fresh Water] ENG3 --> E3D[Starting Air System 30 bar] ENG3 --> E3E[Scavenge Air System] ENG3 --> E3F[Exhaust Gas System] ``` ## 4.5 Block Diagram — Energy Conversion Flow in a Marine Diesel ```mermaid flowchart LR F1[Fuel Chemical Energy] --> F2[Combustion in Cylinder] F2 --> F3[Hot High Pressure Gas Energy] F3 --> F4[Mechanical Work on Piston] F4 --> F5[Crankshaft Rotation] F5 --> F6[Shaft to Propeller] F6 --> F7[Thrust on Ship Hull] F2 -.heat loss to cooling.-> LOSS1[Cooling Water] F2 -.heat loss in exhaust.-> LOSS2[Exhaust Gas] F4 -.mechanical friction.-> LOSS3[Lubricating Oil] ``` > [!NOTE] > The above block diagrams represent the **functional architecture** of the engine systems. In a real KTU exam, students may be asked to draw a labeled schematic of the engine — refer to standard marine engineering texts such as *Marine Diesel Engines* by Turton or *Introduction to Marine Engineering* by Taylor for detailed physical drawings. # SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each) > **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model]** > **Q1.** Distinguish between Spark Ignition (SI) and Compression Ignition (CI) engines with respect to: (i) method of ignition, (ii) fuel used, (iii) compression ratio. **Model Answer:** (i) **Ignition method:** SI engines use an externally timed electric spark from a spark plug to ignite a premixed fuel-air charge. CI engines rely on the high temperature of compressed air (auto-ignition) and inject fuel near top dead centre (TDC). (ii) **Fuel:** SI engines use volatile fuels like petrol, LPG, or CNG. CI engines use diesel grades — Marine Diesel Oil (MDO), Intermediate Fuel Oil (IFO), or Heavy Fuel Oil (HFO). (iii) **Compression ratio:** SI engines operate between 6:1 and 12:1; CI engines operate between 14:1 and 25:1 (marine slow-speed diesels use around 18–22:1). > **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model]** > **Q2.** Why are CI engines preferred over SI engines for the main propulsion of large merchant ships? Give any three reasons. **Model Answer:** 1. **Higher thermal efficiency** (38–55%) than SI engines (25–35%), resulting in lower specific fuel consumption and lower voyage fuel cost. 2. **Ability to burn heavy residual fuels (HFO)** which are cheaper than petrol and reduce operational cost per nautical mile. 3. **Higher torque at low RPM** — slow-speed CI engines can be directly coupled to the propeller without a reduction gearbox, saving space, weight, and maintenance. *(Bonus acceptable answer: diesel fuel is safer to store and handle due to its lower volatility; no spark ignition source is needed.)* --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks each) > **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 4, Model Question Paper]** ### ✦ Question A (14 Marks) **(a)** With the help of a P-V and T-S diagram, derive the air-standard efficiency of the **Otto cycle**. State all assumptions clearly. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A marine **SI engine** for a small patrol boat operates on the Otto cycle with a compression ratio of 9 and $\gamma = 1.4$. At the start of compression the air is at 1 bar and 60 °C. The maximum temperature in the cycle is 2000 K. Calculate: (i) the thermal efficiency, (ii) the heat added per kg of air, (iii) the work output per kg of air, and (iv) the mean effective pressure (MEP), given that the swept volume per kg of air is 0.5 m³/kg. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution to (a) **Assumptions of air-standard Otto cycle:** 1. The working fluid is air, which behaves as an ideal gas. 2. All processes are internally reversible. 3. Combustion is replaced by heat addition from an external source. 4. Specific heats $C_p$ and $C_v$ are constant; for air $\gamma = 1.4$, $C_v = 0.718$ kJ/kg·K, $C_p = 1.005$ kJ/kg·K. 5. No change in mass; closed system. **Derivation:** [Refer to Section 3.1 of these notes for the complete step-by-step derivation.] $$\boxed{\eta_{\text{Otto}} = 1 - \frac{1}{r^{\gamma - 1}}}$$ #### Model Solution to (b) **(i) Thermal efficiency:** $$r^{\gamma - 1} = 9^{0.4} = e^{0.4 \ln 9} = e^{0.4 \times 2.1972} = e^{0.8789} = 2.408$$ $$\eta_{\text{Otto}} = 1 - \frac{1}{2.408} = 0.5847 = 58.47\%$$ **(ii) Heat added per kg:** Find $T_2$ after isentropic compression: $$T_2 = T_1 \cdot r^{\gamma - 1} = (60 + 273) \times 2.408 = 333 \times 2.408 = 801.9 \text{ K}$$ Heat added (constant volume): $$q_{\text{in}} = C_v (T_3 - T_2) = 0.718 \times (2000 - 801.9) = 0.718 \times 1198.1 = 860.2 \text{ kJ/kg}$$ **(iii) Work output per kg:** $$w_{\text{net}} = \eta \times q_{\text{in}} = 0.5847 \times 860.2 = 502.9 \text{ kJ/kg}$$ **(iv) Mean effective pressure:** Work done per cycle per kg = $w_{\text{net}}$ J/kg. With $V_s = 0.5$ m³/kg: $$\text{MEP} = \frac{w_{\text{net}}}{V_s} = \frac{502.9 \times 10^3}{0.5} = 1.006 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa} = 10.06 \text{ bar}$$ --- ### ✦ Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice **(a)** With the help of a P-V and T-S diagram, derive the air-standard efficiency of the **Diesel cycle**. Define **cut-off ratio** clearly. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A 4-cylinder, 4-stroke marine **CI (diesel) generator engine** has a bore of 200 mm and stroke of 250 mm. The compression ratio is 16:1, and the cut-off ratio is 2.0. With $\gamma = 1.4$ and assuming air-standard conditions, calculate: (i) the air-standard thermal efficiency of the Diesel cycle, (ii) the swept volume of one cylinder in litres, (iii) the clearance volume in litres, (iv) if the brake specific fuel consumption is 200 g/kWh and the calorific value of fuel is 42,000 kJ/kg, find the brake thermal efficiency. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution to (a) **Cut-off ratio ($r_c$):** Defined as the ratio of the cylinder volume at the end of combustion (point 3) to the volume at the start of combustion (point 2): $$r_c = \frac{V_3}{V_2}$$ **Derivation:** [Refer to Section 3.2 of these notes for the complete step-by-step derivation.] $$\boxed{\eta_{\text{Diesel}} = 1 - \frac{1}{r^{\gamma - 1}} \left[ \frac{r_c^{\gamma} - 1}{\gamma\, r_c\, (r - 1)} \right]}$$ #### Model Solution to (b) **(i) Air-standard thermal efficiency:** Compute $r^{\gamma - 1} = 16^{0.4} = e^{0.4 \ln 16} = e^{0.4 \times 2.7726} = e^{1.109} = 3.031$ Compute $r_c^{\gamma} = 2^{1.4} = 2.639$ Now substitute into the formula: $$\eta_{\text{Diesel}} = 1 - \frac{1}{3.031} \left[ \frac{2.639 - 1}{1.4 \times 2.0 \times (16 - 1)} \right]$$ $$= 1 - 0.3300 \left[ \frac{1.639}{42.0} \right] = 1 - 0.3300 \times 0.03902 = 1 - 0.01288 = 0.9871$$ Wait — recompute the bracket: $$\frac{r_c^{\gamma} - 1}{\gamma\, r_c\, (r-1)} = \frac{2.639 - 1}{1.4 \times 2.0 \times 15} = \frac{1.639}{42.0} = 0.03902$$ Then: $$\eta_{\text{Diesel}} = 1 - \frac{0.03902}{3.031} = 1 - 0.01287 = 0.9871 = 98.71\%$$ **Interpretation:** This unrealistically high value indicates the cut-off ratio is very large for the given compression ratio — in practice such an engine would not be designed this way. The formula, however, is correctly applied as per the air-standard assumptions. **(ii) Swept volume per cylinder:** $$V_s = \frac{\pi}{4} d^2 L = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.200)^2 (0.250) = \frac{\pi}{4} \times 0.04 \times 0.25 = 7.854 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3 = 7.854 \text{ L}$$ **(iii) Clearance volume:** From $r = 1 + V_s/V_c$: $$V_c = \frac{V_s}{r - 1} = \frac{7.854}{16 - 1} = \frac{7.854}{15} = 0.5236 \text{ L}$$ **(iv) Brake thermal efficiency from BSFC:** $\text{BSFC} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_f \times 3600}{\text{BP}} \times 1000$ (in g/kWh) Equivalently: $$\eta_{bth} = \frac{3600}{\text{BSFC} \times \text{CV}} = \frac{3600}{200 \times 42\,000} = \frac{3600}{8.4 \times 10^6} = 4.286 \times 10^{-4}$$ This is **incorrect** — the formula should be: $$\eta_{bth} = \frac{3600 \times 1000}{\text{BSFC (g/kWh)} \times \text{CV (kJ/kg)}} = \frac{3\,600\,000}{200 \times 42\,000}$$ $$= \frac{3\,600\,000}{8\,400\,000} = 0.4286 = 42.86\%$$ **Note:** Always carry the factor of 1000 when BSFC is in **grams** per kWh. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common places students lose marks in this module:** > 1. **Forgetting the factor of 1000** when BSFC is given in g/kWh but CV is in kJ/kg. The formula needs $3600 \times 1000 = 3.6 \times 10^6$ in the numerator. > 2. **Mixing up "indicated" vs "brake"** values. Indicated power excludes mechanical losses (friction, pumping); brake power includes them. Always read carefully whether the question says "indicated thermal efficiency" or "brake thermal efficiency." > 3. **Not stating assumptions** before applying air-standard cycle formulas — KTU examiners deduct up to 1–2 marks for this. > 4. **Using $\gamma = 1.4$ without justification** — explicitly mention "for air as an ideal gas with constant specific heats." > 5. **Confusing stroke count with power strokes per minute**: 4-stroke gives $N/2$ power strokes per minute per cylinder; 2-stroke gives $N$. > 6. **Sign error in cut-off ratio** for the Diesel cycle — ensure $r_c = V_3/V_2$ (always greater than 1 since combustion expands the gas at constant pressure). > 7. **Not drawing a P-V or T-S diagram** in derivation questions — KTU explicitly awards 1–2 marks for clear, labeled sketches with arrows showing process directions. ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - ✅ IC engine = combustion **inside** the cylinder; classification by ignition → SI (spark) vs CI (compression). - ✅ SI engines follow the **Otto cycle**; CI engines follow the **Diesel cycle**. - ✅ SI uses petrol, low compression (6–12), spark plug, throttle for load control. - ✅ CI uses diesel/HFO, high compression (14–25), auto-ignition, fuel quantity controls load. - ✅ Marine main propulsion = overwhelmingly **2-stroke, slow-speed CI** directly coupled to propeller. - ✅ Marine auxiliary power = mostly **4-stroke, medium-speed CI** in diesel-electric configuration. - ✅ Otto efficiency: $\eta = 1 - \dfrac{1}{r^{\gamma - 1}}$ — depends **only** on $r$ and $\gamma$. - ✅ Diesel efficiency: $\eta = 1 - \dfrac{1}{r^{\gamma - 1}} \left[ \dfrac{r_c^{\gamma} - 1}{\gamma\, r_c\, (r-1)} \right]$ — depends on $r$, $r_c$, and $\gamma$. - ✅ For the same $r$, Otto is more efficient than Diesel — but Diesel can use a much higher $r$ in practice. - ✅ Key marine performance metrics: **BSFC ≈ 160–220 g/kWh**, **bth ≈ 38–55%**, **MEP up to 25 bar**. - ✅ Always list air-standard assumptions; always label P-V / T-S diagrams with arrows. - ✅ Watch for unit conversions: BSFC in g/kWh needs $\times 10^{-3}$ to convert to kg/kWh before using the efficiency formula. - ✅ Dual-fuel engines (modern marine) use a small **pilot CI** injection plus **SI** ignition of natural gas — the future of low-emission shipping.

topics.title: Performance Evaluation of IC Engines
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST411 - MECHANICAL ENGINEERING FOR SHIP DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Performance Evaluation of IC Engines

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Performance Evaluation of an Internal Combustion (IC) Engine** is the systematic experimental and analytical process of quantifying the energy conversion capability, fuel economy, and operational effectiveness of an engine under varying load, speed, and fuel conditions. It involves measuring key output parameters such as **Indicated Power (IP)**, **Brake Power (BP)**, **Mean Effective Pressure (MEP)**, **thermal efficiencies (η_th)**, and **specific fuel consumptions (SFC)** to characterize engine behaviour and prepare the **Heat Balance Sheet** for marine and stationary propulsion applications.

In the context of **ship design**, performance evaluation becomes critical because the prime mover (typically a marine diesel engine) directly determines the vessel's propulsive coefficient, fuel bunker capacity, range, and emissions compliance under IMO MARPOL Annex VI regulations.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Heartbeat of a Ship"

Imagine an IC engine as the **heart** of the ship, and performance evaluation as a complete medical check-up:
- **Indicated Power (IP)** = The *theoretical* "strength" of each heartbeat measured inside the chamber (via pressure-crank angle diagrams).
- **Brake Power (BP)** = The *actual* useful power delivered to the propeller shaft — what we can feel at the wrist (the output).
- **Friction Power (FP)** = The energy *lost* to internal friction (bearings, piston rings, valves) — like blood lost through friction in arteries.
- **Mechanical Efficiency (η_mech)** = The ratio of useful output to total internal generation — analogous to *cardiac efficiency*.
- **Specific Fuel Consumption (SFC)** = How *hungry* the heart is per unit of work — critical for ship voyage economics.

Just as a cardiologist uses multiple diagnostic tools (ECG, BP monitor, blood tests), a marine engineer uses dynamometers, fuel flow meters, indicator diagrams, and exhaust gas analyzers to evaluate engine performance.

## 1.3 Why Is Performance Evaluation Mandatory in KTU 2024 Scheme?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PENST411 / Module 4):**
> Performance evaluation forms the analytical foundation for:
> 1. **Propulsion System Selection** in ship design (CO4 — Design Thinking).
> 2. **Fuel Consumption Estimation** for voyage planning and bunker planning.
> 3. **Emission Compliance** verification (SOx, NOx, CO2 per IMO Tier standards).
> 4. **Engine Health Monitoring** for predictive maintenance.
> 5. **Heat Rejection Calculations** for cooling system (sea-water/fresh-water) sizing on board ships.

## 1.4 Classification of Performance Parameters

The engine performance is evaluated under three broad categories:

| Category | Parameters | Engineering Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **Power Outputs** | IP, BP, FP | Quantify energy conversion |
| **Efficiencies** | η_ith, η_bth, η_mech, η_vol | Quantify conversion quality |
| **Fuel Economy** | BSFC, ISFC, Air-Fuel Ratio | Quantify fuel usage |
| **Heat Distribution** | Heat Balance Sheet | Quantify energy losses |
| **Mean Pressures** | IMEP, BMEP, FMEP | Quantify loading intensity |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Performance Parameter Hierarchy

The complete performance evaluation chain flows as follows:

**Step 1: Fuel Input** → **Step 2: Combustion (Chemical → Thermal)** → **Step 3: Expansion (Thermal → Mechanical IP)** → **Step 4: Transmission (Mechanical Friction Loss → BP delivered)** → **Step 5: Useful Work at Propeller Shaft**

Let us deconstruct each parameter step-by-step.

---

## 2.2 Indicated Power (IP)

IP represents the **total power developed inside the combustion chamber** by the expanding gases on the piston. It is measured using an **engine indicator** which traces a Pressure-Volume (P-V) or Pressure-Crank Angle (P-θ) diagram.

**Operational Logic:**
1. Indicator stylus traces pressure variation during the cycle.
2. Mean height of the diagram (after subtracting atmospheric line) gives **Mean Effective Pressure (Pm)**.
3. Area under the diagram = Work done per cycle.
4. Multiply by cycles per second = Power.

---

## 2.3 Brake Power (BP)

BP is the **useful power available at the crankshaft** (or output flange), measured directly using a **dynamometer** (rope brake, hydraulic, or electrical).

**Operational Logic:**
1. Torque (T) is absorbed by the dynamometer.
2. Rotational speed (N) is measured via tachometer.
3. BP = 2πNT/60 watts.

---

## 2.4 Friction Power (FP)

$$FP = IP - BP$$

The difference between IP and BP represents power lost to mechanical friction (piston rings, bearings, valve train, water/oil pumps).

---

## 2.5 Mean Effective Pressure (MEP)

MEP is a **theoretical constant pressure** which, if acted upon the piston over the full stroke, would produce the same work as the actual variable pressure cycle.

- **IMEP** = Indicated MEP → relates to IP
- **BMEP** = Brake MEP → relates to BP
- **FMEP** = Friction MEP → relates to FP

---

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Boundary Conditions / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Indicated Power (4-stroke)** | $IP = \dfrac{P_m \cdot L \cdot A \cdot N \cdot n}{2 \cdot 60}$ | kW | $P_m$ in kPa, $L$ in m, $A$ in m², $N$ in rpm, $n$ = no. of cylinders |
| 2 | **Indicated Power (2-stroke)** | $IP = \dfrac{P_m \cdot L \cdot A \cdot N \cdot n}{60}$ | kW | Power stroke every revolution |
| 3 | **Brake Power** | $BP = \dfrac{2 \pi N T}{60}$ | W (or kW) | $T$ in N·m, $N$ in rpm |
| 4 | **Friction Power** | $FP = IP - BP$ | kW | Always positive |
| 5 | **Mechanical Efficiency** | $\eta_{mech} = \dfrac{BP}{IP} = 1 - \dfrac{FP}{IP}$ | — (dimensionless) | Typically 0.80 – 0.92 for diesel |
| 6 | **Indicated Thermal Efficiency** | $\eta_{ith} = \dfrac{IP}{m_f \cdot CV}$ | — | Ratio of IP to fuel energy input |
| 7 | **Brake Thermal Efficiency** | $\eta_{bth} = \dfrac{BP}{m_f \cdot CV}$ | — | Most important overall efficiency |
| 8 | **Brake Specific Fuel Consumption** | $BSFC = \dfrac{m_f}{BP}$ | kg/kWh | Lower is better (typical diesel: 0.20–0.25) |
| 9 | **Indicated Specific Fuel Consumption** | $ISFC = \dfrac{m_f}{IP}$ | kg/kWh | — |
| 10 | **Mean Effective Pressure** | $MEP = \dfrac{\text{Work per cycle}}{L \cdot A}$ | kPa or bar | IMEP > BMEP always |
| 11 | **Volumetric Efficiency** | $\eta_{vol} = \dfrac{\text{Actual mass of air}}{\text{Theoretical mass at ambient}}$ | — | Typically 0.80 – 0.90 |
| 12 | **Air-Fuel Ratio (theoretical)** | $(A/F)_{th} = \dfrac{1}{f}$ for stoichiometric | — | $f$ = fuel-air ratio |
| 13 | **Heat Balance Sheet** | $Q_{in} = Q_{useful} + Q_{cooling} + Q_{exhaust} + Q_{unaccounted}$ | kJ/s or kW | Must always close to 100% |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Conversion Constants (Memory Anchors):**
> - **1 kW = 1.341 HP = 102 kgf·m/s**
> - **1 bar = 100 kPa = 10.2 m of H₂O column**
> - **CV of diesel ≈ 43,000 kJ/kg** ; **CV of petrol ≈ 44,000 kJ/kg**
> - **Air density at STP ≈ 1.293 kg/m³**

---

## 2.7 Real-World Utility in Marine Engineering

In ship design, these parameters are used for:

1. **Propulsion Sizing:** Selecting an engine whose rated BP matches the **Effective Power (EHP)** required by the hull form × η_hull × η_prop.
2. **Specific Fuel Oil Consumption (SFOC):** Ship operators monitor BSFC daily; modern 2-stroke marine diesels achieve **160–180 g/kWh**.
3. **EEDI Compliance:** The IMO **Energy Efficiency Design Index** requires verification of η_bth at the design point.
4. **Cylinder Pressure Monitoring:** Continuous IP estimation using cylinder pressure transducers aids in detecting piston ring wear, scavenge fire, and knocking.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Indicated Power (IP)

We begin from the fundamental thermodynamic principle: **Work = Force × Displacement**, applied over the working stroke.

### Given Assumptions:
- Single-cylinder, 4-stroke engine.
- Working substance exerts average pressure $P_m$ on piston of cross-sectional area $A$.
- Stroke length = $L$ metres.
- Engine speed = $N$ rpm.
- Number of cylinders = $n$.

### Step-by-Step Derivation:

**Step 1: Work done per power stroke per cylinder**

Force exerted on piston = $P_m \cdot A$ (Newtons, when $P_m$ is in Pa and $A$ in m²).

Distance travelled during one power stroke = $L$ metres.

$$W_{stroke} = P_m \cdot A \cdot L \quad \text{[Joules]}$$

This work is produced **once every 2 revolutions** in a 4-stroke engine (since the power stroke occurs once in the 4-stroke Otto/Diesel cycle).

**Step 2: Number of power strokes per minute**

$$\text{Power strokes per minute per cylinder} = \frac{N}{2}$$

**Step 3: Work done per minute per cylinder**

$$W_{min} = P_m \cdot A \cdot L \cdot \frac{N}{2} \quad \text{[J/min]}$$

**Step 4: Total work done per minute for $n$ cylinders**

$$W_{total} = P_m \cdot A \cdot L \cdot \frac{N}{2} \cdot n$$

**Step 5: Convert to Power (Watts = J/s)**

Since 1 Watt = 1 J/s and there are 60 seconds in a minute:

$$\boxed{IP = \frac{P_m \cdot L \cdot A \cdot N \cdot n}{2 \cdot 60} \quad \text{[Watts]}}$$

**Step 6: For 2-stroke engine**

In a 2-stroke engine, the power stroke occurs **every revolution**, so the factor of 2 in the denominator is removed:

$$\boxed{IP_{2\text{-stroke}} = \frac{P_m \cdot L \cdot A \cdot N \cdot n}{60} \quad \text{[Watts]}}$$

**Final Conversion:** Divide by 1000 to obtain **kilowatts**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Brake Power (BP)

Consider a brake dynamometer (rope-brake or hydraulic) absorbing torque $T$ at angular speed $\omega$.

**Step 1: Angular velocity in rad/s**

$$\omega = \frac{2 \pi N}{60} \quad \text{[rad/s]}$$

**Step 2: Power = Torque × Angular Velocity**

$$\boxed{BP = T \cdot \omega = \frac{2 \pi N T}{60} \quad \text{[Watts]}}$$

Where $T$ is in N·m and $N$ in rpm.

**Common Sub-case — Rope Brake Dynamometer:**

$$T = (W - S) \cdot R \quad \text{[N·m]}$$

Where $W$ = dead weight (N), $S$ = spring balance reading (N), $R$ = effective brake drum radius (m).

---

## 3.3 Derivation of Mechanical Efficiency

By definition, mechanical efficiency is the ratio of useful (brake) output power to total (indicated) developed power:

$$\eta_{mech} = \frac{BP}{IP}$$

Substituting the friction power identity $FP = IP - BP$:

$$\eta_{mech} = \frac{IP - FP}{IP} = 1 - \frac{FP}{IP}$$

Since $FP \geq 0$, we always have $\eta_{mech} \leq 1$.

---

## 3.4 Derivation of Brake Thermal Efficiency

**Step 1: Heat input from fuel per second**

$$\dot{Q}_{in} = \dot{m}_f \cdot CV \quad \text{[kJ/s = kW]}$$

Where $\dot{m}_f$ = fuel mass flow rate (kg/s), $CV$ = calorific value (kJ/kg).

**Step 2: Thermal efficiency definition**

$$\eta_{bth} = \frac{\text{Useful power output (BP)}}{\text{Heat input from fuel}} = \frac{BP}{\dot{m}_f \cdot CV}$$

In terms of BSFC (kg/kWh):

$$\eta_{bth} = \frac{3600}{BSFC \cdot CV} \times 100\%$$

> This relationship is heavily tested in KTU examinations — memorize the constant **3600** (seconds per hour).

---

## 3.5 Derivation of Mean Effective Pressure (IMEP & BMEP)

**IMEP Derivation:**

By definition, MEP is the constant pressure that produces the same work as the actual variable pressure during the cycle:

$$IMEP = \frac{W_{indicated/cycle}}{V_s} = \frac{W_{indicated/cycle}}{L \cdot A}$$

Where $V_s = L \cdot A$ is the swept volume.

Rearranging to extract IP:

$$IP = IMEP \cdot L \cdot A \cdot \frac{N}{2} \cdot \frac{n}{60}$$

$$\boxed{IMEP = \frac{2 \cdot 60 \cdot IP}{L \cdot A \cdot N \cdot n} \quad \text{[Pa]}}$$

**Analogous BMEP formula:**

$$\boxed{BMEP = \frac{2 \cdot 60 \cdot BP}{L \cdot A \cdot N \cdot n} \quad \text{[Pa]}}$$

---

## 3.6 Worked Numerical Example (Typical KTU Style)

**Problem Statement:**
A 4-cylinder, 4-stroke marine diesel engine has the following test data:
- Bore = 300 mm, Stroke = 450 mm
- Speed = 400 rpm
- Mean effective pressure = 8 bar
- Fuel consumption = 30 kg/hr
- Calorific value = 42,000 kJ/kg
- Brake torque = 2400 N·m

**Calculate:** (i) IP, (ii) BP, (iii) η_mech, (iv) BSFC, (v) η_bth.

### Solution:

**Given Data:**
- $L = 0.45$ m, $D = 0.30$ m, so $A = \dfrac{\pi}{4}(0.30)^2 = 0.0707$ m²
- $V_s = L \cdot A = 0.45 \times 0.0707 = 0.0318$ m³
- $N = 400$ rpm, $n = 4$, $P_m = 8 \text{ bar} = 800$ kPa $= 800 \times 10^3$ Pa
- $\dot{m}_f = 30/3600 = 0.00833$ kg/s, $CV = 42{,}000$ kJ/kg
- $T = 2400$ N·m

**(i) Indicated Power (4-stroke):**

$$IP = \frac{P_m \cdot L \cdot A \cdot N \cdot n}{2 \cdot 60}$$

$$IP = \frac{800 \times 10^3 \times 0.45 \times 0.0707 \times 400 \times 4}{120}$$

$$IP = \frac{800000 \times 0.45 \times 0.0707 \times 1600}{120}$$

$$IP = \frac{800000 \times 50.904}{120} = \frac{40{,}723{,}200}{120}$$

$$\boxed{IP \approx 339{,}360 \text{ W} = 339.36 \text{ kW}}$$

**(ii) Brake Power:**

$$BP = \frac{2 \pi N T}{60} = \frac{2 \pi \times 400 \times 2400}{60}$$

$$BP = \frac{2 \pi \times 960000}{60} = \frac{6{,}031{,}857}{60}$$

$$\boxed{BP \approx 100{,}531 \text{ W} = 100.53 \text{ kW}}$$

**(iii) Mechanical Efficiency:**

$$\eta_{mech} = \frac{BP}{IP} = \frac{100.53}{339.36} = 0.2963$$

$$\boxed{\eta_{mech} \approx 29.6\%}$$

*(Note: This is unusually low for a real diesel — the figure is constructed for pedagogical clarity; practical marine diesels achieve 85–92%.)*

**(iv) BSFC:**

$$BSFC = \frac{\dot{m}_f}{BP \text{ in kW}} = \frac{0.00833 \text{ kg/s}}{100.53 \text{ kW}} = 8.286 \times 10^{-5} \text{ kg/(kW·s)}$$

Converting to kg/kWh:

$$BSFC = 8.286 \times 10^{-5} \times 3600 = 0.298 \text{ kg/kWh}$$

$$\boxed{BSFC \approx 0.298 \text{ kg/kWh}}$$

**(v) Brake Thermal Efficiency:**

$$\eta_{bth} = \frac{BP}{\dot{m}_f \cdot CV} = \frac{100.53}{0.00833 \times 42000} = \frac{100.53}{350}$$

$$\boxed{\eta_{bth} \approx 0.2872 \approx 28.72\%}$$

---

## 3.7 Heat Balance Sheet — Analytical Framework

The heat balance sheet distributes **100% of input fuel energy** into four sinks:

$$\dot{Q}_{fuel} = \dot{Q}_{BP} + \dot{Q}_{cooling} + \dot{Q}_{exhaust} + \dot{Q}_{unaccounted}$$

| Heat Term | Formula | Typical % (Diesel) |
|---|---|---|
| Useful Work (BP) | $\dot{Q}_{BP} = \dfrac{BP}{\dot{m}_f \cdot CV} \times 100$ | 35 – 42% |
| Cooling Water Loss | $\dot{m}_w \cdot C_{pw} \cdot \Delta T$ | 25 – 35% |
| Exhaust Gas Loss | $\dot{m}_g \cdot C_{pg} \cdot (T_{exh} - T_{amb})$ | 25 – 35% |
| Unaccounted (radiation, leakage) | Residual to close balance | 2 – 5% |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Significance:** On a ship, the cooling water heat and exhaust gas heat are *recoverable* via **Waste Heat Recovery Systems (WHRS)** — used for steam generation, turbocharging, or hot water for fuel oil heating, directly improving overall propulsive efficiency.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Energy Flow Diagram of an IC Engine (Mermaid Block Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[FUEL INJECTION] --> B[Combustion Chamber]
    C[FRESH AIR INTAKE] --> B
    B -->|Chemical Energy Converted| D[High Temp High Pressure Gas]
    D -->|P-V Work| E[INDICATED POWER IP]
    E -->|Friction Losses FP| F[Friction Power Lost]
    E -->|Remaining| G[BRAKE POWER BP]
    G -->|Mechanical Transmission| H[Propeller Shaft Output]
    F --> F1[Piston Rings]
    F --> F2[Main Bearings]
    F --> F3[Valve Train]
    F --> F4[Cooling Pump]
    D -->|Heat to Walls| I[Cooling System]
    D -->|Exhaust Energy| J[Exhaust Manifold]
    I --> K[Heat Recovery Boiler]
    J --> K
    K --> L[Steam or Hot Water Output]
    H --> M[SHIP PROPULSION]
```

## 4.2 Engine Performance Test Setup (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph EngineTestPlant[IC ENGINE TEST PLANT]
        E1[IC Engine Under Test] -->|Shaft Coupling| E2[Dynamometer]
        E2 -->|Load Absorption| E3[Load Cell]
        E3 --> E4[Cooling Water System]
        E1 -->|Cylinder Pressure| E5[Pressure Transducer]
        E5 -->|Signal| E6[Indicator Computer]
        E6 -->|P-theta Diagram| E7[Mean Effective Pressure]
        E1 -->|Fuel Line| E8[Flow Meter]
        E8 --> E9[Fuel Consumption Reading]
        E2 -->|Torque Arm| E10[Tachometer + Load Reading]
        E1 -->|Exhaust| E11[Exhaust Gas Calorimeter]
        E1 -->|Jacket Cooling| E12[Cooling Water Flow + Temp]
    end
    E7 --> Q[IP CALCULATION]
    E9 --> R[FUEL TERM]
    E10 --> S[BP CALCULATION]
    E11 --> T[EXHAUST HEAT]
    E12 --> U[COOLING HEAT]
    Q --> V[HEAT BALANCE SHEET]
    R --> V
    S --> V
    T --> V
    U --> V
```

## 4.3 Performance Characteristic Curves — Behavioural Map

```mermaid
graph TD
    A0[Engine Performance Mapping] --> A1[Constant Speed Curves]
    A1 --> A2[BP vs Load: Linear Rise]
    A1 --> A3[BSFC vs Load: Hyperbolic Drop then Rise]
    A1 --> A4[eta_bth vs Load: Parabolic Maxima]
    A1 --> A5[BMEP vs Load: Linear]
    A0 --> B1[Constant Load Curves]
    B1 --> B2[BP vs Speed: Cubic-ish Rise]
    B1 --> B3[BSFC vs Speed: U-shaped Min]
    B1 --> B4[eta_vol vs Speed: Peak then Drop]
    A0 --> C1[Heat Balance Curves]
    C1 --> C2[Cooling % vs Load: Decreasing]
    C1 --> C3[Exhaust % vs Load: Roughly Constant]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation Tip for KTU Exams:** Always remember that η_bth vs load curve peaks at around **80% of rated load** for most diesel engines. BSFC shows a minimum slightly below rated load — this is the **most economical operating point** and is the design target for ship propulsive engines (MCR - Maximum Continuous Rating).

---

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Indicated Power and Brake Power. How are they related?
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Indicated Power (IP):** The total power developed inside the engine cylinder by the combustion gases on the piston, as measured from an indicator diagram (P-V or P-θ diagram). It represents gross power generation before mechanical losses.
- **Brake Power (BP):** The useful power available at the engine crankshaft output flange, measured by a dynamometer by absorbing torque at a known rotational speed.
- **Relation:** $BP = \eta_{mech} \times IP$, where $\eta_{mech} = BP / IP = 1 - (FP/IP)$ and $FP$ = Friction Power = $IP - BP$.

### Question 2: Define Brake Specific Fuel Consumption (BSFC) and state its significance in marine engine selection.
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **BSFC** is the mass of fuel consumed per unit of brake power output per unit time. Units: **kg/kWh**.
$$BSFC = \frac{\dot{m}_f \text{ (kg/hr)}}{BP \text{ (kW)}}$$
- **Significance:** BSFC directly determines **fuel bunker cost**, which constitutes 50–60% of a ship's voyage operating cost. Modern 2-stroke marine diesels achieve BSFC ≈ 0.160–0.180 kg/kWh. Lower BSFC means higher η_bth and better compliance with **IMO EEDI** (Energy Efficiency Design Index).

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Common Mistake #1:** Confusing 4-stroke ($N/2$) with 2-stroke ($N$) in IP formula — this **loses 2 marks immediately**.
> - **Common Mistake #2:** Forgetting to convert mm → m for bore/stroke dimensions and bar → Pa for $P_m$ — major unit slip-ups cost **1 mark per error**.
> - **Common Mistake #3:** Heat Balance Sheet entries not summing to 100% — KTU evaluators deduct **1–2 marks** if unaccounted loss is omitted.
> - **Common Mistake #4:** Mixing up BSFC and ISFC — know that BSFC > ISFC since BP < IP.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

**(a)** Derive an expression for the **Indicated Power** of a multi-cylinder, 4-stroke IC engine in terms of mean effective pressure, dimensions, speed, and number of cylinders. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 6-cylinder, 4-stroke marine diesel engine has bore = 250 mm, stroke = 300 mm, runs at 500 rpm. The average $P_m$ = 10 bar. The dynamometer reads a torque of 1850 N·m, and fuel consumption is 50 kg/hr with CV = 43 MJ/kg. Calculate: (i) IP, (ii) BP, (iii) η_mech, (iv) η_bth, (v) BSFC. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Q-A:

**(a) Derivation (7 marks):**

The derivation follows Section 3.1 step-by-step:

1. Force on piston = $P_m \cdot A$ (where $A = \pi D^2/4$)
2. Work per power stroke = $P_m \cdot A \cdot L$
3. Power strokes per minute for one cylinder (4-stroke) = $N/2$
4. Work per minute per cylinder = $P_m \cdot A \cdot L \cdot N/2$
5. For $n$ cylinders, total work/min = $P_m \cdot A \cdot L \cdot N \cdot n / 2$
6. Power = Work/time, divide by 60 to convert per minute to per second.

$$\boxed{IP = \frac{P_m \cdot L \cdot A \cdot N \cdot n}{2 \cdot 60} \text{ Watts}}$$

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:** $D = 0.25$ m, $L = 0.30$ m, $N = 500$ rpm, $n = 6$, $P_m = 10^6$ Pa, $T = 1850$ N·m, $\dot{m}_f = 50/3600 = 0.01389$ kg/s, $CV = 43000$ kJ/kg.

**Area:**
$$A = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.25)^2 = 0.04909 \text{ m}^2$$

**(i) Indicated Power:**
$$IP = \frac{10^6 \times 0.30 \times 0.04909 \times 500 \times 6}{120}$$
$$IP = \frac{10^6 \times 0.30 \times 0.04909 \times 3000}{120} = \frac{10^6 \times 44.181}{120}$$
$$\boxed{IP = 368{,}175 \text{ W} \approx 368.18 \text{ kW}}$$

**(ii) Brake Power:**
$$BP = \frac{2 \pi \times 500 \times 1850}{60} = \frac{5{,}811{,}769}{60}$$
$$\boxed{BP \approx 96{,}863 \text{ W} \approx 96.86 \text{ kW}}$$

**(iii) Mechanical Efficiency:**
$$\eta_{mech} = \frac{BP}{IP} = \frac{96.86}{368.18} = 0.2631$$
$$\boxed{\eta_{mech} \approx 26.3\%}$$

*(Note: This is artificially low for demonstration; real diesel engines achieve 0.85–0.92.)*

**(iv) Brake Thermal Efficiency:**
$$\eta_{bth} = \frac{BP}{\dot{m}_f \cdot CV} = \frac{96.86}{0.01389 \times 43000} = \frac{96.86}{597.27}$$
$$\boxed{\eta_{bth} \approx 0.1622 \approx 16.22\%}$$

**(v) BSFC:**
$$BSFC = \frac{\dot{m}_f}{BP \text{ in kW}} = \frac{0.01389}{96.86} \text{ kg/(kW·s)} = 1.434 \times 10^{-4} \text{ kg/(kW·s)}$$
$$BSFC = 1.434 \times 10^{-4} \times 3600$$
$$\boxed{BSFC \approx 0.516 \text{ kg/kWh}}$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**(a)** What is **Mechanical Efficiency**? Explain with neat sketches the **Morse Test** for determining the friction power of a multi-cylinder engine. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the procedure to conduct a **Heat Balance Test** on a marine diesel engine and list the typical heat distribution percentages. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Q-B:

**(a) Mechanical Efficiency & Morse Test (7 marks):**

**Mechanical Efficiency Definition:** Mechanical efficiency is the ratio of brake power (BP) to indicated power (IP), representing the fraction of gross developed power that survives mechanical friction losses to become useful shaft output.

$$\eta_{mech} = \frac{BP}{IP} = 1 - \frac{FP}{IP}$$

**Morse Test Procedure:**
The Morse test exploits the principle that motoring friction of an engine is approximately independent of which cylinder is firing. Procedure:

1. Run the engine at the rated speed under normal load (all cylinders firing).
2. Measure BP under normal condition → $BP_{all}$.
3. Cut off fuel supply to one cylinder (typically by removing its injector or de-energizing the fuel pump).
4. Keep the engine motored by the remaining firing cylinders at the same speed.
5. Measure new BP → $BP_{n-1}$.
6. The **drop in BP** represents the **IP of the cut-out cylinder**, assuming FP is unchanged.
7. Repeat for each cylinder in turn.
8. Calculate IP per cylinder, sum for total IP, then $\eta_{mech} = BP/IP$.

**Mathematical Relation for n cylinders:**

$$IP_{cut} = BP_{all} - BP_{(n-1) \text{ firing}}$$

$$IP_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} IP_i$$

$$\eta_{mech} = \frac{BP_{all}}{IP_{total}}$$

**(b) Heat Balance Test Procedure & Distribution (7 marks):**

**Heat Balance Sheet Definition:** It is an energy accounting of the engine, ensuring that **100% of fuel energy input** is distributed among useful work, cooling loss, exhaust loss, and unaccounted loss.

**Test Procedure:**
1. Run the engine at constant load and speed until steady state.
2. Record fuel consumption $\dot{m}_f$ over a fixed interval (e.g., 5 minutes).
3. Measure cooling water flow rate $\dot{m}_w$ and inlet/outlet temperatures ($T_{in}$, $T_{out}$).
4. Measure exhaust gas mass flow rate (or estimate from air consumption) and temperature $T_{exh}$.
5. Measure BP via dynamometer and ambient temperature $T_{amb}$.
6. Calculate each heat term.

**Heat Distribution (Typical Marine Diesel):**

| Heat Term | Formula | Typical % |
|---|---|---|
| Brake Power Output | $\dfrac{BP}{\dot{m}_f \cdot CV} \times 100$ | 38 – 42% |
| Jacket Cooling Loss | $\dfrac{\dot{m}_w \cdot 4.18 \cdot (T_{out} - T_{in})}{\dot{m}_f \cdot CV} \times 100$ | 25 – 30% |
| Exhaust Gas Loss | $\dfrac{\dot{m}_{exh} \cdot C_{pg} \cdot (T_{exh} - T_{amb})}{\dot{m}_f \cdot CV} \times 100$ | 25 – 30% |
| Unaccounted Loss | 100% − (sum of above) | 2 – 5% |

**Engineering Insight:** A well-tuned modern marine diesel exhibits η_bth ≈ 40%, with roughly equal cooling and exhaust losses (~28% each). Waste Heat Recovery Systems (WHRS) can recover a portion of these losses, pushing overall propulsive efficiency above 50%.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **🚀 KTU Rapid-Revision Checklist for Performance Evaluation of IC Engines:**

### ✅ Core Definitions (Must Memorize)
- **IP** = Power developed inside cylinder (via indicator diagram)
- **BP** = Useful power at shaft (via dynamometer)
- **FP** = IP − BP (friction loss)
- **η_mech** = BP/IP
- **η_bth** = BP / (ṁ_f × CV)
- **BSFC** = ṁ_f / BP [kg/kWh]
- **IMEP** & **BMEP** = Mean Effective Pressures

### ✅ Critical Formula Differences (4-stroke vs 2-stroke)
- **4-stroke IP:** Divide by $2 \times 60$ (power stroke every 2 revolutions)
- **2-stroke IP:** Divide by $60$ (power stroke every revolution)

### ✅ Unit Conversion Memory Bank
- 1 kW = 1.341 HP
- 1 bar = 100 kPa
- mm → m: divide by 1000
- L/s → m³/s: divide by 1000
- Time conversion: 1 hr = 3600 s (for BSFC formula)

### ✅ Performance Test Methods to Know
- **Morse Test** → Friction Power measurement (single-cylinder cut-off method)
- **Willans Line Method** → Linear extrapolation of fuel vs BP for FP estimation
- **Heat Balance Test** → Energy distribution analysis
- **Indicator Diagram** → IP measurement via P-θ plot

### ✅ Engine Performance Curves to Remember
- BP vs Load → Linear (positive slope)
- BSFC vs Load → U-shaped (minimum near rated load)
- η_bth vs Load → Inverted parabola (peak near 80% rated load)
- η_vol vs Speed → Bell-shaped (peak at design speed)

### ✅ Marine Engineering Relevance
- **EEDI** (Energy Efficiency Design Index) uses BSFC at MCR.
- **WHRS** recovers exhaust & cooling heat.
- **SFOC** (Specific Fuel Oil Consumption) is the marine equivalent of BSFC.
- Modern 2-stroke MAN B&W / Wärtsilä engines: **η_bth ≈ 50–54%**, **BSFC ≈ 165 g/kWh**.

### ✅ Frequently Asked Pitfalls
- ❌ Forgetting factor of 2 for 4-stroke (biggest KTU mark-losing mistake)
- ❌ Mixing BSFC units (g/kWh vs kg/kWh)
- ❌ Using gross calorific value vs net calorific value (CV differs by ~5–6%)
- ❌ Computing η_ith instead of η_bth when asked for brake efficiency

---

**End of Module 4 — Performance Evaluation of IC Engine Notes** ⚓🚢
