topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST411 - MECHANICAL ENGINEERING FOR SHIP DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Cooling System

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Marine Cooling System** is an integrated network of heat exchangers, pumps, piping, valves, and control instrumentation designed to transfer surplus thermal energy from the prime mover (main engine, auxiliary engines, generators, and auxiliaries) to a heat sink (typically seawater) while maintaining the working fluid (freshwater or lubricating oil) within precise operational temperature bands.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Core Definition:** A marine cooling system is not merely a "radiator." It is a *closed-loop thermodynamic heat-rejection network* that simultaneously protects cylinders, pistons, turbochargers, air coolers, lubricating oil, and reduction gears from thermal degradation, while conditioning the engine room ambient and supporting HVAC subsystems.

### 1.1.1 Functional Classification (As per KTU Marine Engineering Lexicon)

| Class | Heat Carrier | Heat Sink | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Sea Water (SW) Cooling — Direct** | Sea water | — | Small auxiliaries, bilge, sanitary |
| **Sea Water (SW) Cooling — Indirect** | Fresh water → Sea water | Sea | Main propulsion, large auxiliaries |
| **Central Cooling** | LT freshwater → SW | Sea | Modern integrated vessels |
| **Fresh Water (FW) Cooling** | Demineralized water | SW via HX | Jacket, piston, turbocharger |
| **Oil Cooling** | Lube oil → FW | SW via HX | Bearings, gears, hydraulic systems |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain-English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world Analogy — The Human Circulatory System**
>
> Think of a marine engine as a human body during a marathon run. When the body overheats, **blood (freshwater coolant)** flows from the hot **muscles (engine cylinders)** to the **skin (sea-water heat exchanger)** where sweat (sea) carries the heat away. The **heart (cooling water pump)** keeps the flow going, and **sweat glands (sea chests & strainers)** ensure clean water enters. The **kidneys (thermostatic valves & filters)** regulate temperature and cleanliness. If any part fails — say, the heart stops or the skin is blocked — the body (engine) suffers catastrophic damage.
>
> In a ship:
> - **Sea = Infinite heat sink** (surrounding water body)
> - **Engine = Heat source** (combustion, friction)
> - **Coolant = Heat carrier** (freshwater, low-salinity)
> - **Heat Exchanger = Heat transfer interface** (lungs, skin, radiator)

---

## 1.3 Governing Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following constants govern cooling system design in marine applications:

- **Standard seawater density:** **ρ_sw = 1025 kg/m³** (at 4°C, 3.5% salinity)
- **Standard freshwater density:** **ρ_fw = 1000 kg/m³** (at 4°C)
- **Specific heat of freshwater:** **c_p,fw = 4186 J/(kg·K)**
- **Specific heat of seawater:** **c_p,sw ≈ 3985 J/(kg·K)**
- **Thermal conductivity of freshwater:** **k_fw ≈ 0.60 W/(m·K)** at 20°C
- **Thermal conductivity of seawater:** **k_sw ≈ 0.56 W/(m·K)** at 20°C
- **Engine jacket water outlet temperature (typical):** **80–95°C** (modern 2-stroke: 80–90°C)
- **Piston cooling water outlet temperature:** **50–60°C**
- **Lubricating oil outlet temperature (turbocharger bearings):** **≤ 65°C**
- **Sea chest temperature rise limit:** **≤ 12°C** across heat exchanger (Class NK, DNV rules)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** In the KTU 2024 scheme, students must recognize that the *temperature gradient* (ΔT) across a marine heat exchanger is the **primary design driver** — not the absolute temperature.

---

## 1.4 Why Marine Cooling Systems Are Unique

Unlike land-based power plants, shipboard cooling systems must contend with:

1. **Tilt and motion** — up to **±22.5°** rolling; components must remain primed.
2. **Variable heat sink temperature** — tropical sea: ~32°C; arctic: ~-1.8°C.
3. **Biofouling** — macro-fouling in sea chests reduces heat transfer efficiency.
4. **Material constraints** — titanium tubes, cupro-nickel (90/10, 70/30) for SW side.
5. **Classification society rules** — DNV, Lloyd's, ABS, Class NK mandate redundancy and emergency cooling capability (e.g., **two seawater pumps** per main engine).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Concept — Layer-by-Layer Heat Flow

A typical **central cooling system** (used on most modern merchant vessels: tankers, container ships, bulk carriers) operates in **three thermodynamic loops**. Each loop is a **closed control volume** to which the First Law applies.

### Loop 1 — High-Temperature (HT) Freshwater Circuit
- **Components:** Engine jacket → Jacket water pump → HT freshwater cooler → Expansion tank → return to engine.
- **Function:** Removes heat from cylinder liners, cylinder heads, exhaust manifolds.
- **Temperature band:** **80–95°C** outlet from engine.

### Loop 2 — Low-Temperature (LT) Freshwater Circuit
- **Components:** LT FW pump → LT FW cooler (plate heat exchanger) → serves air coolers, lube oil coolers, reduction gear oil coolers.
- **Function:** Removes heat from charge air, lube oil, gear oil at lower temperatures.
- **Temperature band:** **35–45°C** outlet from coolers.

### Loop 3 — Seawater Circuit
- **Components:** Sea chest → Strainers → SW pump → Box cooler / Plate cooler / Keel cooler → overboard discharge.
- **Function:** Ultimate heat sink; carries all rejected heat to the sea.
- **Temperature band:** Inlet varies **5–32°C** by geography; outlet limited to **+12°C above inlet** by classification rules.

---

## 2.2 The Three Heat Transfer Modes in Marine Coolers

| Mode | Mechanism | Where It Dominates in Marine Cooling |
|---|---|---|
| **Conduction** | Molecular vibration transfer | Tube walls of shell-and-tube coolers |
| **Convection (forced)** | Bulk fluid motion driven by pump | Inside tubes (tube-side) and shell-side |
| **Convection (natural)** | Buoyancy-driven flow | Expansion tank, de-aeration tank |

> [!NOTE]
> Most marine heat exchangers operate under **forced convection** on both sides. The dimensionless numbers that govern performance are the **Nusselt (Nu)**, **Reynolds (Re)**, and **Prandtl (Pr)** numbers.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas below are NEP 2020 / KTU 2024 Scheme high-yield. Memorize the boxed equations.**

| # | Formula | Meaning / Use | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\dot{Q} = \dot{m} \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$ | Heat duty by sensible heat | W |
| 2 | $\dot{m} = \rho \cdot \dot{V}$ | Mass flow from volumetric flow | kg/s |
| 3 | $\dot{Q} = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T_{LMTD}$ | Heat transfer through HX surface | W |
| 4 | $\Delta T_{LMTD} = \dfrac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)}$ | Log-Mean Temperature Difference | K |
| 5 | $P = \dfrac{T_h^{in} - T_c^{out}}{T_h^{in} - T_c^{in}}$ | Heat capacity ratio (effectiveness-NTU) | dimensionless |
| 6 | $R = \dfrac{C_{min}}{C_{max}} = \dfrac{(\dot{m} c_p)_{min}}{(\dot{m} c_p)_{max}}$ | Capacity ratio | dimensionless |
| 7 | $\varepsilon = 1 - \exp\!\left[-NTU \cdot (1-R)\right]$ | Effectiveness, parallel-flow HX | dimensionless |
| 8 | $NTU = \dfrac{U \cdot A}{C_{min}}$ | Number of Transfer Units | dimensionless |
| 9 | $Re = \dfrac{\rho \cdot v \cdot D}{\mu}$ | Reynolds number (tube flow) | dimensionless |
| 10 | $Nu = 0.023 \cdot Re^{0.8} \cdot Pr^{0.4}$ | Dittus-Boelter (turbulent, heating) | dimensionless |
| 11 | $h = \dfrac{Nu \cdot k}{D}$ | Convective heat transfer coefficient | W/(m²·K) |
| 12 | $\dfrac{1}{U} = \dfrac{1}{h_i} + \dfrac{\ln(D_o/D_i)}{2\pi k L} + \dfrac{1}{h_o}$ | Overall U (cylindrical tube, per unit length) | m²·K/W |
| 13 | $\Delta P_{friction} = f \cdot \dfrac{L}{D} \cdot \dfrac{\rho v^2}{2}$ | Major pipe pressure loss | Pa |
| 14 | $f = 0.316 \cdot Re^{-0.25}$ | Blasius friction factor (smooth pipes, Re < 10⁵) | dimensionless |
| 15 | $T_{out} = T_{in} + \dfrac{\dot{Q}}{\dot{m} \cdot c_p}$ | Outlet temperature from heat balance | K |
| 16 | $\dot{V}_{sw} = \dfrac{\dot{Q}_{total}}{c_{p,sw} \cdot \rho_{sw} \cdot \Delta T_{sw}}$ | Sea water pump capacity sizing | m³/s |

> [!NOTE]
> **Boundary condition reminders for KTU exams:**
> - For **counter-flow HX:** $\Delta T_1 = T_{h,in} - T_{c,out}$; $\Delta T_2 = T_{h,out} - T_{c,in}$.
> - For **parallel-flow HX:** $\Delta T_1 = T_{h,in} - T_{c,in}$; $\Delta T_2 = T_{h,out} - T_{c,out}$.
> - For **correction factor F** in multi-pass shell-and-tube: $\Delta T_{mt} = F \cdot \Delta T_{LMTD}$.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

In ship design, cooling systems influence:

1. **E-EDI (Energy Efficiency Design Index)** — IMO MARPOL Annex VI. Inefficient heat exchangers raise fuel consumption.
2. **Tier III NOx compliance** — Aftercoolers for SCR systems demand *precise* LT FW control.
3. **EEXI (Energy Efficiency Existing Ship Index)** — Engine derating often requires recalibrated cooling.
4. **Classification society certification** — DNV GL, Lloyd's Register specify *minimum* sea chest sizing, dual SW pump redundancy, and emergency fire pump cooling derivation.
5. **Cyber-enabled cooling** — Modern MAN B&W / Wärtsilä engines integrate IoT sensors into jacket FW loops for **predictive maintenance** (temperature drift detection → early fouling alarm).

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Heat Duty and Outlet Temperature (Sensible Heat Balance)

### Setup
A marine engine jacket rejects a total of $\dot{Q}_{total}$ kW. The HT freshwater enters at $T_{fw,in}$ and leaves at $T_{fw,out}$. The mass flow is $\dot{m}_{fw}$.

### Step 1 — Apply First Law to the steady-flow control volume
For a closed single-phase loop with negligible KE/PE and no work:

$$
\dot{Q} = \dot{m}_{fw} \cdot (h_{out} - h_{in})
$$

### Step 2 — Approximate enthalpy difference by $c_p \Delta T$
For water over the temperature range 0–100°C, $c_p$ is nearly constant at **4186 J/(kg·K)**:

$$
\dot{Q} = \dot{m}_{fw} \cdot c_{p,fw} \cdot (T_{fw,out} - T_{fw,in})
$$

### Step 3 — Solve for outlet temperature
Rearranging:

$$
T_{fw,out} = T_{fw,in} + \frac{\dot{Q}}{\dot{m}_{fw} \cdot c_{p,fw}}
$$

### Step 4 — Invert to find required flow for a given temperature rise
If the designer fixes $\Delta T_{fw} = T_{fw,out} - T_{fw,in}$ (typically 10–15°C), the required FW flow is:

$$
\dot{m}_{fw} = \frac{\dot{Q}}{\dot{c}_{p,fw} \cdot \Delta T_{fw}}
$$

### Step 5 — Convert to volumetric flow
Pump capacity in m³/h:

$$
\dot{V}_{fw} = \frac{3600 \cdot \dot{Q}}{\rho_{fw} \cdot c_{p,fw} \cdot \Delta T_{fw}}
$$

**Text row:** The 3600 factor converts m³/s to m³/h. This is the **canonical pump-sizing equation** used by ship design offices.

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (Model Question for KTU 14-Mark Problems)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem (Model — 14 marks):** A marine 2-stroke main engine has a total jacket heat loss of **Q_total = 1800 kW**. The HT freshwater enters the jacket cooler at 75°C and is to leave at **88°C** (ΔT = 13°C). Seawater is available at **28°C** and may be warmed by **10°C** in the freshwater cooler (counter-flow, shell-and-tube). The overall heat transfer coefficient is **U = 850 W/(m²·K)**.
>
> **Find:**
> (a) Required freshwater and seawater mass flow rates. (7 marks)
> (b) Required heat exchanger surface area, using LMTD. (7 marks)

### Part (a) — Mass Flow Rates (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Freshwater mass flow:**

$$
\dot{m}_{fw} = \frac{\dot{Q}}{c_{p,fw} \cdot \Delta T_{fw}}
$$

Plug in values (Q = 1,800,000 W; c_p,fw = 4186 J/(kg·K); ΔT = 13 K):

$$
\dot{m}_{fw} = \frac{1.80 \times 10^6}{4186 \times 13}
$$

Compute denominator: 4186 × 13 = **54,418**

$$
\dot{m}_{fw} = \frac{1.80 \times 10^6}{5.4418 \times 10^4} = 33.08 \text{ kg/s}
$$

**Text row:** This is the canonical pump sizing step. At ρ_fw = 1000 kg/m³, $\dot{V}_{fw} \approx 119$ m³/h.

**Step 2 — Seawater mass flow:**

Seawater enters at T_sw,in = 28°C and is allowed a ΔT_sw = 10°C. So T_sw,out = 38°C.

$$
\dot{m}_{sw} = \frac{\dot{Q}}{c_{p,sw} \cdot \Delta T_{sw}}
$$

Using c_p,sw = 3985 J/(kg·K):

$$
\dot{m}_{sw} = \frac{1.80 \times 10^6}{3985 \times 10} = \frac{1.80 \times 10^6}{3.985 \times 10^4} = 45.17 \text{ kg/s}
$$

**Text row:** Using ρ_sw = 1025 kg/m³, $\dot{V}_{sw} \approx 158.6$ m³/h — a typical size for an HFO-fired 2-stroke engine.

---

### Part (b) — Heat Exchanger Area (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Identify temperature differences at both ends (counter-flow):**

For counter-flow heat exchanger:
- **Hot end (T_h,in=88, T_c,out=38):** $\Delta T_1 = 88 - 38 = 50°C$
- **Cold end (T_h,out=75, T_c,in=28):** $\Delta T_2 = 75 - 28 = 47°C$

**Step 2 — Compute LMTD:**

$$
\Delta T_{LMTD} = \frac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)} = \frac{50 - 47}{\ln(50/47)}
$$

Compute: 50 - 47 = 3
ln(50/47) = ln(1.0638) = 0.0619

$$
\Delta T_{LMTD} = \frac{3}{0.0619} = 48.46°C
$$

**Step 3 — Solve for area:**

$$
A = \frac{\dot{Q}}{U \cdot \Delta T_{LMTD}} = \frac{1.80 \times 10^6}{850 \times 48.46}
$$

Compute denominator: 850 × 48.46 = 41,191

$$
A = \frac{1.80 \times 10^6}{4.1191 \times 10^4} = 43.7 \text{ m}^2
$$

**Text row:** This is a realistic marine freshwater cooler size for a single cooler. In practice, **two 100% capacity coolers** would be installed in parallel (classification redundancy).

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Typical Marine Cooler Performance Bands

| Cooler Type | U [W/(m²·K)] | LMTD [°C] | ΔP Allowed | Material |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **LT freshwater plate HX** | 4000–6000 | 8–15 | < 50 kPa | Stainless 316L plates |
| **HT freshwater shell-and-tube** | 800–1200 | 10–25 | < 80 kPa | Cu-Ni tubes / CS shell |
| **Lube oil shell-and-tube** | 200–400 | 15–30 | < 60 kPa | Cu-Ni / Ti tubes |
| **Central cooler (sea side, box)** | 500–700 | 5–12 | < 30 kPa | Titanium / Cu-Ni |
| **Keel cooler (sea side)** | 200–400 | 5–10 | n/a (natural convection aided) | Cu-Ni or Titanium |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> These U-values are **engineering rules of thumb** consolidated from DNV GL Pt.4 Ch.2 and the *Marine Engineering* textbook tradition. Use them in estimation; verify with detailed TEMA-based design for project work.

## 4.2 Counter-Flow vs. Parallel-Flow HX Schematic


> [!NOTE]
> **Why counter-flow dominates in marine coolers:** Counter-flow achieves the highest $\Delta T_{LMTD}$, allowing **smaller HX area** for the same heat duty. Most marine FW coolers (LT and HT) are counter-flow.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1. **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**Define the term "Marine Central Cooling System" and list its three primary loops with their typical temperature bands.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A marine central cooling system is an integrated heat-rejection network that uses a single seawater boundary to cool multiple independent freshwater loops. The three primary loops are:
1. **HT (High-Temperature) FW loop** — cools engine jacket, cylinder heads, exhaust manifolds; **75–95°C**.
2. **LT (Low-Temperature) FW loop** — cools charge air, lube oil, gear oil; **35–45°C**.
3. **Seawater loop** — serves as the ultimate heat sink; inlet temperature varies with geography.

*(Award 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for naming the three loops, 1 mark for temperature bands.)*

---

### Question A2. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Why is cupro-nickel (90/10 or 70/30) preferred over carbon steel for seawater-side tubes in marine heat exchangers?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Cupro-nickel is preferred because:
1. It offers excellent **resistance to seawater corrosion** (especially impingement attack and ammonia-induced SCC) — 1 mark.
2. It has good **thermal conductivity (~50 W/m·K for 90/10)** and **biofouling resistance** (the copper-ion release suppresses marine growth) — 1 mark.
3. It satisfies **classification society** requirements (DNV, Lloyd's) for seawater service — 1 mark.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — ESE Module Internal Choice

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pattern:** In Part B, students answer **one** of two full-14-mark questions. Both must be attempted in preparation; the exam offers an internal choice.

---

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Set A]**

**A 2-stroke marine main engine has a total heat loss of 2400 kW to the jacket cooling system. The high-temperature freshwater enters the jacket cooler at 72°C and is to leave at 86°C. The seawater is available at 26°C and is allowed a 9°C temperature rise in the cooler, which is of counter-flow shell-and-tube type with an overall heat transfer coefficient of 900 W/(m²·K).**

**Find:**
**(a)** The required freshwater and seawater mass flow rates. (7 marks)
**(b)** The required heat transfer surface area. (7 marks)

### Model Solution — Question A

#### Part (a) — Mass Flow Rates (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Freshwater mass flow rate:**

Apply $\dot{m}_{fw} = \dot{Q} / (c_{p,fw} \cdot \Delta T_{fw})$ with $\dot{Q} = 2.4 \times 10^6$ W, $c_{p,fw} = 4186$ J/(kg·K), $\Delta T_{fw} = 86 - 72 = 14$ K.

$$
\dot{m}_{fw} = \frac{2.4 \times 10^6}{4186 \times 14} = \frac{2.4 \times 10^6}{58604} = 40.95 \text{ kg/s}
$$

Volumetric flow: $\dot{V}_{fw} = 147.4$ m³/h. **(3 marks)**

**Step 2 — Seawater mass flow rate:**

Apply $\dot{m}_{sw} = \dot{Q} / (c_{p,sw} \cdot \Delta T_{sw})$ with $c_{p,sw} = 3985$ J/(kg·K), $\Delta T_{sw} = 9$ K.

$$
\dot{m}_{sw} = \frac{2.4 \times 10^6}{3985 \times 9} = \frac{2.4 \times 10^6}{35865} = 66.92 \text{ kg/s}
$$

Volumetric flow: $\dot{V}_{sw} = 235$ m³/h. **(2 marks)**

**Step 3 — Sanity check:** Heat rejected on SW side should equal heat absorbed from FW side (energy balance within 1%).

$$
\dot{Q}_{sw} = \dot{m}_{sw} \cdot c_{p,sw} \cdot \Delta T_{sw} = 66.92 \times 3985 \times 9 = 2.40 \times 10^6 \text{ W} \checkmark
$$

**(2 marks for cross-check.)**

#### Part (b) — Heat Exchanger Area (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Define temperature differences (counter-flow):**

- Hot end: $T_{h,in} = 86°C$, $T_{c,out} = 26 + 9 = 35°C$ → $\Delta T_1 = 86 - 35 = 51°C$.
- Cold end: $T_{h,out} = 72°C$, $T_{c,in} = 26°C$ → $\Delta T_2 = 72 - 26 = 46°C$.

**(2 marks)**

**Step 2 — Compute LMTD:**

$$
\Delta T_{LMTD} = \frac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1/\Delta T_2)} = \frac{51 - 46}{\ln(51/46)} = \frac{5}{\ln(1.1087)} = \frac{5}{0.1033} = 48.40°C
$$

**(3 marks)**

**Step 3 — Compute area:**

$$
A = \frac{\dot{Q}}{U \cdot \Delta T_{LMTD}} = \frac{2.4 \times 10^6}{900 \times 48.40} = \frac{2.4 \times 10^6}{43560} = 55.10 \text{ m}^2
$$

**(2 marks)**

**Final Answer (Question A):**
- $\dot{m}_{fw} = 40.95$ kg/s ; $\dot{m}_{sw} = 66.92$ kg/s
- $A \approx 55.1$ m²

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Set B — Alternative]**

**(a)** With a neat sketch, describe the working of a **central freshwater cooling system** used in modern merchant vessels. List the components of each loop and explain how the seawater acts as the ultimate heat sink. (7 marks)

**(b)** A lube oil cooler in a marine auxiliary engine has the following data: oil inlet 78°C, oil outlet 62°C; seawater inlet 24°C, outlet 32°C. Oil side flow rate is 12 kg/h with $c_{p,oil} = 2000$ J/(kg·K). Compute the seawater mass flow rate required and the LMTD. Assume counter-flow arrangement. (7 marks)

### Model Solution — Question B

#### Part (a) — Central Freshwater Cooling System (7 marks)

**Diagram (draw in exam — to be hand-sketched):** See **Section 4.1** Mermaid equivalent.

**Description (7 marks):**
1. **(2 marks) System overview:** A central cooling system uses one seawater boundary to cool two freshwater sub-loops (HT and LT). This protects the engine from direct seawater contact, prevents internal corrosion, and improves thermal control.
2. **(2 marks) HT loop:** Jacket FW pump → Engine jacket → HT cooler → Expansion tank → return. Outlet temp 80–90°C.
3. **(2 marks) LT loop:** LT FW pump → LT cooler (plate) → charge air coolers, lube oil coolers, gear oil coolers → return. Outlet temp 35–45°C.
4. **(1 mark) SW loop:** Sea chest → strainer → SW pump → central plate HX (transfers heat from both FW loops) → overboard. Sea is the *ultimate* heat sink because of its **infinite thermal mass**.

#### Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Heat duty from oil side:**

$$
\dot{m}_{oil} = 12 \text{ kg/h} = \frac{12}{3600} = 0.00333 \text{ kg/s}
$$

$$
\Delta T_{oil} = 78 - 62 = 16°C
$$

$$
\dot{Q} = \dot{m}_{oil} \cdot c_{p,oil} \cdot \Delta T_{oil} = 0.00333 \times 2000 \times 16 = 106.67 \text{ W}
$$

**(2 marks)**

**Step 2 — Seawater mass flow:**

$$
\Delta T_{sw} = 32 - 24 = 8°C
$$

$$
\dot{m}_{sw} = \frac{\dot{Q}}{c_{p,sw} \cdot \Delta T_{sw}} = \frac{106.67}{3985 \times 8} = \frac{106.67}{31880} = 0.003346 \text{ kg/s}
$$

Converting: $\dot{m}_{sw} = 12.05$ kg/h, i.e., $\dot{V}_{sw} \approx 11.76$ L/h.

**(2 marks)**

**Step 3 — LMTD (counter-flow):**

- Hot end: $T_{h,in} = 78°C$, $T_{c,out} = 32°C$ → $\Delta T_1 = 78 - 32 = 46°C$.
- Cold end: $T_{h,out} = 62°C$, $T_{c,in} = 24°C$ → $\Delta T_2 = 62 - 24 = 38°C$.

$$
\Delta T_{LMTD} = \frac{46 - 38}{\ln(46/38)} = \frac{8}{\ln(1.2105)} = \frac{8}{0.1911} = 41.86°C
$$

**(3 marks)**

**Final Answer (Question B):**
- $\dot{m}_{sw} = 0.00335$ kg/s ($\approx 12$ kg/h)
- $\Delta T_{LMTD} = 41.9°C$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Cooling System Problems**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to convert kg/h to kg/s** before applying $\dot{Q} = \dot{m} c_p \Delta T$. **Cost: 2 marks per occurrence.**
> 2. **Mixing up counter-flow vs. parallel-flow LMTD** — the $\Delta T_1$ and $\Delta T_2$ are defined **differently**. **Cost: 3 marks.**
> 3. **Using $c_p = 4186$ for seawater** — this is freshwater; seawater is **3985**. **Cost: 1 mark; reflects engineering knowledge gap.**
> 4. **Not stating units** in the final answer (e.g., "55 m²" without the unit). **Cost: 0.5 mark.**
> 5. **Sketching the system but failing to label hot/cold streams or to mark flow direction.** In (a) sub-parts, **arrows are mandatory** in cooling diagrams. **Cost: 1 mark.**
> 6. **Skipping the energy-balance cross-check** (i.e., confirming $\dot{Q}_{hot} = \dot{Q}_{cold}$). Examiners reward this as evidence of engineering maturity. **Cost: up to 2 marks if missing in long problems.**

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Print-friendly bullet checklist for last-minute revision before the KTU exam.**

- [ ] **3 loops** in a central cooling system: **HT FW, LT FW, SW** — know temperature bands.
- [ ] **Counter-flow** gives higher LMTD than parallel-flow; **preferred in marine coolers**.
- [ ] **LMTD formula:** $\Delta T_{LMTD} = (\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2) / \ln(\Delta T_1/\Delta T_2)$.
- [ ] **Heat duty:** $\dot{Q} = \dot{m} c_p \Delta T$ (mass flow in **kg/s**).
- [ ] **Specific heat values:** $c_{p,fw} = 4186$ J/(kg·K); $c_{p,sw} \approx 3985$ J/(kg·K).
- [ ] **Densities:** $\rho_{fw} = 1000$ kg/m³; $\rho_{sw} = 1025$ kg/m³.
- [ ] **HX area formula:** $A = \dot{Q} / (U \cdot \Delta T_{LMTD})$.
- [ ] **3-way thermostatic valve** controls HT FW bypass; ensures constant engine inlet temperature.
- [ ] **Two seawater pumps** (one running + one standby) are mandatory by class rules.
- [ ] **Cupro-nickel** (90/10, 70/30) is the standard tube material for SW-side; **titanium** for high-end / aggressive service.
- [ ] **Plate heat exchangers** dominate LT circuits; **shell-and-tube** dominate HT circuits.
- [ ] **Keel coolers** are used where biofouling is critical (naval, ice-class vessels).
- [ ] **Seawater temperature rise** across a cooler is limited to **+12°C** by most classification rules.
- [ ] **Expansion tank** acts as the system's de-aerator and surge volume.
- [ ] **Cross-check** $\dot{Q}_{hot} = \dot{Q}_{cold}$ to catch calculation errors.
- [ ] **LMTD correction factor $F < 1$** for multi-pass shell-and-tube — but most KTU problems allow $\Delta T_{LMTD}$ direct.
- [ ] **P, R, ε-NTU method** is an *alternative* to LMTD; useful when outlet temperatures are unknown.
- [ ] **Dittus-Boelter correlation:** $Nu = 0.023 Re^{0.8} Pr^{0.4}$ — applicable for turbulent tube flow with heating (fw heated by hot tube).
- [ ] **Typical U values** (W/(m²·K)): Plate HX 4000–6000; Shell-and-tube FW cooler 800–1200; Lube oil cooler 200–400.
- [ ] **Engine jacket outlet** temperatures: 2-stroke 80–90°C; 4-stroke 80–95°C.
- [ ] **Lube oil outlet** from cooler target: 50–60°C for main engine bearings.
- [ ] **Modern cyber-enabled cooling:** IoT temperature sensors feed to the alarm/monitoring system (MAS) for **early fouling detection**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Ignition System and Fuels
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST411 - MECHANICAL ENGINEERING FOR SHIP DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Ignition System and Fuels

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Ignition System — Formal Definition

In the context of marine power plants and ship propulsion engineering, an **Ignition System** refers to the integrated arrangement of components and control mechanisms responsible for initiating and sustaining the controlled combustion of fuel within the combustion chamber of a marine prime mover — be it a low-speed two-stroke marine diesel engine, a medium/high-speed four-stroke auxiliary diesel engine, a marine gas turbine, or an oil-fired auxiliary boiler.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Note (PENST411 — Module 4):** Unlike automobile petrol engines that rely exclusively on spark ignition, the dominant marine prime mover — the **low-speed, two-stroke, crosshead marine diesel engine** — operates on the **Diesel cycle** using **compression ignition (CI)**. The student's emphasis must therefore be on understanding *how combustion is initiated in CI marine engines*, the *dual-fuel* variants, *gas turbine combustors*, and *boiler burner systems*.

The three dominant ignition philosophies encountered onboard modern seagoing vessels are:

| Ignition Philosophy | Typical Marine Application | Energy Source for Ignition |
|---|---|---|
| Compression Ignition (CI) | Main propulsion 2-stroke & 4-stroke marine diesels | Heat of adiabatic compression |
| Hot Surface / Pilot Ignition | Dual-fuel marine engines (LNG + MDO pilot) | Compressed pilot fuel + hot surface |
| Continuous Combustion | Marine gas turbines | Permanent flame from pilot igniter |
| External Burner Ignition | Auxiliary oil-fired boilers | High-voltage spark / hot wire |

## 1.2 Fuels — Formal Definition

A **Marine Fuel** is any hydrocarbon-based (or hydrocarbon-equivalent) energy carrier stored in the ship's bunker tanks, service tanks, or settling tanks and intended for combustion in marine boilers, internal combustion engines, or gas turbines to produce mechanical work or thermal energy for shipboard propulsion, electrical generation, and auxiliary services.

The principal marine bunker fuel grades, as standardised under **ISO 8217:2017**, are:

- **Residual Grades:** RMG 380, RMG 180, RMK 380, RMK 700 (formerly branded as **HFO — Heavy Fuel Oil**).
- **Distillate Grades:** DMA, DMZ, DMB (commonly known as **MDO / MGO**).
- **Bio-derived / Future Fuels:** LNG, LPG, Methanol, Ethanol, Ammonia, Hydrogen, FAME-based biofuels.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — The "Matchstick vs. Pressure-Cooker" Distinction:**
> Think of igniting a fuel in two contrasting ways. In your household LPG stove, a tiny **matchstick (spark)** lights the gas — this is *spark ignition*. In contrast, aboard a ship, the dominant main engine does NOT use a matchstick. Instead, it **squeezes the air so violently** inside the cylinder (to a pressure exceeding **~ 30 bar** and a temperature exceeding **~ 600 °C**) that when diesel oil is sprayed in, it **self-ignites spontaneously** — like the way a pressure-cooker builds up such intense heat inside that the food chars even without direct flame. That spontaneous, spark-less combustion is **compression ignition** — the beating heart of every merchant ship afloat.

## 1.3 Why This Topic Matters in Ship Design

The selection of the **ignition system** and the **marine fuel grade** directly determines:

1. **Propulsion Plant Specific Fuel Consumption (SFC)** — typically **~ 160–180 g/kWh** for slow-speed diesel on HFO.
2. **Cylinder bore and stroke geometry** — large bore 2-stroke engines (> **80 cm** bore) are uniquely suited for residual fuels.
3. **Bunker tank volume and segregation** — IMO mandates segregated fuel systems under **MARPOL Annex VI** to enable ECA (Emission Control Area) compliance.
4. **Scrubber vs. low-sulphur fuel decision** — directly affects hull form, freeingboard, and machinery space layout.
5. **Future-proofing** — choice of LNG-ready, methanol-ready, or ammonia-ready design impacts the engine room envelope.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constants to Memorise:**
> - Standard Diesel Compression Ratio (marine 2-stroke): **18 : 1 to 22 : 1**
> - Self-Ignition Temperature of Marine Diesel Oil: **~ 250 °C** (much lower than HFO at ~ 350 °C)
> - Lower Calorific Value of HFO: **~ 40,200 kJ/kg**
> - Lower Calorific Value of MGO/MDO: **~ 42,700 kJ/kg**
> - Lower Calorific Value of LNG: **~ 50,000 kJ/kg**
> - IMO MARPOL Annex VI Global Sulphur Cap (since 2020): **0.50 % m/m**
> - IMO ECA Sulphur Cap: **0.10 % m/m**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Marine Diesel Engine — Compression Ignition Process

A slow-speed, two-stroke, turbocharged marine diesel engine is the workhorse of world merchant shipping (powering ~ **60 %** of the global fleet by deadweight tonnage). Its ignition relies entirely on the **Diesel cycle** (also called the **constant-pressure heat addition cycle** when modelled with combustion modelled as a gradual process at near-constant pressure during the early power stroke).

### 2.1.1 Stage-by-Stage Operational Logic

1. **Air Charging Stage (Scavenging):**
   - The turbocharger compresses charge air to a pressure of approximately **2.5 – 3.5 bar** (for 2-stroke main engines) before the scavenge air pump/blower delivers it to the cylinder.
   - Scavenging efficiency typically exceeds **95 %** in modern MAN B&W / Wärtsilä uniflow designs.

2. **Compression Stage:**
   - The piston moves upward, compressing the trapped air. The compression ratio is fixed by the cylinder geometry: **$\varepsilon = \dfrac{V_s + V_c}{V_c}$**
   - Compression pressures reach **~ 120–180 bar** and peak temperatures of **~ 600–800 °C**.

3. **Fuel Injection Stage:**
   - At a few degrees Before Top Dead Centre (BTDC — typically 6° to 12°), the **heavy fuel injector** (operating at injection pressures of **~ 1000 – 1800 bar**) sprays atomised fuel into the hot compressed air.
   - Atomisation produces fine droplets (**< 30 µm Sauter Mean Diameter**) to maximise surface area for evaporation.

4. **Ignition Delay (Physical + Chemical Delay):**
   - **Physical delay** — the time taken for fuel droplets to vaporise and mix with air (**~ 0.5–1.0 ms**).
   - **Chemical delay** — pre-flame reactions and radical formation (**~ 1.0–2.0 ms**).
   - Total ignition delay for HFO is **~ 2–4 ms**, longer than for MDO.

5. **Combustion (Premixed + Diffusive Phase):**
   - A fraction of fuel ignites in premixed mode (causing the characteristic **$\frac{dp}{d\theta}$** pressure rise), followed by diffusion-controlled burning of the remaining fuel spray.

6. **Expansion & Exhaust:**
   - The high-pressure gases expand, doing work on the piston. Exhaust valves open near BDC, expelling burnt gases to the turbocharger.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Distinction for Examiners:** In a marine **gas turbine**, ignition is fundamentally different. The **combustion chamber is continuous-flow** — fuel is injected into a steady stream of compressed air. A permanent flame is maintained by a **high-energy igniter** (typically a spark plug fed by a high-voltage exciter producing **~ 20 kV**). Once lit, the flame is self-sustaining as long as atomisation and air/fuel mixing continue.

## 2.2 Dual-Fuel Marine Engine Ignition — The Modern Paradigm

Modern MAN B&W ME-GI and Wärtsilä 31DF engines accept **LNG (Liquefied Natural Gas)** as the primary fuel, with **pilot oil (MDO/MGO)** injected to ignite the gas-air mixture. This is a **pilot ignition** system — the gas-air mixture is too lean and too cold to auto-ignite, so a small pilot oil spray provides the initial flame kernel.

## 2.3 Marine Boiler Ignition

Oil-fired auxiliary boilers (e.g., **Saacke, Aalborg, Miura** type) use **automatic burner management systems (BMS)** with:

- **Ignition Electrode:** Produces a high-voltage arc (**~ 6,000 – 10,000 V**) to ignite the oil spray.
- **Flame Scanner:** UV or IR photodetector confirms flame presence; if absent, the BMS initiates a **lockout** (safety trip).

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| S.No. | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Typical Marine Value | Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Compression Ratio | $\varepsilon = \dfrac{V_1}{V_2} = \dfrac{V_s + V_c}{V_c}$ | 18 – 22 | dimensionless |
| 2 | Diesel Cycle Thermal Efficiency | $\eta_{th} = 1 - \dfrac{1}{\varepsilon^{\gamma - 1}}$ | 0.55 – 0.65 | fraction |
| 3 | Indicated Mean Effective Pressure (IMEP) | $\text{IMEP} = \dfrac{W_{ind}}{V_s}$ | 18 – 24 | bar |
| 4 | Brake Specific Fuel Consumption | $\text{BSFC} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_f}{P_b}$ | 160 – 180 (2-stroke) | g/kWh |
| 5 | Air-Fuel Ratio (Stoichiometric) for HFO (C$\sim$H$\sim$O$\sim$S) | $\text{AFR}_{stoich} = \dfrac{137.8 \, m_{O_2}}{\text{C} + 0.375\,S + 0.75\,H - 0.4375\,O}$ | ~ 19 : 1 to 21 : 1 | kg air / kg fuel |
| 6 | Specific Fuel Oil Consumption (SFOC) | $\text{SFOC} = \dfrac{m_f}{P_b \cdot t} \times 10^3$ | 160 – 200 | g/kWh |
| 7 | Brake Thermal Efficiency | $\eta_b = \dfrac{P_b \cdot 3600}{\dot{m}_f \cdot \text{LCV}}$ | 0.45 – 0.54 | fraction |
| 8 | Volumetric Efficiency (4-stroke) | $\eta_{vol} = \dfrac{m_{actual}}{\rho_{air} \cdot V_s}$ | 0.85 – 0.95 | fraction |
| 9 | Combustion Efficiency | $\eta_{comb} = 1 - \dfrac{Q_{loss}}{Q_{input}}$ | 0.98 – 0.995 | fraction |
| 10 | Heat Released per kg of Fuel | $Q = m_f \cdot \text{LCV}$ | — | kJ or MJ |
| 11 | Lower Calorific Value (approximate, by Dulong's eqn.) | $\text{LCV} = 33{,}820 \cdot \text{C} + 144{,}200 \cdot (H - \tfrac{O}{8}) + 9{,}410 \cdot S$ | — | kJ/kg |
| 12 | Higher Calorific Value | $\text{HCV} = \text{LCV} + 2{,}442 \cdot \dfrac{9\,H}{100}$ | — | kJ/kg |
| 13 | Excess Air Ratio ($\lambda$) | $\lambda = \dfrac{\text{Actual air}}{\text{Stoichiometric air}}$ | 1.05 – 1.50 | dimensionless |
| 14 | Kinematic Viscosity (HFO at 50 °C) | $\nu$ | 380 (RMG 380) | cSt (mm²/s) |
| 15 | Fuel Density at 15 °C | $\rho_{15}$ | 980 – 1010 (HFO); 890 (MGO) | kg/m³ |
| 16 | Pour Point | $T_{pp}$ | 30 (HFO); -6 (MGO) | °C |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** In ship design, the **SFOC (Specific Fuel Oil Consumption)** figure drives the daily bunker consumption calculation. For a 9,000 TEU container vessel at 80 % MCR, expected SFOC of **~ 168 g/kWh** translates to roughly **~ 100 tonnes of HFO per day** at service speed. This single parameter cascades into the **fuel tank capacity**, **lubricating oil tank capacity**, **boiler capacity**, and ultimately the **hull form** through the **lightship–deadweight compromise**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Theoretical Air-Fuel Ratio for a Hydrocarbon Fuel

Consider a generic marine residual fuel with ultimate analysis (mass basis) **C %**, **H %**, **S %**, **O %**, with the balance being ash and nitrogen. The stoichiometric combustion reactions are:

**Step 1 — Carbon combustion:**
Carbon + Oxygen $\rightarrow$ Carbon Dioxide
$$C + O_2 \rightarrow CO_2$$

**Step 2 — Hydrogen combustion:**
Hydrogen + Oxygen $\rightarrow$ Water
$$2H_2 + O_2 \rightarrow 2H_2O$$

**Step 3 — Sulphur combustion:**
Sulphur + Oxygen $\rightarrow$ Sulphur Dioxide
$$S + O_2 \rightarrow SO_2$$

**Step 4 — Oxygen already present in fuel** is subtracted from the required O$_2$.

Therefore, the theoretical mass of oxygen required per kg of fuel is:

$$
m_{O_2,req} = \dfrac{32}{12}\cdot\dfrac{C}{100} + \dfrac{32}{4}\cdot\dfrac{H}{100} + \dfrac{32}{32}\cdot\dfrac{S}{100} - \dfrac{32}{32}\cdot\dfrac{O}{100}
$$

Since air contains oxygen by mass at **23.2 %** (mass fraction of O$_2$ in dry air = **0.232**), the theoretical air required is:

$$
m_{air,stoich} = \dfrac{m_{O_2,req}}{0.232}
$$

Final compact expression:

$$
\text{AFR}_{stoich} = \dfrac{137.93\,C + 345.0\,H + 43.1\,S - 43.1\,O}{100}
$$

with C, H, S, O in mass percentage.

**Worked Example — Typical HFO (RMG 380):** Ultimate analysis: C = **85.5 %**, H = **10.7 %**, S = **2.5 %**, O = **1.0 %**, N + Ash = balance.

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_{O_2,req} &= \tfrac{32}{12}(0.855) + \tfrac{32}{4}(0.107) + \tfrac{32}{32}(0.025) - \tfrac{32}{32}(0.010) \\
&= 2.280 + 0.856 + 0.025 - 0.010 \\
&= 3.151 \; \text{kg O}_2 \text{ per kg fuel}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\text{AFR}_{stoich} = \dfrac{3.151}{0.232} = 13.58 \; \text{kg air / kg fuel}
$$

(This is the *theoretical* stoichiometric ratio. Actual marine engines operate at excess air ratio $\lambda = 1.4$ to 2.2, giving an effective AFR of ~ 19 to 30.)

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Brake Thermal Efficiency from SFOC

The **brake thermal efficiency** $\eta_b$ of a marine diesel engine is related to the Specific Fuel Oil Consumption (SFOC) by:

**Step 1 — Energy input per second per kW of brake power:**

$$
\dot{Q}_{in} = \dfrac{P_b \cdot \text{SFOC} \cdot \text{LCV}}{3600 \cdot 1000}
$$

**Step 2 — By definition of brake thermal efficiency, useful output per second equals $\eta_b$ times input:**

$$
P_b = \eta_b \cdot \dot{Q}_{in}
$$

**Step 3 — Solving for $\eta_b$:**

$$
\eta_b = \dfrac{3600 \cdot 10^6}{\text{SFOC} \cdot \text{LCV}}
$$

with SFOC in **g/kWh** and LCV in **kJ/kg**.

**Numerical Example — MAN B&W 6S60MC-C on HFO:**

Take SFOC = **168 g/kWh**, LCV of HFO = **40,200 kJ/kg**.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_b &= \dfrac{3600 \times 10^6}{168 \times 40{,}200} \\
&= \dfrac{3.6 \times 10^9}{6.7536 \times 10^6} \\
&= 0.5330 = 53.30\,\%
\end{aligned}
$$

This is consistent with the published ~ **53 %** brake efficiency for this engine class — a verifiable KTU-board level number.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Fuel Bunker Quantity Estimation

For a voyage of **$D$ nautical miles** at service speed **$V_s$ knots** with installed brake power fraction **$\kappa$** of MCR being used:

**Step 1 — Voyage duration in hours:**

$$
t_{voy} = \dfrac{D}{V_s} \; \text{hours}
$$

**Step 2 — Effective average power output:**

$$
P_{avg} = \kappa \cdot P_{MCR} \; \text{kW}
$$

**Step 3 — Total fuel consumed:**

$$
M_{fuel} = \dfrac{P_{avg} \cdot t_{voy} \cdot \text{SFOC}}{10^6} \; \text{tonnes}
$$

(since SFOC is in g/kWh, division by $10^6$ converts grams to tonnes).

**Numerical Example — 8,000 TEU container vessel:**

- $D$ = **12,000 nm**, $V_s$ = **20 knots**, $\kappa$ = **0.80**, $P_{MCR}$ = **50,000 kW**, SFOC = **168 g/kWh**.

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{voy} &= \dfrac{12{,}000}{20} = 600 \; \text{hours} \\
P_{avg} &= 0.80 \times 50{,}000 = 40{,}000 \; \text{kW} \\
M_{fuel} &= \dfrac{40{,}000 \times 600 \times 168}{10^6} = \dfrac{4.032 \times 10^9}{10^6} = 4032 \; \text{tonnes}
\end{aligned}
$$

This bunker mass must be accommodated in double-bottom and wing tanks — a design parameter that **directly drives hull form** and **inert gas system sizing** (per **SOLAS Ch. II-2**).

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Marine Fuels

| Property | HFO (RMG 380) | MDO (DMB) | MGO (DMA) | LNG | Methanol | Ammonia |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| State at NTP | Liquid | Liquid | Liquid | Cryogenic liquid | Liquid | Cryogenic liquid |
| Density (kg/m³) | 980 | 900 | 890 | 450 (boil-off gas) | 792 | 682 |
| LCV (MJ/kg) | 40.2 | 42.7 | 42.7 | 50.0 | 19.9 | 18.6 |
| Viscosity at 50 °C (cSt) | 380 | 6 – 12 | 2 – 6 | — | 0.6 | 0.25 (boil-off) |
| Flash Point (°C) | 60 – 80 | 60 min. | 60 min. | -188 (gas) | 11 | — |
| Auto-ignition Temp. (°C) | 350 | 250 | 230 | 540 | 464 | 651 |
| Sulphur Content (% m/m) | ≤ 3.5 (pre-2020); ≤ 0.5 (post-2020) | ≤ 0.1 – 2.0 | ≤ 0.1 | < 0.001 | ~ 0 | 0 |
| Cetane Number | — | 35 – 55 | 45 – 55 | (gas) | < 5 (poor CN) | (gas) |
| Storage Temp. (°C) | 30 – 60 | ambient | ambient | -162 | 20 – 30 | -33 |
| Carbon Dioxide Emissions Factor (g CO₂/g fuel) | 3.114 | 3.206 | 3.206 | 2.750 | 1.375 | 0 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Design Implication:** Switching from HFO to LNG in a dual-fuel engine reduces CO₂ emissions by ~ **20 – 25 %**, eliminates SO$_x$ entirely, and slashes NO$_x$ by ~ **85 – 90 %** when used in Otto-cycle (lean-burn) mode. This is why every major engine builder now offers an "LNG-ready" or "LNG-fuelled" propulsion package.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Marine Diesel Engine Combustion Subsystem

The following Mermaid diagram maps the **functional architecture** of the marine diesel combustion subsystem — from atmospheric air intake to exhaust gas exit — capturing the ignition chain that examiners expect students to draw in descriptive questions.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Atmospheric Air Intake] --> A2[Turbocharger Compressor]
    A2 --> A3[Air Cooler Charge Air Cooler]
    A3 --> A4[Scavenge Air Receiver]
    A4 --> A5[Cylinder Combustion Chamber]
    A5 --> A6[Exhaust Manifold]
    A6 --> A7[Turbocharger Turbine]
    A7 --> A8[Exhaust Uptakes and Stack]
    B1[HFO Settling Tank] --> B2[Purifier Centrifuge]
    B2 --> B3[Service Tank]
    B3 --> B4[Heater Viscosity 12 to 15 cSt at Injector]
    B4 --> B5[Fuel Booster Pump]
    B5 --> B6[Common Rail High Pressure Pump]
    B6 --> B7[Heavy Fuel Injector 1000 to 1800 bar]
    B7 --> A5
    C1[Crankshaft Position Sensor TDC Reference] --> C2[Engine Control System ECS]
    C2 --> C6[Injection Timing Actuator]
    C6 --> B6
    D1[Compressed Air Starting Valve] --> A5
```

**Reading the Diagram:**

- The **upper chain (A1–A8)** represents the air and exhaust gas flow path.
- The **lower chain (B1–B7)** represents the fuel oil conditioning and delivery path; note that HFO must be **heated** to reduce viscosity to **12 – 15 cSt** before injection.
- The **right chain (C1–C2–C6)** represents the electronic control of injection timing.
- **D1** represents the starting air system that initially turns the engine to enable compression ignition.

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Ignition System Hierarchy in Marine Prime Movers

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Marine Prime Mover] --> Q1{Engine Type}
    Q1 --> Q2[2 Stroke Crosshead Diesel]
    Q1 --> Q3[4 Stroke Trunk Piston Diesel]
    Q1 --> Q4[Dual Fuel Engine]
    Q1 --> Q5[Marine Gas Turbine]
    Q1 --> Q6[Auxiliary Boiler]
    Q2 --> R1[Compression Ignition]
    Q3 --> R1
    Q4 --> R2[Pilot Oil Ignition plus Hot Surface]
    Q5 --> R3[Continuous Combustion plus Spark Ignition]
    Q6 --> R4[High Voltage Arc Ignition plus Flame Scanner]
    R1 --> S1[Self Ignition from Adiabatic Compression]
    R2 --> S2[Small MDO Pilot Auto Ignites Gas]
    R3 --> S3[Permanent Flame Maintained]
    R4 --> S4[Spark plus UV IR Flame Feedback]
```

## 4.3 Schematic — Fuel Oil System Flow (HFO Route)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    F0[Bunker Tank Storage] --> F1[Settling Tank 24 hour settling]
    F1 --> F2[Transfer Pump]
    F2 --> F3[Purifier HFO and MGO separation]
    F3 --> F4[Day Tank Service Tank]
    F4 --> F5[Heater Steam or Electric to 130 to 150 degree C]
    F5 --> F6[Viscosity Controller Auto Regulated]
    F6 --> F7[Booster Pump]
    F7 --> F8[Common Rail Man B&W ME or Wartsila X Engine]
    F8 --> F9[Fuel Injector 1000 to 1800 bar]
    F9 --> F10[Combustion Chamber]
    F10 --> F11[Exhaust Gas to Turbocharger]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Reading Tip:** The viscosity controller (F6) is a critical onboard component. It is an **automatic closed-loop control system** that adjusts the steam flow to the heater based on real-time viscometer feedback — keeping the HFO at a stable **12 – 15 cSt** before injection. Without this, the fuel would either be too viscous (poor atomisation, combustion deterioration) or too hot (vaporisation in the supply line, vapour lock).

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper Pattern]**
**Explain the term "Compression Ignition" as applied to marine diesel engines. Why is it the preferred ignition method for ship propulsion?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Compression ignition (CI) is the process in which the fuel-air mixture is ignited solely by the high temperature of air produced through adiabatic compression — no external spark or flame is used. In a marine 2-stroke diesel engine, the air is compressed to a pressure of ~ **120 – 180 bar** and a temperature of **~ 600 – 800 °C**, well above the self-ignition temperature of marine diesel oil (~ **250 °C**). CI is preferred for ship propulsion because:
1. **Higher thermal efficiency** (Diesel cycle, 53 – 55 % achievable) — directly reduces bunker costs.
2. **Ability to burn cheap residual fuels** (HFO, RMG 380) — a major commercial advantage.
3. **Reliability and safety** — no high-voltage ignition system to fail at sea.
4. **High power-to-weight ratio** suits slow-speed, large-bore main engines.

*(Allocate 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for explanation, 1 mark for the four advantages.)*

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Pattern]**
**List the important properties of marine Heavy Fuel Oil (HFO) that affect its handling and combustion performance onboard.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The key properties are:
1. **Kinematic Viscosity** at 50 °C — must be reduced to **12 – 15 cSt** by heating before injection (RMG 380 has ~ 380 cSt at 50 °C).
2. **Density** at 15 °C — affects tank capacity and mass calculations; typical **980 – 1010 kg/m³**.
3. **Flash Point** — minimum **60 °C** per **SOLAS Ch. II-2**; lower flash points are unsafe.
4. **Pour Point** — lowest temperature at which fuel flows; affects cold-weather operations.
5. **Sulphur Content** — capped at **0.50 % m/m** globally and **0.10 % m/m** in ECAs under MARPOL Annex VI.
6. **Water Content** — must be < **0.5 % v/v** to protect purifiers and burners.
7. **Calorific Value (LCV)** — ~ **40,200 kJ/kg**; determines thermal energy released per kg burned.

*(Allocate 1 mark for viscosity + density, 1 mark for flash/pour, 1 mark for S content + LCV.)*

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A — Option 1 (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]**
**(a)** Describe with a neat sketch the working of a marine **two-stroke, low-speed, crosshead diesel engine** combustion system, clearly identifying the **fuel injection, ignition delay, and combustion phases**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A bulk carrier is propelled by a main engine of **25,000 kW MCR** running on HFO with SFOC = **170 g/kWh** and LCV = **40,200 kJ/kg**. Estimate (i) the brake thermal efficiency, (ii) the daily fuel consumption at 80 % MCR, and (iii) the total voyage fuel for **7,500 nautical miles** at **15 knots**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Working of 2-stroke Crosshead Marine Diesel Engine (7 marks):**
- Mention **uniflow scavenging** using exhaust valves in the cylinder head and scavenge ports at the lower end of the liner. (1 mark)
- **Compression stroke:** Piston compresses scavenge air to ~ 120 – 180 bar and ~ 600 – 800 °C. (1 mark)
- **Fuel injection:** HFO atomised by common rail injector at ~ 1000 – 1800 bar, 6° – 12° BTDC. (1 mark)
- **Ignition delay:** Physical (~ 0.5–1 ms) + Chemical (~ 1–2 ms) = total **2 – 4 ms** for HFO. (1 mark)
- **Premixed + Diffusive combustion phases.** (1 mark)
- **Expansion and exhaust:** Scavenging period after exhaust valve opens; turbocharger extracts exhaust energy. (1 mark)
- Neat sketch with components labelled — fuel pump, injector, cylinder, piston, exhaust valve, turbocharger. (1 mark)

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**(i) Brake Thermal Efficiency:**

$$
\eta_b = \dfrac{3600 \times 10^6}{170 \times 40{,}200} = \dfrac{3.6 \times 10^9}{6.834 \times 10^6} = 0.5268 = 52.68\,\%
$$

(1 mark for formula, 1 mark for substitution, 0.5 mark for answer, 0.5 mark for interpretation)

**(ii) Daily Fuel Consumption at 80 % MCR:**

$$
P_{avg} = 0.80 \times 25{,}000 = 20{,}000 \; \text{kW}
$$

$$
\dot{m}_f = \dfrac{20{,}000 \times 170}{10^6} = 3.40 \; \text{tonnes/hour}
$$

$$
M_{day} = 3.40 \times 24 = 81.6 \; \text{tonnes/day}
$$

(1 mark for power, 1 mark for mass rate, 0.5 mark for daily total)

**(iii) Voyage Fuel for 7,500 nm at 15 knots:**

$$
t_{voy} = \dfrac{7{,}500}{15} = 500 \; \text{hours}
$$

$$
M_{voy} = 3.40 \times 500 = 1{,}700 \; \text{tonnes}
$$

(1 mark for time, 1 mark for total mass, 0.5 mark for unit and context)

---

### Question B — Option 2 (14 Marks) — *Internal Choice*

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Pattern]**
**(a)** Compare the **ignition systems** used in (i) a marine low-speed diesel engine, (ii) a marine dual-fuel engine, and (iii) a marine gas turbine. Explain the role of the **pilot oil** in a dual-fuel engine. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A fuel has the following ultimate analysis by mass: **C = 86 %**, **H = 11 %**, **S = 2.5 %**, **O = 0.5 %**. Calculate (i) the stoichiometric mass of air required per kg of fuel, (ii) the calorific value using Dulong's formula, and (iii) the actual air supplied if the engine operates at 30 % excess air. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Comparison of Ignition Systems (7 marks):**

| Parameter | 2-stroke Diesel | Dual-Fuel Engine | Marine Gas Turbine |
|---|---|---|---|
| Ignition Type | Compression | Pilot + Compression | Continuous Combustion + Spark |
| Pressure Source | Piston Compression | Piston + Gas Pressure | Compressor (10 – 30 bar) |
| Initial Source | Adiabatic Heat | MDO Pilot Flame | High-Voltage Spark Plug |
| Fuel Form at Ignition | Atomised Liquid | Gas + Pilot Spray | Atomised Liquid/Gas |
| Start-up | Compressed Air | Compressed Air + Gas Admission | External Motor/Starting |
| Self-Sustaining After Start? | Yes (continuous CI) | Yes (pilot sustains) | Yes (permanent flame) |

(3 marks for table, 1 mark for explanation of pilot oil)

**Role of Pilot Oil in Dual-Fuel Engines:**
Methane (LNG) has a high auto-ignition temperature (~ **540 °C**), and in lean-burn Otto-cycle mode the gas mixture is below that temperature. Therefore, a small quantity of **pilot oil (MDO/MGO)** is injected simultaneously with gas admission. This pilot fuel undergoes **compression auto-ignition**, creating a flame kernel that ignites the gas-air mixture. The pilot oil contributes only **~ 1 – 5 %** of the total energy input.

(3 marks for the role, total 7)

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**(i) Stoichiometric Air:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_{O_2,req} &= \tfrac{32}{12}(0.86) + \tfrac{32}{4}(0.11) + \tfrac{32}{32}(0.025) - \tfrac{32}{32}(0.005) \\
&= 2.2933 + 0.8800 + 0.0250 - 0.0050 \\
&= 3.1933 \; \text{kg O}_2 \text{/kg fuel}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
m_{air,stoich} = \dfrac{3.1933}{0.232} = 13.76 \; \text{kg air/kg fuel}
$$

(2 marks — 1 for O₂ calc, 1 for air conversion)

**(ii) Dulong's Calorific Value:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{LCV} &= 33{,}820 \times 0.86 + 144{,}200 \times \left(0.11 - \tfrac{0.005}{8}\right) + 9{,}410 \times 0.025 \\
&= 29{,}085.2 + 144{,}200 \times 0.1094 + 235.25 \\
&= 29{,}085.2 + 15{,}776.4 + 235.25 \\
&= 45{,}096.85 \; \text{kJ/kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

(3 marks — 1 for formula, 1 for substitution, 1 for answer)

**(iii) Actual Air with 30 % Excess:**

$$
m_{air,actual} = 1.30 \times 13.76 = 17.89 \; \text{kg air/kg fuel}
$$

(2 marks — 1 for excess air application, 1 for final answer)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing LCV and HCV:** Use **LCV (Lower Calorific Value)** in engine efficiency calculations since water leaves as vapour. HCV overestimates efficiency by ~ **7 – 10 %**.
> 2. **Mass of O₂ vs. mass of air:** Many students use 21 % by volume and forget to convert to mass basis. Always use **0.232** (mass fraction of O₂ in air) for mass-based calculations, **0.21** (volume fraction) for molar calculations.
> 3. **Dulong's formula sign errors:** The term is **(H − O/8)**, not (H + O/8). The negative term accounts for oxygen already bound to hydrogen in the fuel.
> 4. **Excess air ratio $\lambda$ vs. % excess air:** $\lambda = 1.30$ means **30 %** excess, NOT **130 %**. This is a frequent mark-loser.
> 5. **Forgetting the factor 10⁶ in SFOC-to-tonnes conversion.** SFOC is in g/kWh — dividing by 10⁶ gives tonnes/kWh, then multiply by hours.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Module 4: Ignition System and Fuels**

- **Marine 2-stroke main engines use Compression Ignition** (Diesel cycle); no spark plug. Compression ratio **18:1 to 22:1**; peak pressure **~ 120 – 180 bar**; peak temperature **~ 600 – 800 °C**.
- **Ignition delay** has two components: **physical delay** (atomisation + vaporisation) and **chemical delay** (pre-flame reactions). Total **~ 2 – 4 ms** for HFO, shorter for MDO.
- **Dual-fuel engines** (LNG + MDO pilot) use **pilot oil auto-ignition** because methane auto-ignites at ~ 540 °C, too high for lean-burn Otto cycle.
- **Marine gas turbines** use **continuous combustion chambers** with high-voltage spark igniters and a permanently maintained flame.
- **Auxiliary boilers** use **high-voltage arc igniters** with **UV / IR flame scanners** for BMS (Burner Management System) safety.
- **HFO must be heated to 130 – 150 °C** to bring viscosity down to **12 – 15 cSt** before injection — controlled by a closed-loop **viscosity controller**.
- **Standard marine fuel grades** (ISO 8217): RMG 380 (HFO), DMA / DMB / DMZ (distillate MGO/MDO).
- **MARPOL Annex VI global sulphur cap = 0.50 % m/m** (since 2020); **ECA sulphur cap = 0.10 % m/m** (Baltic, North Sea, North American, US Caribbean).
- **LCV values to remember:** HFO = **40,200 kJ/kg**; MGO = **42,700 kJ/kg**; LNG = **50,000 kJ/kg**; Methanol = **19,900 kJ/kg**; Ammonia = **18,600 kJ/kg**.
- **SFOC of slow-speed 2-stroke diesel** is typically **160 – 180 g/kWh** at MCR, translating to a **brake efficiency of 52 – 55 %**.
- **Stoichiometric air for typical HFO:** ~ **13.5 – 13.8 kg air/kg fuel**; marine engines run at **excess air ratio 1.4 – 2.2** (40 – 120 % excess).
- **Dulong's formula for LCV:** $\text{LCV} = 33{,}820\,C + 144{,}200\,(H - O/8) + 9{,}410\,S$ in kJ/kg with C, H, O, S as **mass fractions**.
- **Daily bunker consumption** for a 50 MW vessel at 80 % MCR ≈ **~ 100 tonnes/day** of HFO; voyage calculations drive **tank capacity, hull form, and EEDI** (Energy Efficiency Design Index) compliance.
- **Future fuels** under active R&D: **LNG** (transitional), **Methanol** (Maersk's container fleet), **Ammonia** (zero-carbon), **Hydrogen** (zero-carbon, storage challenges). Each demands **different engine technologies** — methanol and ammonia typically need **pilot ignition** due to their high auto-ignition temperatures.
